### Post by englishaugust on 2007-04-03
Paperdiesel: First of all, this is the most helpful post i have found on this forum. Kudos to you for not only setting this up but also following up on every single query. In this hope, i will tell you my saga of tryst with ubuntu:
1. one month ago, installed ubuntu on my inspiron 6400, used your guide to get wireless working, installed ati x1400 drivers etc, everything working flawlessly.
2. one fine day, ubuntu does some updates, then wireless does not work.
3. i search forums etc, think i screwed up my machine, reinstalled ubuntu.
4. followed your guide again. But this time, i run into a problem. wireless light is on, i can see the networks in my area but cannot connect to anything. 
5. check my etc/network/interfaces, looks like my essid and wireless key are correct (128 bit wep hexadecimal 26 character key) but cannot connect to my router.
6. do ifconfig and find that essid under wlan0 is off/any
7. do dmesg and find wlan0: link is not ready

Any ideas?

another side question :) I would really like to know what do the different commands mean but cannot find a resource that explains the commands as well, can you help?

### Post by Wampcat on 2007-04-03

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-03
> **englishaugust said:**
> Paperdiesel: First of all, this is the most helpful post i have found on this forum. Kudos to you for not only setting this up but also following up on every single query.
1. one month ago, installed ubuntu on my inspiron 6400, used your guide to get wireless working, installed ati x1400 drivers etc, everything working flawlessly.
2. one fine day, ubuntu does some updates, then wireless does not work.

I'm guessing in those updates, your kernel version got updated as well.  Any kernel update will break your wireless.  To get it back, recompile ndiswrapper and you should be good to go.

> 
5. check my etc/network/interfaces, looks like my essid and wireless key are correct (128 bit wep hexadecimal 26 character key) but cannot connect to my router.

Can you post the contents of your interfaces file?  Change the password so we don't all see it, of course.

I'm thinking that since your wireless light is on and you can scan for networks, this isn't a hardware issue.  My first thought is that ubuntu may have mistakenly put your wireless-key arguments backwards in your interfaces file.  For example, if you're using wep on an open system, your line might look like this:

wireless-key open thisismypassword

Or, it could look like this:

wireless-key thisismypassword open

And believe it or not, one of those will work and the other one doesn't.  I'm not sure why, and I haven't dug deep enough to find out which one works under which circumstances.  My advice is just to try one and then other other and see if it helps.

One other alternative it to use network-manager to manage your wireless connection.  It's far superior, in my opinion, and you don't have to mess around with your interfaces file (after the initial set-up).

> another side question :) I would really like to know what do the different commands mean but cannot find a resource that explains the commands as well, can you help? 


Which commands, specifically?

### Post by marquedios on 2007-04-03
Dell Latitude D520 with the Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI worked wonderfully.  I had found this post a week and a half ago or so when I got this laptop but when I went to actually install the wireless drivers for some reason I wasn't typing in the search right so I couldn't find it :(.  Finally after not dealing with 26 kbps wireless connection and just plugging in I decided that I'd attempt to find this again.  I FOUND IT and it worked.  This works SOOO much better than the bcm43xx which didn't detect the proper signal strength, frequency nor could it connect to anything other than my router which was 5 feet away so I'm now in love and can actually use this laptop like a laptop instead of a small desktop.

Thank you, thank you, thank you

### Post by marquedios on 2007-04-03
> **numerical said:**
> I did everything except when  i get up to the following part:


When i try to blacklist the default ubuntu driver: 

I keep getting this bash message : bash: /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist: Permission denied


can I bypass this part or if not ,how can i fix it?

Are you sure you are using the command:

```
sudo echo blacklist bcm43xx >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```

Gotta make sure the sudo is there otherwise yes, you will get a permission denied.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-04
> **numerical said:**
> I did everything except when  i get up to the following part:


When i try to blacklist the default ubuntu driver: 

I keep getting this bash message : bash: /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist: Permission denied


can I bypass this part or if not ,how can i fix it?

Like marq pointed out, you need to make sure you're running that command with sudo.  ```
sudo echo blacklist bcm43xx >> etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```

It's probably easiest to just copy/paste the commands to avoid typos, etc.

### Post by englishaugust on 2007-04-04
> **paperdiesel said:**
> I'm guessing in those updates, your kernel version got updated as well.  Any kernel update will break your wireless.  To get it back, recompile ndiswrapper and you should be good to go.



Can you post the contents of your interfaces file?  Change the password so we don't all see it, of course.

I'm thinking that since your wireless light is on and you can scan for networks, this isn't a hardware issue.  My first thought is that ubuntu may have mistakenly put your wireless-key arguments backwards in your interfaces file.  For example, if you're using wep on an open system, your line might look like this:

wireless-key open thisismypassword

Or, it could look like this:

wireless-key thisismypassword open

And believe it or not, one of those will work and the other one doesn't.  I'm not sure why, and I haven't dug deep enough to find out which one works under which circumstances.  My advice is just to try one and then other other and see if it helps.

One other alternative it to use network-manager to manage your wireless connection.  It's far superior, in my opinion, and you don't have to mess around with your interfaces file (after the initial set-up).



Which commands, specifically?
Thanks for your help. It worked but I had to make two changes total.

Like you said, I added open just before my wireless key. But I also noticed that my wireless was using eth1 instead of wlan0. I remembered that somwhere during the install alias wlan0 ndiswrapper was added somwhere. Thought that might be screwy. So went to /etc/iftab and changed eth1 to wlan0.

Wireless is now working.

Thanks again.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-04
> **ph0neman said:**
> [COLOR="Red"][B]drats!!!!  after a reboot i can scan networks and see them but cant connect, cant obtain an IP address :confused:

the networks actually have signal strength... any idea why it would be that I cannot reconnect.  I read something here that I needed to comment out some statements, could that be my issue?[/B][/COLOR]

After trying to connect to a network and failing, post here the logs from dmesg and /var/log/syslog.  It might also help to turn encryption off on the router and your laptop -- just temporarily -- to make sure it's not a key issue.  Also, if you're using DHCP from the router, make sure the DHCP option is on in your wireless settings for your particular network.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-06
> **numerical said:**
> I still get the permission denied message although i am using sudo and iam pretty sure I m in root mode with full admin priveleges.ANy ideas why i keep getting the permission denied meesage when i try to black list the default driver.

steven@steven:~$ sudo echo blacklist bcm43xx >> etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
-bash: etc/modprobe.d/blacklist: No such file or directory

I think it failed here because you didn't put a front slash / in front of the etc.  The command should have looked like this: ```
sudo echo blacklist bcm43xx >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```

> steven@steven:~$ sudo echo blacklist bcm43xx >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
-bash: /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist: Permission denied
steven@steven:~$  echo blacklist bcm43xx >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
-bash: /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist: Permission denied
steven@steven:~$

That time it looks like you did it right.  I'm wondering if maybe the file just doesn't exist.  Do this command:

```
sudo ls -l /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```

If that comes back with information about the file, then it exists.  If the file DOES exist, you could try forcing the file permissions with this:

```
sudo chmod 777 /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```

Then try the echo command again.

---

### Post by spifbv on 2007-04-09
using echo with sudo will not work.  what you want is something like this:

echo blacklist bcm43xx | sudo tee -a /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist

or just:

sudo vi /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist

or for the vi-challenged:

gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist

but then again the vi-challenged may not want to be messing with this :)

in other news, the bcm43xx-mac80211 native driver is supposed to be working as of 2.6.20-14, but I still can't quite get it to work.  anyone know what the magic formula is?  I've tried several firmware versions to no avail.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-09
> **spifbv said:**
> using echo with sudo will not work.  what you want is something like this:

echo blacklist bcm43xx | sudo tee -a /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist

or just:

sudo vi /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist

or for the vi-challenged:

gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist

but then again the vi-challenged may not want to be messing with this :)


Using echo in a sudo statement works just fine.  Sometimes people have trouble writing to that blacklist file for various reasons, but it's not a sudo echo issue.  Sudo simply executes your command in a temporary super user mode, and then returns to normal user permissions once the command has exited (regardless of the exit code).

> 
in other news, the bcm43xx-mac80211 native driver is supposed to be working as of 2.6.20-14, but I still can't quite get it to work.  anyone know what the magic formula is?  I've tried several firmware versions to no avail.

The bcm43xx drivers have been "supposed" to be working for several kernel releases now.  But they never do.  Hence the need for ndiswrapper.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-09
> **numerical said:**
> Also : weather I type sudo make install ndiswrapper or whatever else I m still having no luck with this. can someone tell em whsat im doing wrong?

This isn't a compile problem.  You're simply not in the right directory when you run that make command.  To get a list of files and directories inside your current directory, type ls.  That should show you the correct name of that ndiswrapper directory (which is probably ndiswrapper-1.41).  It might look something like this:

```

ls
cd ndiswrapper-1.41
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make
sudo make install
```

etc etc.

Why have you reinstalled ubuntu 3 times?

---

weell any way im gonna get back to trying to make this wirless work . i ll keep you posted thanks for your help.

### Post by st33med on 2007-04-11
Hey, spifbv! I know how to fix what you are doing.

do this:
```
sudo -s
echo blacklist bcm43xx >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```

sudo -s turns the terminal into root. I don't know why only sudo in front of the command rejects it, even though it is telling it you're root.

That worked for me...:D

But not the part where I try to hook up, unfortunately.:???:

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-12
> **mountielad said:**
> (Using Inspiron 1501)
After trying to blacklist bcm43xx (sudo echo blacklist bcm43xx >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist) I get no response.

When I get to the compile stage (step 3) and try to uninstall I'm faced with the following response:
......home/conorflavin/.drivers# sudo make uninstall 
(and the folowing arises. I enter 'sudo make uninstall' multiple times but with the same response.)
make: *** No rule to make target `uninstall'. Stop.

No, you're not in the ndiswrapper directory which is why you're seeing that message.  Look a few posts back in this thread -- I answered this same question for someone else.

### Post by Ocxic on 2007-04-12
my buddies inspirion 6400 runs wireless perfectly fine, with a native driver, ipw3945, or something very similer. he's running edgy 64-bit, and knowing this would have saved me alot of time with ndiswrapper. this worked out of the box by the way.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-12
> **stormbringr said:**
> this worked great for me until the prompt to run updates came up and I let them run and now the WIFI light is still on but when I run the sudo iwlist scanning my wlan0 interface is no longer there

I'm new at this, been messing with this for a week until I found this walk through

thanks for any help you can give

Whenever you update your kernel, you will have to re-compile ndiswrapper.  I've been over this with others previously in the thread -- go through the posts for an explanation, or simply go back to the first page and follow the ndiswrapper compile steps.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-12
> **Ocxic said:**
> my buddies inspirion 6400 runs wireless perfectly fine, with a native driver, ipw3945, or something very similer. he's running edgy 64-bit, and knowing this would have saved me alot of time with ndiswrapper. this worked out of the box by the way.

The E1505/6400 is offered by Dell with many different options for the wireless card.  If your friend's worked out of the box, it's most likely because he/she doesn't have a Broadcom WLAN 1390 wireless card.  This how-to is intended for users with the Broadcom 1390 specifically.

I get the following.  Any ideas ?

> hgordon@uclinux:~/Desktop$ sudo ndiswrapper -m
module configuration contains directive install pci:v000014E4d00004311sv00001363sd0000103Cbc*sc*i* /sbin/modprobe ndiswrapper
;you should delete that at /usr/sbin/ndiswrapper line 719, <MODPROBE> line 410.
module configuration contains directive install pci:v000014E4d00004311sv00001364sd0000103Cbc*sc*i* /sbin/modprobe ndiswrapper


==============================================

UPDATE -

bcmwl6 is a Vista driver, and it doesn't work with ndiswrapper.  I installed bcmwl5 instead with

ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
depmod -a
modprobe ndiswrapper
ndiswrapper -mi
ndiswrapper -ma
depmod -a modprobe ndiswrapper

The command "ndiswrapper -l" gives me:
installed drivers:
bcmwl5 driver installed, hardware (14E4:4328) present

and iwconfig shows the WLAN on eth1.  

one more reboot, and the BLUE network light is on, plus iwconfig shows the WLAN on eth1.  still no connection, but at least I have the right driver now.

=======================

DONE !!!

I had to disable the ethernet device, and now packets are routing through the WLAN.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-20
> **humphry said:**
> Help?

**[COLOR="Red"]TO ALL WHO REQUEST HELP:[/COLOR]**

**1) Please indicate:**
[LIST=1]
[*]your laptop model
[*]ubuntu version
[*]your lspci output
[*]the relevant rows from /var/log/syslog
[*]the relevant rows from dmesg
[*]any unexpected output when following the steps in the how-to
[/LIST]

2) **If your wireless card is being recognized by the system**, but you simply cannot connect to your desired wireless network, please understand that your issue is **beyond the scope of this how-to**, due largely to the fact that such issues are very complicated and often have absolutely nothing to do with the way you got ubuntu to recognize your wireless card.  That said, I will do what I can to help.  My first suggestion will **always** be to **turn off all encryption first** to see if you can at least connect to an open network.

Humphry, I'll be happy to help.  Please see above and post your results.

---

### Post by antifaithstl on 2007-04-21
This worked (after performing the "cleaning process" a couple times & rebooting at the point that some had suggested) on my HP Pavilion DV6205us laptop.  The key for me was looking in "system/preferences/hardware information" & seeing that my card (which in windoze is listed as Broadcom 802.11 b/g) was detected as a "Dell 1390 WLAN".  Searching for help w/ that brought me to this how-to, & to FINAL-FxCKING-LY getting my card to work & w/ WPA.  
There are WAY TOO MANY HOW-TOs for these damn broadcom cards.  It's rediculous.  After about the 10th install of various Ubuntu distros & never getting the damn card working, THIS was going to be the last damn how-to I was gonna try before cursing ubuntu forever.  Now, maybe I can make the full switch!
Anyway thanks for this how-to bro.

### Post by mistergq on 2007-04-22
Thank you.  I am typing this wireless. \\:D/ 

My only suggestion is emphasizing changing directories and to revise the black list to have this code:

```
sudo -s
echo blacklist bcm43xx >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```

I got tied up at the blacklist.  It was not until I became root that I could blacklist.

Also, I had to reboot after all the installation for Ubuntu to recognize the card.

Thanks for developing this how to: :popcorn:

### Post by humphry on 2007-04-23
Ok, I'm thorougly confused now. It worked earlier tonight.   Not only does the wireless no longer work, Fiesty doesn't even recognize that I have a wireless card anymore!  I find that triply weird because the little Wi-Fi LED remains on.  All I did was go to a different part of the college with a different router (same name and configuration, though).  Here is relevant information:


Dell Inspiron E1705 (again, just like 1505 but with a bigger screen)
Fiesty--just installed the other day.  
```
mdavidson@humphry:~$ lspci
00:00.0 Host bridge: Intel Corporation Mobile 945GM/PM/GMS/940GML and 945GT Express Memory Controller Hub (rev 03)
00:02.0 VGA compatible controller: Intel Corporation Mobile 945GM/GMS/940GML Express Integrated Graphics Controller (rev 03)
00:02.1 Display controller: Intel Corporation Mobile 945GM/GMS/940GML Express Integrated Graphics Controller (rev 03)
00:1b.0 Audio device: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) High Definition Audio Controller (rev 01)
00:1c.0 PCI bridge: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) PCI Express Port 1 (rev 01)
00:1c.1 PCI bridge: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) PCI Express Port 2 (rev 01)
00:1c.3 PCI bridge: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) PCI Express Port 4 (rev 01)
00:1d.0 USB Controller: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) USB UHCI #1 (rev 01)
00:1d.1 USB Controller: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) USB UHCI #2 (rev 01)
00:1d.2 USB Controller: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) USB UHCI #3 (rev 01)
00:1d.3 USB Controller: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) USB UHCI #4 (rev 01)
00:1d.7 USB Controller: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) USB2 EHCI Controller (rev 01)
00:1e.0 PCI bridge: Intel Corporation 82801 Mobile PCI Bridge (rev e1)
00:1f.0 ISA bridge: Intel Corporation 82801GBM (ICH7-M) LPC Interface Bridge (rev 01)
00:1f.2 IDE interface: Intel Corporation 82801GBM/GHM (ICH7 Family) Serial ATA Storage Controller IDE (rev 01)
00:1f.3 SMBus: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) SMBus Controller (rev 01)
03:00.0 Ethernet controller: Broadcom Corporation BCM4401-B0 100Base-TX (rev 02)
03:01.0 FireWire (IEEE 1394): Ricoh Co Ltd Unknown device 0832
03:01.1 Generic system peripheral [0805]: Ricoh Co Ltd R5C822 SD/SDIO/MMC/MS/MSPro Host Adapter (rev 19)
03:01.2 System peripheral: Ricoh Co Ltd Unknown device 0843 (rev 01)
03:01.3 System peripheral: Ricoh Co Ltd R5C592 Memory Stick Bus Host Adapter (rev 0a)
03:01.4 System peripheral: Ricoh Co Ltd xD-Picture Card Controller (rev 05)
0c:00.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Card (rev 01)
```

As for /var/log/syslog, it was working earlier and there are many linse talking about that, but looking at the time log, this is probably the only relevant line:
```
Apr 23 05:26:52 humphry dhclient: There is already a pid file /var/run/dhclient.eth1.pid with pid 134993416
```

dmesg has a couple lines about eth0, but nothing about eth1 or wlan.

Nothing unexpected during installation.  

Again, this worked fine earlier tonight and magically stopped working.  The wireless card works in windows.  

While I was typing this response, I rebooted a few times for good measure, and now the wi-fi LED is off.  

Thanks in advance.

EDIT:  the wi-fi LED is back on after a reboot into windows and then back into ubuntu, but my system still can't find eth1.

### Post by Trinisan on 2007-04-23
yesssssssssssssssssss!!! I can't even measure my enthusiasm.

I have the E1405 with the same wireless card.

I switched to this howto after fwcutter not working to well for me.
One extra step i had to make was change wlan0 to eth0 in /etc/modprobe.d/ndiswrapper.
I then got wifi-radar as there are a lot of APs where i live but i wasn't connecting. I saw
the APs, picked mine (the strongest signal :)) and I'm browsing here wirelessly now.

Thanks!!

5 minutes later, after a restart...

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-23
> **benfeldman said:**
> Hi.

First, please take my most sincere apologies because I'm sure this has been asked a hundred times already.

Here are my details:
Laptop: Dell Inspiron 1501
Wireless Card: Dell Wireless 1490 (the drivers are the same)
Ubuntu Feisty - 32 bit
AMD Turion 64x2

Okay, now I've gotten through every step successfully, until we get to the last step--checking for wireless networks. Here's what happens:



I'm currently "plugged" into the Internet using my Ethernet cable... so that at least works, but I would like wireless. :)

Thank you very much,
Ben

Edit: now that I am looking at System -> Administration -> Network, my wireless device, which appeared prior to following this How-To, isn't appearing any more. Is this a problem? Please forgive me once again, I know very little about these things.

If you've completed every step correctly and it still doesn't work, I'd try toggling the wifi hotkey.  Or, you could go in to your BIOS and set the wifi hotkey to always on / hotkey disabled.  If that doesn't work, please supply the log files (see original post for details).

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-23
> **humphry said:**
> I just don't understand it.  After a reboot, it stops working again.  Unless it was messed up by installing some things off of synaptic (tetex, python-visual, xscreensaver, gnome-art, folding@home client, and I think that's it), I'm destined not to be able to have wireless.  

5 minutes later, after a restart...

Working perfectly again.  What's going on?!?!?!?!?!?

Try disabling the wireless hotkey in your BIOS (set it to always on / hotkey disabled).

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-23
> **Parmenion said:**
> Ive got the same type of card but I'm on a Compaq Presario V3000.

Problems: 
[LIST=1]
[*]build-essentials has a problem
[*]i cant compile ndiswrapper
[/LIST]

Can i just use the normal ones from the ubuntu repos ?

EDIT: Below shows what happens when i "make install" ndiswrapper ...

If you had a problem installing build-essential (note the lack of "s" in essential), then you won't be able to compile the ndiswrapper program.  Are you sure you spelled it correctly?  Also, last time I checked, using the ndiswrapper in the repositories doesn't work because they use a very outdated version (which is why you have to compile it yourself).

### Post by Super_6_4 on 2007-04-23

HP Pavillion dv6000 series

### Post by Static-X on 2007-04-23
HI,  this "HowTo..." worked in Edgy but not in my Feisty.... I want  know Can i delete this ?

I put this command...

sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo apt-get remove ndiswrapper-utils

OK, but look the pic, later.... when check with Nautilus...  Thank you.

---

### Post by jeepville01 on 2007-04-23
Thanks a TON! I've been trying different work-arounds to get Wifi working on my Gateway MT6451 since Feisty was released last week.  This How-To works perfectly for me.  Only thing I had to do differently was the last part of Step 2 to blacklist the broken and useless bcm43xx firmware drivers that try to load in a default ubuntu install.  I had to do a **sudo -s** to become root because permission was denied to blacklist drivers otherwise.  You might want to add this as a caveat in step 2 for Feisty users.

---

### Post by Cbjaxx on 2007-04-24
Dell XPS M1710. 
Feisty Fawn
Confirmed working


Thanks a great deal for this post it has worked wonders for a great deal of people as I can see. Took me until about page 4 or so to realize I had not disabled my hotkey and once done it was working well. Very informative and educational.

---

### Post by surndliha on 2007-04-24
bcm43xx drivers for 4311 are now quite good, 
[http://bcm43xx.berlios.de/?go=devices](http://bcm43xx.berlios.de/?go=devices)
Supported for kernel 2.6.20.6 and later, but before that you can patch with larry patches [ftp://lwfinger.dynalias.org/patches](ftp://lwfinger.dynalias.org/patches)
I managed to get 1.65mb/sec at university on 2.6.20.3 with larry patch and same speed with 2.6.21-rc7 kernel, without the larry patch
Also tested speeds at home with iperf over WPA-TKIP protected network and got pretty much the same speed as with ndiswrapper

---

I managed to get 1.65mb/sec at university on 2.6.20.3 with larry patch and same speed with 2.6.21-rc7 kernel, without the larry patch

### Post by pratap on 2007-04-25
1. Model: Dell Inspiron 1501
   2. Feisty Linux 2.6.20-15-generic
   3. Lspci: Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Card (rev 01)
   4. dmesg: The only relevant error in dmesg is "ADDRCONF(NETDEV_UP): eth1: link is not ready"

I followed all the instructions as diligently as I could. However, this is the trouble

> sudo iwlist scanning 
lo        Interface doesn't support scanning.
eth0      Interface doesn't support scanning.
eth1   No scan results

Other wireless scanners including wifi-radar gives me zero results. I tried to enter the wireless details "ESSID" and key, but that connection also does not connect. I get the feeling that the problem i sbecause my driver is still not configured properly.

Any ideas on what is wrong? How do I know if the driver has been set up properly?

Thanks a lot for all the help already. I need the help for the last mile :-)
-pr

---

### Post by pratap on 2007-04-25
I solved the problem.

I had switched off the wireless using "Fn + F2". Once I switched it on, the "wi-fi" led started glowing and everything came back to normal. My wireless card is running like a dream :-)

-pr

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-04-29
> **loso said:**
> I've tried and failed :( here are my specs:

   1. Dell Inspiron 1501 with wireless 1490 802.11a/g mini card
   2. feisty desktop 7.04 64bit version

The only change I made to the suggested install was to use the R151520.exe driver from dell (I think that's the correct driver for my laptop..), and I installed the bcmwl6.inf one, as this driver didn't have a bcmwl5.inf file.  Oh, ya, and I couldn't uninstall ndiswrapper.  I did the command 100 times, and it still said that another one is installed.

please help me out, I've been trying all day :S

Don't use that driver.  I'm fairly certain that bcmwl6.inf is a vista driver, and it won't work with ndiswrapper.  Go to the dell website and download the 32 bit windows xp driver for your laptop, and use that.

As far as uninstalling the other version of ndiswrapper, you'll need to provide more details.  My bet is that you'll need to get this other version off of your system before this will work.

### Post by humphry on 2007-04-30
paperdiesel, just so you know, I got mine working great!  The problem was that I didn't have ndiswrapper running on startup, so I had the same problem ek0nomik had--I had to modprobe ndiswrapper at startup.  By adding "ndiswrapper" to /etc/modules, I got it working perfectly.  

Thanks!

### Post by dislocated on 2007-05-05
I've been having one hell of a time getting wireless up and running but this seemed to do the trick:

Instead of cleaning things up with this:
```
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo apt-get remove ndiswrapper-utils
```
I used this instead:
```
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
sudo apt-get remove ndiswrapper-utils
```
That puts me one step closer to cleaning Window$ off my hard drive for good! :)

Hope this helps people having trouble like I was.

---

### Post by ky4623 on 2007-05-08
I have an e1505, took me a while to get it right. i used the newest ndiswrapper and the newest winxp wireless driver from dell. and great guide thank you for putting it together.

/var/log/syslog:[/COLOR]
May  4 14:21:13 michael-laptop NetworkManager: <information>^IUpdating allowed wireless network lists. 
May  4 14:21:13 michael-laptop NetworkManager: <WARNING>^I nm_dbus_get_networks_cb (): error received: org.freedesktop.NetworkManagerInfo.NoNetworks - There are no wireless networks stored.. 

I realize my dmesg says I still have version 1.38 installed from an earlier try at this but shouldn't it have been taken out with the multiple repeats in Step 3 of sudo make uninstall?  I got to a point of no replies to that command
When I get to sudo ndiswrapper -l, I get:  bcmwl5  invalid driver!
Just to clarify--the driver name is bcmw_**l**_5, as in the letter "ell" & not the number one after bcmw, correct?  (they look so similar)

Correct, it's the letter l not the number 1.  And the most likely culprit here is that old version of ndiswrapper.  I'd try the sudo make uninstall command, do a sudo apt-get remove ndiswrapper-utils, and then do a locate ndiswrapper to see any other places on the system that might still have parts of the program.

### Post by mrrobertbates on 2007-05-14
I´m running feisty and have followed all of the steps in the guide.  Everything works great except that I have to modprobe ndiswrapper every time that I log on in order to get wireless working.  I tried ndiswrapper -m and it says there is already and alias directive.  What´s going on here?

-Rob

EDIT:  I found the solution. Ndiswrapper wasn't added to /etc/modules.  Anybody haven't a similar problem make sure you add ndiswrapper to the list at etc/modules.

### Post by jzsmart3 on 2007-05-22
Quick note if you are typing in the commands (instead of pasting from browser) and are not having success.  Kept having problems until realized that in the command:

sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf

the "l5" is not the number "15" but is instead the letter "l" followed by the number "5".

### Post by snitramc on 2007-05-23

(replacing the step that gets it from Dell) that I will be ok? Seems to make sense to me.

Also, I am curious about the linux-headers results. I see 4 different packages when I run the command inside Feisty:

linux-headers-2.6.20-15-server 2.6.20-15.27
linux-headers-2.6.20-15-lowlatency 2.6.20-15.27
inux-headers-2.6.20-15-generic 2.6.20-15.27
linux-headers-2.6.20-15 2.6.20-15.27

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-05-23
> **snitramc said:**
> (replacing the step that gets it from Dell) that I will be ok? Seems to make sense to me.

Should be fine.

> Also need help with the unzip. The driver from HP is an .exe. Is it safe to extract it in winblows, put it on a thumbdrive and copy it over to Feisty, and then do ndiswrapper -i bcmwl6.inf in the correct folder? I don't see how to unzip a self-extracting .exe in this case.

Yep, thumbdrive should work.  Keep in mind that if the windows driver you are using was intended for Windows Vista, then it probably won't work.  In that case, just grab the dell one from the link in the howto.

> Also, I am curious about the linux-headers results. I see 4 different packages when I run the command inside Feisty:

linux-headers-2.6.20-15-server 2.6.20-15.27
linux-headers-2.6.20-15-lowlatency 2.6.20-15.27
inux-headers-2.6.20-15-generic 2.6.20-15.27
linux-headers-2.6.20-15 2.6.20-15.27

Where do I look to find out which one should I install where I replace `uname -r` with the package?

Just copy/paste the command from the how-to.  That `uname -r` is using back-tics to execute a command within another command, and the apt-get should read it and pick the proper package automaticaly.  However, if it does not, my best guess is that since you're running a 64 bit processor, you probably downloaded the 64 bit version of ubuntu.  If that's true, get the inux-headers-2.6.20-15-generic 2.6.20-15.27 one.  If you're running 32 bit ubuntu, linux-headers-2.6.20-15 2.6.20-15.27 is probably what you want.

---

### Post by namrocks on 2007-05-25
Thanks to you, the 1390 card on my Dell Inspiron 1501 is now working perfectly. I installed Ubuntu with a separate partition for my home directory. I tried a clean install. All of my files and settings remained intact, but your 1390 setup was hosed. I tried your procedure on the new install and it failed. I did another clean install, including a reformat of the home partition, and your procedure, once again was flawless. Is it possible to do a clean install of just the operating system, without touching the home partition and still get the 1390 wireless card to work? If so, how would you suggest I go about doing that? Thank you again for taking the time to develop this process.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-05-25
> **namrocks said:**
> Thanks to you, the 1390 card on my Dell Inspiron 1501 is now working perfectly. I installed Ubuntu with a separate partition for my home directory. I tried a clean install. All of my files and settings remained intact, but your 1390 setup was hosed. I tried your procedure on the new install and it failed. I did another clean install, including a reformat of the home partition, and your procedure, once again was flawless. Is it possible to do a clean install of just the operating system, without touching the home partition and still get the 1390 wireless card to work? If so, how would you suggest I go about doing that? Thank you again for taking the time to develop this process.

It sound to me like this was a problem with having a previous ndiswrapper instance.  You certainly don't have to reformat a partition to get the wireless working.  Next time around, I'd suggest doing the apt-get remove step, the sudo make uninstall step, and then search the filesystem for any mention of ndiswrapper.  Be sure the old driver is unloaded first with a sudo ndiswrapper -e <driver>.

---

### Post by all4als on 2007-05-27
Complete guide. I admit that I did have some trouble. But that trouble was due to my ignorance of linux, as well as file structure. If you follow the instructions you should be up and running. Great Job Diesel. Here are my specs:

Dell Latitude D520
Fiesty Fawn 7.04

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-05-27
> **KyleSeigler said:**
> 
In the how-to, when i typed: sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
it output: driver bcmwl5 is already installed

And then i tried the next command because i wasn't sure if that was unexpected, which was: sudo ndiswrapper -l
and it output: bcmwl5 : invalid driver!

If the driver is already installed, then you didn't clean your system before you tried this how-to.  Go back and perform the clean steps first (see various posts thoughout this thread for examples) then try again.  You can't have a bcmwl5.inf driver installed prior to attempting this how-to.

---

Okay,

I'm new to ubuntu, but I have been successful in every part of this how to except the very last step of step 4. 

dave@dave-laptop:~/poop/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -m
module configuration already contains alias directive

dave@dave-laptop:~/poop/DRIVER$ sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
dave@dave-laptop:~/poop/DRIVER$ sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-05-29
> **cecilmoney said:**
> Okay,

I'm new to ubuntu, but I have been successful in every part of this how to except the very last step of step 4. 

dave@dave-laptop:~/poop/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -m
module configuration already contains alias directive

dave@dave-laptop:~/poop/DRIVER$ sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
dave@dave-laptop:~/poop/DRIVER$ sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
bash: /etc/modules: Permission denied


I have no idea what to do here and I have been unable to find anyone on this how-to with the same problem. 

Please help me or redirect me to a place where this problem is addressed. 

Thanks!

Try this instead:

```

sudo -s
echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
exit
```

### Post by jazzlsx on 2007-05-30
I have been trying everything under the sun to get my wireless card to work to the point where I don't even think Ubuntu knows it's there when I scan for it.
When I run:
       sudo iwlist scanning 
I get:
       lo        Interface doesn't support scanning.

       eth0      Interface doesn't support scanning.

I've tried going through the forum as well as others trying to find a solution, but I've been doing this for a few days now and I think I now need direct help.
When I run:
       lspci
I get:
             0c:00.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Card (rev 01)

I'm using a Dell Inspiron 640m laptop with Ubuntu Feisty Fawn 7.04.

Thank you in advance,
Jazz

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-05-30
> **joml88 said:**
> 
0b:00.0 Network controller: Intel Corporation PRO/Wireless 3945ABG Network Connection (rev 02)

Your lspci output shows that you do not have a broadcom wireless card -- you have an intel card.  This how-to is for people with broadcom cards.  That said, this method will PROBABLY still work, but you need to get the proper intel driver from dell.com, not the broadcom one.

---

### Post by ice60 on 2007-05-30
hi, it looks like you miss-spelt unzip. there may be something else, i'm not sure.

you can paste commands in to the terminal with ctrl-insert :)

EDIT sorry i didn't read on from the spelling mistake :|  

i shouldn't really have replied, but as i have i think you should look at the output of lspci and check the Network controller: line, it should be something like - Broadcom Corperation Dell Wireless1390 WLAN

---

you can paste commands in to the terminal with ctrl-insert :)

EDIT sorry i didn't read on from the spelling mistake :|  

i shouldn't really have replied, but as i have i think you should look at the output of lspci and check the Network controller: line, it should be something like - Broadcom Corperation Dell Wireless1390 WLAN

lspci:
0c:00.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Card (rev 01)

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-05-30
> **jazzlsx said:**
> 
jimmy@jimmy-laptop:~/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
driver bcmwl5 is already installed
jimmy@jimmy-laptop:~/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : invalid driver!
bcmwl5a : driver installed
mcmw15 : invalid driver!
mcmwi5 : invalid driver!

You have four different drivers installed!  You need to read my posts more carefully -- when cleaning your system, you need to remove ALL previous attempts to get ndiswrapper working.  Do a sudo ndiswrapper -e <driver> for each driver you have listed up there.  For example, the first command would be: ```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
```then you'd do bcmwl5a, etc.  Then try installing the new driver and pick back up with the how-to.

### Post by my_ilium on 2007-06-03
Thanks for the great tutorial! It went without a hiccup.

A caveat: When you unpack the Dell driver, all the unpacked files must stay where they are or  wireless won't work. Same for the ndiswrapper tarball. So don't do it in your home directory, unless you want fifty single files sitting there permanently. 

Myself, I managed to move the driver files to a different directory by saying "sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5" and then "sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf" in the new directory. But I still have a ndiswrapper-1.45/ (the unpacked ndiswrapper tarball) in my home directory, which I don't want. How do I move it? 

My wireless behaves strangely while the directory ndiswrapper-1.45 is in a different place.  "sudo iwlist scanning" brings up wireless networks, but Firefox can't find anything. 

Other than that, everything's peachy...thanks for posting this.

~JiLe

---

Thank you for a perfect instructional!

### Post by dgdubya on 2007-06-03
I'm suggesting that if you're new to Ubuntu and new to Linux that you invest in [Ubuntu Hacks from O'Reilly.]("http://www.oreilly.com/catalog/ubuntuhks/") That being said on page 154, Hack #41 Get proprietary Wireless Cards working they suggest the following. They too suggest "ndiswrapper" but they suggest an easier gui version of the program.

$sudo aptitude install ndisgtk

after installing ndisgtk, insert your wireless adapter and run* ndiskgtk*, i.e. $ ***sudo ndiskgtp***

ndiskgtk will present you with a window allowing you to install the new driver. You will still need to download the correct Broadcomm driver from Dell's site but now you have a little easier GUI interface if your not comfortable on the command line.

I also suggest you download the [One Page Linux Manual]("http://homepage.powerup.com.au/~squadron/"). It will be a big help to you.  ;)

---

### Post by fermirmen on 2007-06-03
Thank you very much for posting these instructions. My laptop is a HP Pavilion DV5218nr, with the same Wireless 1390 and it now works with Ubuntu 7.04!

Just as you suggested, I reinstalled Ubuntu to have a fresh start. I also had to download the  corresponding driver from hp.com and extract the files using cabextract instead of unzip -a. (Using the driver from dell.com does not work, had to remove /etc/ndiswrapper/bcmwl5/ manually and start again).

Your post was the most useful, but I also used these two:

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/1390?highlight=%28WifiDocs%2FDriver%29](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/1390?highlight=%28WifiDocs%2FDriver%29)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=407663](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=407663)

Thank you very much again, I appreciate your help.  Hope this helps anyone else with the same, or similar laptop.

> okay, so i tried again and went through the steps and what you told me to do twice, and both times when i input "sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf" i get:

[FONT="Courier New"]kyle@ubuntukyle:~/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
driver bcmwl5 is already installed[/FONT]

I'm not sure what now to do. I thought i removed everything by cleansing my system and manually removing the binaries...I'm so sorry to keep bothering you, thank you for your help!

You need to remove the old driver first.  sudo ndiswrapper -e <driver> does the trick.  e.g. sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5.

You can delete or move the ndiswrapper tarball.  Your wireless setup does not care about that file after you've extracted the contents.

### Post by genti on 2007-06-07
I am using Feisty and I do **not** need the ndiswrapper.
I checked the homepage for the **bcm43xx** driver and they are supposed to support my chipset 
4311, you can find that from the Device Manager
**pci.product_id int 17169 (0x4311)**
Am I wrong?
The great guys at [http://bcm43xx.berlios.de/?go=devices](http://bcm43xx.berlios.de/?go=devices) say that the chipset should be supported.
Just in case I did a search in **Synaptic** for Broadcom and I found **bcm43xx-fwcutter**

That seemed interesting.

Now with kernel **2.6.20-16** and that, I have native wireless support for the wireless card.

I have to admit that with ndiswrapper I was picking up more networks, the ones with poor signal are not showing up while the ones that had a good signal now show still as good signal, even better.
Either the driver is omitting some and adding power to the others or my neighbours have been moving around :)

### Post by JamesGrimlee on 2007-06-08
Hey there. I picked up a brand new HP Pavilion laptop(which happens to come with the same cheapo Broadcom wireless) and have been without any reliable wireless for over a month now. I've briefly got it working before with the bcm fwcutter but speeds were super slow and connections were unreliable. I exchanged the dell driver download with one from HP's website, but I'm now running at my usual wireless speed and everything seems to be working as normal, which means that I've now replaced that useless Vista partition I was dual booting with something much more useful: the new Gutsy alpha release. Anyhow, thanks a bunch for the walkthrough.

It's hard to tell.  My first guess would be that you didn't do the first steps -- the sudo apt-get update, install inux-headers, etc.  Or maybe you did and then updated to a new kernel.  Run those first steps again and try installing after that.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-06-08
> **JamesGrimlee said:**
> Hey there. I picked up a brand new HP Pavilion laptop(which happens to come with the same cheapo Broadcom wireless) and have been without any reliable wireless for over a month now. I've briefly got it working before with the bcm fwcutter but speeds were super slow and connections were unreliable. I exchanged the dell driver download with one from HP's website, but I'm now running at my usual wireless speed and everything seems to be working as normal, which means that I've now replaced that useless Vista partition I was dual booting with something much more useful: the new Gutsy alpha release. Anyhow, thanks a bunch for the walkthrough.

This has been my exact experience when I try to get the fwcutter method to work.  Most of the time it simply won't work, but even when I can get it to recognize my wireless card, the speeds are awful and there's all sort of quirks and bugs that make it unreliable.  

Not surprisingly, this is also why I get so frustrated when people come in here and post "This method is useless!  Just use fwcutter!  It works on my machine!".  99% of the time, the reliability and quality of the connection from fwcutter (for this chipset) is very poor.

Anyway, thanks for the feedback -- I'm glad you found this useful.

### Post by phoenix_sc on 2007-06-12
This worked great on my Dell Latitude D620.

I did the following to get build-essential Without Network Access.
(I insert my Feisty Fawn Ubuntu Live CD)
(And then type these commands.)

sudo apt-cdrom add
sudo aptitude update
sudo aptitude install build-essential

I have tried a bunch of other things, and this is the only How-To that I could get to work.
(P.S. wicd is a little client that you can use if you don't want to mess with the annoying keyring manager.)

### Post by bmartin on 2007-06-13
[Here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=457371")'s a post that simplifies the process. It performs the compilation/installation of ndiswrapper for you and it'll download the driver as necessary. All instructions are included. The current version uses ndiswrapper 1.45, but 1.46 and 1.47 packages will be available in about 5 hours.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-06-13
> **koxx1100 said:**
> I am a complete computer nOOb.

Do I need to be already connected to the internet for this wireless setup to work?

It would certainly help, but it's not necessary.  Alternatively, you could use a separate computer to download the required packages, and then transfer those packages via CD to the target computer.  I think that in most cases, it's easiest to connect your laptop using its wired connection, follow the how-to, then unplug the wire when you're ready to test/start your wireless.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-06-13
> **Confusedalot said:**
> I'm new and i dont know what you mean when you say" in the terminal, go to the directory where you extracted ndiswrapper"
how do i do that?

It's true that this how-to assumes a basic level of understanding of linux commands.  To open a terminal, you'll need to navigate to applications -> accessories -> terminal (might also be called "console").  

Once the terminal is open, to change directories you type ```
cd <directory>
``` where <directory> is the name of your target directory.  e.g. cd /home/user/ndiswrapper-1.46.

I bet that if you look around in other how-tos, the linux basics are covered in much better detail than I can do here.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-06-14
> **pontifex3 said:**
> Hi, im having an error when i run sudo ndiswrapper -l, it says im using an invalid driver
any help widely appreciated, thanks!
here is my info:

the error i get!
```

oyuki@Keroberos:~$ sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
installing bcmwl5 ...
couldn't open bcmwl5.inf: No existe el fichero ó directorio at /usr/sbin/ndiswrapper line 217.
oyuki@Keroberos:~/driver$ sudo ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : invalid driver!
oyuki@Keroberos:~/driver$ sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
driver bcmwl5 is already installed
oyuki@Keroberos:~/driver$ sudo ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : invalid driver!

```

It looks like you tried to install the driver but were in the wrong directory.  You need to be in the directory that contains the bcmwl5.inf file in order for that command to work.

First, remove the invalid driver with sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5.  Then go to the proper directory, and install the driver.

---

### Post by ghopper on 2007-06-15
This worked fine on my laptop.  Thanks.
Model: Dell Precision M65
Ubuntu Version: Fiesty Fawn 7.04
lspci output: 0c:00.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Card (rev 01) 

I did not need to compile ndiswrapper.  the bundled version (1.35) appears to work fine.  Here is what I did:

1. Make sure that “ndiswrapper” and “ndiswrapper-utils” are installed.  Use synaptic package manager, or type 
```
sudo apt-get install ndiswrapper-common ndiswrapper-utils-1.9

```

2. Download and the NDIS driver for the Broadcom 1390.
```
wget [http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R151517.EXE](http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R151517.EXE)
unzip -a R151517.EXE
cd DRIVER

```

3. Install and configure the NDIS driver (as root):
```
sudo -s
ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
ndiswrapper -l
ndiswrapper -m
modprobe ndiswrapper
echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
echo blacklist bcm43xx >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-bcm

```

You might need to reboot.

---

### Post by walkerk on 2007-06-20
I wrote a small how to for connecting wireless using Wifi-Radar w/ WPA on a  Dell Inspiron E1505/6400 (also works for any laptop/card) for those of you who dislike Network Manager. The wifi-radar daemon allows you to start up your preffered wireless connection at start up. If interested check out: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=478612]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=478612")

---

### Post by nutz on 2007-06-21
This worked for a Latitude D620 with the same card.

---

### Post by Vulcan on 2007-06-21
I finally got it to work.

Here's the key, guys. Check your spelling! 

sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf

That's an L, not a 1!

---

### Post by t3hbl00dlu5t on 2007-06-21
> **Vulcan said:**
> I finally got it to work.

Here's the key, guys. Check your spelling! 

sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf

That's an L, not a 1!

We aren't morons dude. Dont you think I tried that?

---

### Post by Vulcan on 2007-06-21
> **t3hbl00dlu5t said:**
> We aren't morons dude. Dont you think I tried that?

So you're saying I'm a moron because the "l" looked like a "1" to me? Gee thanks. :roll:

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-06-21
> **t3hbl00dlu5t said:**
> At the 
```
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo ndiswrapper -l
```
stage. I got a "command not found" error, tell me what I need to post in order for me to give more iniformation to you so you can help me. 

Thanks alot.

If you're getting a command not found error, then it means that ndiswrapper wasn't installed correctly.  Did you get any errors when you were doing the make or make install commands?  If so, what were they?

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-06-21
> **Vulcan said:**
> I finally got it to work.

Here's the key, guys. Check your spelling! 

sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf

That's an L, not a 1!

Copy / Paste is your friend!

It looks like you didn't get the build-essential package correctly, or the linux-header files.  Did you do those two steps?  Are you sure you got the headers for the right kernel using `uname -r`?

### Post by thomanski on 2007-06-22
Brilliant,

this has worked for me (I've only tried an unsecured network), although I don't have the Dell machine but a Lenovo 3000 C200 of type 8922-AZG (Celeron). I bought this machine off ebay, so I don't know if the wireless networking mini pci card is the one Lenovo has sold it with or if the seller had replaced it and thus I don't know if this will work for anybody else, but if your lspci output is the same as mine I don't see why it shouldn't. It's "Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Card (rev 01)".

Notable is that the NetworkManager applet that Ubuntu places on the top bar doesn't "get it": It doesn't allow me to connect and it shows no connection even after I connected with wifi-radar.

As wifi-radar involves another manual step that I'm not keen to do every time I boot up I then did an
  apt-get install network-manager
and ever since I've been online shortly after reboot. I'm not sure what the relationship between the so-called "NetworkManager Applet" on the top bar and the network-manager I just installed is, but I can report that the applet still doesn't realise we have a connection.

In any case, it doesn't matter much, I'm online, I'm happy.

Thanks a lot,
Thomas

---

### Post by jefrazie on 2007-06-23
Hey, when i first used this guide to configure and install my wireless, worked great. But somehow, without knowing what i have done, my wireless has stopped working. I am unable to recognize it when i open network configurations. There is not even a wireless option. So i decided to come back to the guide and try to reinstall. All the steps worked up until,
 ```
sudo ndiswrapper -l
```
At this step the output code was,
```
frazier@frazier-laptop:~$ cd DRIVER
frazier@frazier-laptop:~/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
driver bcmwl5 is already installed
frazier@frazier-laptop:~/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : invalid driver!

```
When i run an iwlist scan it says that both lo and eth0 dont support scanning
iwconfig says that neither lo nor eth0 have a wireless extension
if i remember correctly when i last had my wireless working, it ran under eth1
please get back to me as soon as possible

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-06-23
> **jefrazie said:**
> Hey, when i first used this guide to configure and install my wireless, worked great. But somehow, without knowing what i have done, my wireless has stopped working. I am unable to recognize it when i open network configurations. There is not even a wireless option. So i decided to come back to the guide and try to reinstall. All the steps worked up until,
 ```
sudo ndiswrapper -l
```
At this step the output code was,
```
frazier@frazier-laptop:~$ cd DRIVER
frazier@frazier-laptop:~/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
driver bcmwl5 is already installed
frazier@frazier-laptop:~/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : invalid driver!

```
When i run an iwlist scan it says that both lo and eth0 dont support scanning
iwconfig says that neither lo nor eth0 have a wireless extension
if i remember correctly when i last had my wireless working, it ran under eth1
please get back to me as soon as possible

My guess is that you updated your kernel, which means you'll have to remove the ndiswrapper driver, reboot, recompile ndiswrapper, then reinstall the driver.  See previous posts for more details.

---

### Post by Niomi on 2007-06-24
This worked for me. I own an HP with the same wireless card in it.
Every now and then connection manager refuses to connect after the PC has been on for a long time. I'm not sure if this is the fault of the wireless driver or not. However, I can fix this with a reboot.

### Post by JohnStrand on 2007-06-25
This worked Great! I actually used a combination of this and the ndisgtk application, which is a great GUI to get the wireless working. It works great now! Thanks so much for the How-To!

### Post by eddycyclone on 2007-06-25
You are the man.  As of this post I used ndiswrapper-1.47 as that was the latest.  The link was the same except for the version so no problem. I'm new to Linux so it was probably something silly but I got around it by sudo vi'ing the file and manually adding ndiswrapper to the end.  Thanks again.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-06-30
> **jpbefx said:**
> 
jpbefx@jpbefx-laptop:~$ cd /home/jpbefx 
jpbefx@jpbefx-laptop:~$ sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf 
installing bcmwl5 ... 
couldn't open bcmwl5.inf: No such file or directory at /usr/sbin/ndiswrapper line 217. 

This is where you went wrong.  You need to execute that command from WITHIN the DRIVER directory that was created when you unzipped the driver from Dell.  You'll need to do a sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5 first to remove that invalid driver.

---

### Post by blendersfun on 2007-07-01

The directions were clear and precise, and left nothing out. (it did assume I knew how to navigate with a terminal, but that's pretty basic). I am a new user, and I can't tell you what a relief it is to have my internet working.

Much gratitude.

Aaron

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-07-03
Stef, you have two problems that I can see, and I think that both of them stem from the fact that you're operating as root.  Don't do that -- just open a terminal and execute these commands as your normal user.

> **steffey02 said:**
> 

steph@steph-laptop:/root$ sudo apt-get install build-essential
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree       
Reading state information... Done
The following extra packages will be installed:
  dpkg-dev g++ g++-4.1 libc6-dev libstdc++6-4.1-dev linux-libc-dev
Suggested packages:
  debian-keyring gcc-4.1-doc lib64stdc++6 glibc-doc manpages-dev
  libstdc++6-4.1-doc
The following NEW packages will be installed:
  build-essential dpkg-dev g++ g++-4.1 libc6-dev libstdc++6-4.1-dev
  linux-libc-dev
0 upgraded, 7 newly installed, 0 to remove and 0 not upgraded.
Need to get 8053kB of archives.
After unpacking 33.7MB of additional disk space will be used.
Do you want to continue [Y/n]? y
Abort.

This should not say "Abort".  Try doing this again as your normal user.  And make sure you select "y" when prompted.

> steph@steph-laptop:/root$ wget [http://superb-east.dl.sourceforge.net/sourceforge/ndiswrapper/ndiswrapper-1.46.tar.gz](http://superb-east.dl.sourceforge.net/sourceforge/ndiswrapper/ndiswrapper-1.46.tar.gz)
--22:41:00--  [http://superb-east.dl.sourceforge.net/sourceforge/ndiswrapper/ndiswrapper-1.46.tar.gz](http://superb-east.dl.sourceforge.net/sourceforge/ndiswrapper/ndiswrapper-1.46.tar.gz)
           => `ndiswrapper-1.46.tar.gz'
Resolving superb-east.dl.sourceforge.net... 209.160.66.130
Connecting to superb-east.dl.sourceforge.net|209.160.66.130|:80... connected.
HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 200 OK
Length: 198,155 (194K) [application/x-gzip]
ndiswrapper-1.46.tar.gz: Permission denied
Cannot write to `ndiswrapper-1.46.tar.gz' (Permission denied).

Again, don't do this as root.  I think that's your problem.

### Post by lucaa on 2007-07-04
> **paperdiesel said:**
> If you are using a Dell laptop that's **NOT** model E1505, you need to go to Dell.com and search for the drivers that correspond to your specific model.  Use those instead.

Actually this worked for my Compaq presario laptop too, because I had the same model for the wireless card: Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Card (rev 01).

Thanks a million times for this HOW-TO!

### Post by punjabigator on 2007-07-04
Hello,
This post was very helpful to me and I was able to install my wireless card on Latitude D620. I executed sudo iwlist scanning i can see that my wireless connection is being broadcasting in 'b-mode'. However I am unable to connect using Network Tools ver 2.18. I can see the wireless connections in the network settings but unable to connect. I have disabled any key associated with connection. Wired connection works fine. I have tried all options to solve this problem. I will appreciate your help in this matter. If this start working I will say good by to VISTA, pain in the neck!

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-04
> **ross350 said:**
> 00:00.0 Host bridge: ATI Technologies Inc RS480 Host Bridge (rev 10)
00:01.0 PCI bridge: ATI Technologies Inc RS480 PCI Bridge
00:05.0 PCI bridge: ATI Technologies Inc Unknown device 5a37
00:06.0 PCI bridge: ATI Technologies Inc RS480 PCI Bridge
00:12.0 SATA controller: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 Non-Raid-5 SATA
00:13.0 USB Controller: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 USB (OHCI0)
00:13.1 USB Controller: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 USB (OHCI1)

00:13.3 USB Controller: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 USB (OHCI3)
00:13.4 USB Controller: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 USB (OHCI4)
00:13.5 USB Controller: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 USB Controller (EHCI)
00:14.0 SMBus: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 SMBus (rev 13)
00:14.1 IDE interface: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 IDE
00:14.2 Audio device: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 Azalia
00:14.3 ISA bridge: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 PCI to LPC Bridge
00:14.4 PCI bridge: ATI Technologies Inc SB600 PCI to PCI Bridge
00:18.0 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] HyperTransport Technology Configuration
00:18.1 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] Address Map
00:18.2 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] DRAM Controller
00:18.3 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] Miscellaneous Control
01:05.0 VGA compatible controller: ATI Technologies Inc RS482 [Radeon Xpress 200M]
08:00.0 Ethernet controller: Broadcom Corporation BCM4401-B0 100Base-TX (rev 02)
08:01.0 Generic system peripheral [0805]: Ricoh Co Ltd R5C822 SD/SDIO/MMC/MS/MSPro Host Adapter (rev 19)
08:01.1 System peripheral: Ricoh Co Ltd Unknown device 0843 (rev 01)

hmm... looking at your previous posts, the installation of the driver all went successfully. And looking at your lspci... it is missing the line. I get this:
```
jw5801@jw5801-laptop:~$ lspci | grep Broadcom
05:00.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Card (rev 01)
08:00.0 Ethernet controller: Broadcom Corporation BCM4401-B0 100Base-TX (rev 02)
```
It could be something as simple as the WiFi key being toggled off. Try toggling it (on my keyboard there's a key with a blue Fn on it to the right of the left Ctrl, and if you press that in combination with F2, which should have a blue wireless symbol on it, then it should toggle your WiFi on and off). I worked that out three days after my cat stepped on it and turned it off so I thought my driver install had broken, three days later (after a fresh install of feisty and reinstalling everything :P) I figured out that that was what paperdiesel was continually referring to.
If that doesn't get you anywhere report back. Actually, did the laptop come with Ubuntu preinstalled? Or with Windows. If it came with Windows did you notice whether the wireless worked under that? Might be worth checking 'cause if it doesn't, then I think Dell are entitled to a nasty phone call.
As for the USB wireless network adaptors, I have a Netgear one (that my ISP gave me for some reason) and it worked out of the box with Ubuntu. In fact it was what I used to access my network to download ndiswrapper and the driver for my internal card.
Hope I've been some help!
Jason

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-05
Yeah, Windows actually has absolutely terrible driver support straight out of the box. Dell would have given you a couple of Restore CDs full of their pre-loaded software and drivers, so don't forget to chuck those in as well. If it still doesn't recognise your card after that then I'd be ringing Dell and seeing what they have to say for themselves. They're normally pretty helpful... at least until I had a go at them trying to work out why they don't ship wih Ubunutu outside of the US ](*,)

I don't know what I am doing incorrectly here. Thanks.

Ok. First off if you don't want your post to be so huge then you can enclose your terminal output in ["code]["/code] tags and it will appear like I made it above.(Remove the "quotation" marks though, I only put those there so that the tags would actually show and you'd know what I was talking about ;))
Now as for your issues. The install all went good as far as I can see. You fell down at installing the driver. Now, when you use "unzip -a R151517.EXE" it extracts the executable into whatever directory you are in. So you were correct when you tried to "cd" into the driver folder in your home folder as you did first off. The issue was however that the folder was named wrong. Make sure you use caps (Linux sees "DRIVER" as different to "driver"). Also you need to put a space after the "ndiswrapper -i" otherwise it will think the driver name is another option you want it to use (which is why it gave you usage info instead of telling you it couldn't find the driver).
So, provided you haven't changed anything since you last attempted all this, try the following:
```
cd ~/DRIVER
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo ndiswrapper -l
```
and see what happens. Also instead of:
```
sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
```
try:
```
sudo -s
echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
exit
```
If none of that works then, try starting again and following the advice above. Or simply copy and paste from paperdiesel's excellent HOWTO, that way nothing can go wrong because of a typo ;).

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-05
haha! It did look alot like there wasn't a wireless card from the "lspci". I'd assumed you'd got it from Dell, that's why I was alluding them receiving a nasty phone call from you :p
Well I can tell you that a Ubuntu has exceptional support for USB Wireless cards. Other than that I'm not too sure about inbuilt wireless cards, this being the only one I've ever used.
USB could be a quick fix option, but I can imagine it wouldn't be particularly convenient having it stick out the side of your laptop all the time. If you can spare the time to install your own then that's probably better in the long run. Have a look around the forums, I'm sure there's a thread somewere recommending decent Wireless cards for use in Ubuntu. Otherwise, Google is your friend!
Don't be too hard on yourself, I spent 3 days reinstalling Ubuntu and running this guide a ridiculous amount of times after I thought my cat somehow killed it :lol:
Cheers for the laugh!
Jason

### Post by sharky44 on 2007-07-06
(Maybe this has already been addressed in these 59 pages of posts...sorry I did not read them all :-)

For those of you with **Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Cards**, the kernel bcm43xx driver now supports those cards since kernel release 2.6.20.6  (check the [bcm43xx site ]("http://bcm43xx.berlios.de/?go=devices")for details on what is supported).

So, if you would rather not deal with ndiswrapper, you can instead use the bcm43xx driver from the kernel.  NOTE that you have to do a little extra work (installing firmware) to get it to work (explained here:  [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx")).

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-06
> **go602 said:**
> wonder if i could get a little help. I can successfully get to step four, how ever when i run the
 'sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf' command I get a no file or directory error. Then I'll run it again and it will say file already installed. I'm very new to this command line stuff and think that after I unzip the file I'm not in the right directory to run this command. How do I find the directory? Isn't the directory the one I'm in when I unzip the file? Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks.

As I mentioned to Steph on the previous page, the driver itself is found inside the DRIVER folder (note that the case is important). For some commandline pointers, if you're wondering what is contained within the current folder type "ls" (without the quotation marks), or if you want to find a specific file (eg. bcmwl5.inf) type ```
locate bcmwl5.inf
```
or if you want to search a specific directory, find is a better option
```
find ~ -name bcmwl5.inf
```
will search your home directory (~) for bcmwl5.inf.
I'm not entirely sure what it thinks it's installed if it's reporting back that it can't find the file, but it should be in the DRIVER folder once the .exe has been extracted.

> **snwbord2456 said:**
> 1. Inspiron E1505
2. 7.04
I got up to the step where you enter:

sudo ndiswrapper -l

I now get an invalid driver error
Can you give the terminal output of the error? Also were there any other unexpected messages during the install process?

No problems at all! Happy networking! :p

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-07-06
> **snwbord2456 said:**
> mark@Mark:~/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
driver bcmwl5 is already installed
mark@Mark:~/DRIVER$ sudo ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : invalid driver!

It looks like you had made a previous attempt to get ndiswrapper working, and then tried to load ndiswrapper with another driver on top of the previous one.  You'll need to remove the current driver and then install the correct one -- BE SURE that you're in the DRIVER directory that was created when you extracted the .EXE from dell.

You'll need to do a sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5 to remove the invalid driver before you attempt to install the good one.

JW: Thanks for the help with questions.  Much obliged. 

Steph: Glad you're online!

Sharky: They've been "claiming" that it works for over two years now.  Yet, amazingly, it never really does (or if it works, it's slow and buggy).  Thank you, please drive through.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-07
Roanthon:
You need to be inside the DRIVER (note the caps) directory when you try to install the driver. So provided you haven't changed anything, This should fix it:
```
cd
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
cd DRIVER
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo ndiswrapper -l
```

---

### Post by ysgundam on 2007-07-09
Thanks to all the information in this thread. I have followed it carefully 
in DELL Inspiron 9400 and DELL WLAN 1390 mini-card. Linux is Scientific Linux CERN version 4.5 .
I spent two days to figure out how to use 'ndiswrapper' correctly.

I've been in trouble for a while in the stage of

> modprobe ndiswrapper
FATAL: Error inserting ndiswrapper
(/lib/modules/2.6.9-55.EL.cernsmp/kernel/drivers/net/wireless/ndiswrapper/nd
iswrapper.ko): Unknown symbol in module, or unknown parameter (see dmesg)

>dmesg | grep ndiswrapper
ndiswrapper: Unknown symbol task_nice

As a 'trial-and-error', I did download the most recent version of ndiswrapper ....
> wget [http://superb-east.dl.sourceforge.net/sourceforge/ndiswrapper/ndiswrapper-1.47.tar.gz](http://superb-east.dl.sourceforge.net/sourceforge/ndiswrapper/ndiswrapper-1.47.tar.gz)
and then compiled it...
> make 
> make install

After this, 'modprobe ndiswrapper' works and light is on in wifi lit.

But, still I couldn't get a signal from WPA2 AEGIS setup router. So, temporarily, I disabled them.
Then, still I couldn't get a signal.
I found that ESSID is blank when I type
> /sbin/iwconfig
So, I did set up ESSID manually like
> /sbin/iwconfig wlan0 essid linksys
> /sbin/ifdown wlan0
> /sbin/ifup wlan0

Then, I could get connected.
Please, let me know how to set it up to get connected through WPA2 router.

I appreciate you guys help a lot and I hope you all get connected in linux. I believe it.

WP

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-12
> **maluka said:**
> i'm getting stuck at step 3:



what does this part mean: In a terminal, go to the directory where you extracted ndiswrapper and execute the following

In terminal type "cd" followed by the directory in which you extracted ndiswrapper. If you used the terminal command (tar -xzf ndiswrapper-1.46.tar.gz), then it'll be as follows:
```
cd ndiswrapper-1.46
```
If you're still in the directory you executed the first command in.

### Post by uplinked on 2007-07-13
hardKNOXbz:
You're in the wrong directory. You have to do "sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf" in the directory that bcmwl5.inf is in.

CD to the DRIVER directory under where you extracted the R151517.exe file.
(If you were in ~ when you performed 'unzip -a R151517.exe', then you want ~\DRIVER. I used ~\.drivers\wireless\ for my unzip, so my DRIVER is at ~\.drivers\wireless\DRIVER)
Perform "sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf" from there, and you should be alright.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-07-13
> **hardKNOXbz said:**
> 

lookind a little better but hmm.....

[COLOR="Red"]EDIT 2[/COLOR]

ok i restarted and my wireless was pickeed up..... what a relief... thanks uplinked

You still might run in to problems because it looks like you have two driver entries in ndiswrapper.  It wouldn't be a bad idea to uninstall both entries (execute this twice -- sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5) then go to the DELL DRIVER directory and install the driver.  I'm not sure what having an invalid driver entry in ndiswrapper will do to your wireless -- especially on subsequent reboots, suspends, etc.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-13
> **cobalaminco said:**
> 
...
cobalamin@The-Box:~$ sudo ndiswrapper -l
**bcmwl6** : driver installed
        device (14E4:4311) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)
...

Shouldn't that read as "bcmwl5"? :p
Try ```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl6
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5
```
making sure that you're in the DRIVER directory you extracted from R151517.exe.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-16
Ok Skrypt. You weren't in the directory containing the driver you tried to install when first you did it. So you need to go back to that directory. It should be called DRIVER (the upper case is important) and it'll be in the same directory as R151517.EXE. If it's not there then redo the unzip R151517.EXE step. If you want to see what's in a directory using the command line use the "ls" command.

Once you make it to the DRIVER directory, put the following:```

sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5
```

For the permissions error try:```

sudo -s
echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
exit
```

If you need a more detailed explanation have a look at the last couple of pages of this thread as a few people have had the same issue!

### Post by rouge568 on 2007-07-22
Hello. I'm sorry if this question has been answered, but I am not going through 63 pages of posts to find out.
I am running a freshly installed (and updated) Ubuntu 7.04 on an inspiron e1505. I went through the instructions and they worked pefectly until I got to the end. 

> scott@scott-laptop:~$ sudo ndiswrapper -l 
bcmwl5 : driver installed
        device (14E4:4311) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)
scott@scott-laptop:~$ sudo modprobe ndiswrapper 
scott@scott-laptop:~$ sudo iwlist scanning
lo        Interface doesn't support scanning.

eth0      Interface doesn't support scanning.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-22
> **rock1231 said:**
> Installed ndis drivers:
bcmwl5  invalid driver!
bcmwl6          driver present, hardware present
r151517.exe     invalid driver!

That's what I get.

I did the instruction to clean the system before setting up the wireless and my laptop is an E1505 laptop. I'm not quite sure how that can be invalid...

Any ideas? (there were some problems when installing ndiswrapper so I had to find/install it using Synaptic Package Manager)
Ok. There are numerous possibilities. The first is that this driver doesn't work with the really old version of ndiswrapper that's in the repo's. The more likely option though, is that you've tried to install the wrong driver, or the right driver from the wrong folder. To fix it "cd" to the directory you have r151517.exe in, and do the following:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -r R151517.EXE
cd DRIVER
sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5
sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl6

```
and then try reinstalling the correct driver whilst in the DRIVER directory:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5
```

> **rouge568 said:**
> Hello. I'm sorry if this question has been answered, but I am not going through 63 pages of posts to find out.
I am running a freshly installed (and updated) Ubuntu 7.04 on an inspiron e1505. I went through the instructions and they worked pefectly until I got to the end. 
```
 scott@scott-laptop:~$ sudo ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : driver installed
device (14E4:4311) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)
scott@scott-laptop:~$ sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
scott@scott-laptop:~$ sudo iwlist scanning
lo Interface doesn't support scanning.

eth0 Interface doesn't support scanning.

**eth1 No scan results**
```


As seen, the modprobe command does nothing at all, and the scan turns up zilch. The green wifi light has not turned on since I got rid of the preinstalled vista, and my BIOS is fine. Could anyone please help?

The modprobe command isn't meant to output anything if it runs correctly, and the scan is just there to make sure it's working correctly, which it appears to be from that output. eth1 is your wireless card, and apparently it's working correctly there just aren't any wireless networks in range. If your wifi light isn't on, then maybe you could check to make sure you blacklisted the bcm43xx driver correctly. Open /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist with a text editor:```
gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
``` and check to make sure there's a line in there that reads "blacklist bcm43xx."

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-29
You'll need to remove the "driver" you installed the first time you tried (it still makes all the entries even if there's nothing to put in them.), so go back to the DRIVER directory and remove the faulty driver then install the proper driver:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
```
then continue on with the HowTo.

---

### Post by Apex-jb on 2007-07-29
Do I need to remove it from the directory I was in before I went to the DRIVER directory or do I just do it all in the DRIVER dir.?

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-07-29
As far as I know it shouldn't really matter where you run the first command (note that it doesn't have the .inf on the end so it's not a call to a file, but to the ndiswrapper list of drivers), but if you want to be on the safe side running it in the directory you initially tried to install it from is your best bet. The second command definately needs to be run in the DRIVER directory though.

---

### Post by bmartin on 2007-07-29
When you remove the old bcmwl5, you can be in any directory. When you install the new one, you have to be in the same directory as the .inf and .sys files.

---

I'm not sure if that's what the folder was called but if you look at the output you got when you ran the extract command then you should be able to see what the folder is called, else use the "ls" command.
