### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-08
If it's a different driver shouldn't the .inf and .sys files be named something different? Or do they always get named the same thing... no idea.
Anyway, "Invalid Driver" normally means you tried to run the driver install from a folder that didn't contain the .inf and .sys files. So first remove the dodgy driver:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5
```
also if you don't know what the other 3 there are for then remove them too (same command as above just replace bcmwl5 wih the equivalent output, don't put .inf or .sys on the end of anything. You can include the .ini on the end of bcmwls.ini as that shouldn't be there anyway). Then cd to the directory you unzipped the .exe into and find the necessary .inf file and run "ndiswrapper -i" from the directory it's in.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-08
> **Baby Boy said:**
> ...there is a message 'invalid driver!'...
This message pretty much always means you tried to install the driver without actually being in the folder containing bcmwl5.inf (ndiswrapper isn't smart enough to tell you that, it'll just create the driver entry anyway then complain that it's invalid).

So, use the "cd" command to go to the folder you extracted the .zip into, then run:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5
cd DRIVER #note the upper-case, it's important
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
```
It might be a good idea before you run the last command there to use the "ls" command to verify that bcmwl5.inf & bcmwl5.sys are both in the folder.

Make uninstall won't tell you if there aren't any files to remove, only when there are. So you'll only see a different output if you had a previous install of ndiswrapper on your system that it needed to remove. If you want to test to make sure the install is correct then run "ndiswrapper -v" and it should report that the version is 1.46 or whatever we're up to.

Finally, of the 60-odd pages in this thread, around 60% has been this question being answered, so maybe paperdiesel might like to add a troubleshooting section to the original post?

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-08
Go to the directory where your ndiswrapper tarball is and run the following:
```
tar -xzvf ndiswrapper-1.46.tar.gz
cd ndiswrapper-1.46
sudo make uninstall                   #Several times until you no longer get messages about removing files
sudo make
sudo make install
``` then pick up where you left off

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-09
> **Baby Boy said:**
> ...Plus, when I try to connect with my PocketPC through wireless there is no network found on my PPC (I know that I can't actually sync this way but at least the wireless network should be detected right?).
Help?

There aren't any wireless networks nearby you which is why neither your wireless card nor your PocketPC can detect any. Your PPC won't be able to 'see' if your wireless card is operational: having a working driver only enables you to connect to an existing network. It is possible to create an ad hoc access point through your wireless card (in which case the PPC would be able to see it) but you need a card capable of this feature and additional software. I'm not really sure how to go about this therefore I have no idea if the Broadcom 1390 is capable, however I'm sure a quick search of the forums would yield some results!

[QUOTE=AiBo]Wow! I feel really stupid! Thanks jw5801 ...

(I also had to go into the BIOS and disable the Fn button toggle to keep the wireless card on)

This is really good news ... I can now dump micro$oft ... was holding on to it for those business trips when I had to catch up on email via hotspots.

Wa-hoo[/QUOTE] No probs! glad to be of help. Don't feel stupid though, it's hard if you don't know where everything is already!

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-08-09
> **Baby Boy said:**
> Haven't tried to do what you said, but I *did* try to find and delete all the instances of ndiswrapper and try the whole thing again and this time it worked! Well, sort of...

The WiFi lamp on my laptop is on, and I can turn it on and off at any time, but when I try to do  I only get 

Plus, when I try to connect with my PocketPC through wireless there is no network found on my PPC (I know that I can't actually sync this way but at least the wireless network should be detected right?).
Help?

If I'm reading you correctly, you're saying both your linux box and your PPC can't detect any wireless networks.  That sounds more like a wireless router issue than an issue with your laptop's wireless.

Or, perhaps you're thinking that your linux laptop will broadcast as an available wireless AP once the wireless is properly configured.  If that's the case, then the answer is no, by default your linux laptop does not broadcast itself as a wireless AP.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-08-09
> **jw5801 said:**
> I had that issue before but I don't really remember how I fixed it. I think I may have rerun "modprobe ndiswrapper" after I switched the wireless card back on  and I think it came back up. Not entirely sure though.
A better solution is to just disable the card at OS level. I know there's an option in the gnome network manager to Enable/Disable Wireless if you right-click on the icon, not too sure about wicd though: I only used it for a couple of days and didn't have much success with it.

You pretty much got it.  Working with the Fn + Wifi key on the laptop is tricky, which is why I advise people to just disable it in the BIOS by setting it to Always on / Hotkey Disabled.  

You can turn the Wifi off from within the OS itself by issuing a sudo rmmod ndiswrapper.

If you want to leave the Fn + Wifi key enabled, you have to pay attention to what you're doing.  If your wifi light is on and you want to turn it off, just hit the key combo.  To turn it back on, however, you'll most likely have to hit the key combo again and THEN execute a sudo modprobe ndiswrapper.

Your mileage may vary.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-13
What is the output of the command? It should say something like:
Run this command repeatedly until there are no longer components listed below:

So if it goes back to a command prompt straight after that then there aren't any more files. It gives the same output every time you run it, just the first few times it will list a couple of files that it's removing, so if it doesn't say:
Removing *blah.file*....
after the message it always prints then you're in the clear.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-13
[quote=sdrawkcab]Here's the output:

Quote:
NOTE: Not all installed files are removed, as different distributions install ndiswrapper files at different places.
Run uninstall as many times as necessary until no "removing" messages appear below.[/quote]
It appears to be successfully uninstalled!
[quote=sdrawkcab]Also, when I installed it, it seems to have crapped all kinds of files into the /home folder. Should I erase all these?[/quote]
Usually the only things that get put into your home folder by programs (without asking you anyway) are settings for the program, and these are almost always stored in hidden folders/files. If you had R151517.EXE in your home folder when you extracted it then all those files will have been extracted from that. Since all you need is the bcmwl5.inf and bcmwl5.sys from the DRIVER folder it's safe to delete everything else, but this won't affect your installation procedure.

EDIT: I just checked my home folder and ndiswrapper doesn't save any settings there, so any random files in there are not related to the ndiswrapper program, so they most likely came from Dell's driver .exe as I mentioned.
[quote=sdrawkcab]Well, the thing that worries me is that I installed it once, and I haven't gotten any sort of message saying that it's removing files.[/quote]
I've only ever noticed it saying that it was removing files on the first make uninstall, so you may have missed it, but the makefile itself specifies that it should be run a few times so we do. And apart from that your initial error indicates that ndiswrapper wasn't installed at all so the makefile probably won't find anything to delete. You didn't try installing ndiswrapper using synaptic did you? If you have then run ```
sudo apt-get remove ndiswrapper-utils
```

What happens when you try running ```
sudo make
sudo make install
``` (from the correct folder of course) now?

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-13
Did you run make? or **sudo** make? As I'm looking back at what I posted I note that I left out the sudo's... oops! The errors you're getting are permissions errors, you needed to run the command as root, you may need to uninstall again (you might actually see some files being deleted this time!) then try it again.
```
sudo make uninstall
sudo make
sudo make install
```
Twice should do it for make uninstall (you may or may not get something deleted on the first pass, so if you still don't on the 2nd pass then you're good, if you get something on both passes then you know what to do!)

Apologies for leaving bits out! (I might reedit my post so noone else gets confused...)

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-08-14
> **JayBachatero said:**
> I'm having a strange issue.  Following this guide my wireless was working fine for a few months.  Yesterday my battery died while using the laptop and my wireless is now gone.  I tried reinstalling ndiwrapper and the driver but nothing.  

You didn't have to reinstall ndiswrapper.  In fact, if you tried to install the driver a second time, then you probably have two instances of the driver installed in ndiswrapper's registry.  Confirm that there are two identical ones using sudo ndiswrapper -l, then remove the second one with sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5.

If your battery dies again, try issuing a sudo modprobe ndiswrapper and that should bring it back to life.

---

### Post by JayBachatero on 2007-08-14
I removed all instances of ndiswrapper and bvmwl5.

I'll give the modprobe a go in a few and see if it comes back to life.

---

### Post by JayBachatero on 2007-08-14
Ok I'm not sure if it was booting to windows that helped but when I went back to Ubuntu the WiFi led turned on then the modprobe finished fixing it.  Hope it doesn't crash again.

I did notice that the signal strength seems to be a bit weaker for some reason.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-08-14
> **JayBachatero said:**
> Ok I'm not sure if it was booting to windows that helped but when I went back to Ubuntu the WiFi led turned on then the modprobe finished fixing it.  Hope it doesn't crash again.

Cool, glad you're back up.  If your system crashes in the future, remember that you don't need to touch the ndiswrapper settings.  The modprobe command should get you up and running.

> I did notice that the signal strength seems to be a bit weaker for some reason.

A lot of people have noticed slightly better signal strength reported in Windows.  From what I've read, it's not actually any better / worse from one O/S to the other.  But Windows tends to report a slightly "optimistic" measure of the strength, whereas Linux gives a more accurate report.  Your throughput should be the same, however.

### Post by dbsoundman on 2007-08-17
After a few tries I finally got this to work, but I wanted to point out to others what my mistake was; it was a very simple, silly mistake. The driver you want to install at one point is entitled bcmwl5.inf, NOT bcmw15.inf. That's (in caps for clarity) BCMWL5.INF and not BCMW15.INF.

-Dan

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-17
It sure is! If you're ever unsure about a filename, hit tab to autocomplete the path, that way you're guaranteed the file actually exists!

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-20
Howdy Paperdiesel!
Just letting you know that I just checked the NDISwrapper website and version 1.47 is out now (well has been for a while). I'm about to install it and see how everything goes.

EDIT: All installed and everything works great! Although it worked pretty well before, I had been having some random inexplicable crashes with my 64-bit driver (my connection would drop out and ndiswrapper would seem to decide that it needed WPA encryption, so it would try to run that when there was none and crash, had to run rmmod and modprobe again, no biggie, but annoying nonetheless), which this version says it should fix. So we'll see what happens. Thanks again for the great how-to!

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-08-21
> **jw5801 said:**
> Howdy Paperdiesel!
Just letting you know that I just checked the NDISwrapper website and version 1.47 is out now (well has been for a while). I'm about to install it and see how everything goes.

EDIT: All installed and everything works great! Although it worked pretty well before, I had been having some random inexplicable crashes with my 64-bit driver (my connection would drop out and ndiswrapper would seem to decide that it needed WPA encryption, so it would try to run that when there was none and crash, had to run rmmod and modprobe again, no biggie, but annoying nonetheless), which this version says it should fix. So we'll see what happens. Thanks again for the great how-to!

Cool, thanks for the heads up.  I updated the how-to and added some emphasis at the beginning to the importance of being in the proper directory.  I hope that this will alleviate a lot of people's issues when installing.

### Post by macruadhi on 2007-08-23
I just tried to get my wireless working, how many times on average does one have to repeat "STEP 3: COMPILE PROGRAM

Now we'll complile the Ndiswrapper program. In a terminal, go to the directory where you extracted ndiswrapper and execute the following:

Code:

sudo make uninstall

IMPORTANT: Do the above command multiple times. You can stop when you get the message that says something about no files or directories found."


I ran it in excess of 100 times and still got"eric@eric-laptop:~/ndiswrapper$ sudo make uninstall
NOTE: Not all installed files are removed, as different distributions install ndiswrapper files at different places.
Run uninstall as many times as necessary until no "removing" messages appear below."

AND

Then I ran the last bit of code and got "eric@eric-laptop:~/DRIVER$ sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
bash: /etc/modules: Permission denied"



In case you need it I am running a dell E1505, 512 mb ram, Unbuntu 6.06.

---

### Post by bmartin on 2007-08-23
Instead of **sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules**, try **echo ndiswrapper | sudo tee -a /etc/modules**

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-23
> Then I ran the last bit of code and got "eric@eric-laptop:~/DRIVER$ sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
bash: /etc/modules: Permission denied"

As above or else use ```
sudo -s
echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
exit
```

For the first question, it tells you that you should run it until you don't see a message about files being removed below the message it always prints out. Since there aren't any messages there, you're fine. I run it until I don't get any messages like:
```
NOTE: Not all installed files are removed, as different distributions install ndiswrapper files at different places.
Run uninstall as many times as necessary until no "removing" messages appear below."
Removing /etc/ndiswraper
Removing blah blah etc
```
Then I'll run it once or twice after that just to make sure. If you didn't have ndiswrapper installed in the first place then you won't ever get any of those messages, so just run it a couple of times to triple check.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-08-25
In the manual configuration pop-up window just tick the "roaming mode enabled" option to go back to scanning the network.
I've never had an issue with the gnome network manager but if it's not working for you then you might want to try "wicd" or one of the other ones. It's in the repos, so it's nice and easy to get.

### Post by dennis_matutina on 2007-08-28
I don't know if this will help you I have an Acer 4710Z with the same WLAN card.  I installed Feisty Fawn on it. 

I also used this as reference: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/1390](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/1390). However, I find this thread to be more detailed and complete.

I originally had trouble with uname -r myself, forgetting to check if I typed a *backtick.* I typed instead an *aposthrope* which caused the error. So please check for typos:)

I also had several typo errors, resulting to multiple and incorrect drivers installed by ndiswrapper. To check if you have installed the correct driver, do **ndiswrapper -l**.

If you see that indeed you made an error, remove the offending entries by entering **ndiswrapper -r [wrong driver]**.

After that, the worst part is over. I did not need to reboot but went straight to *iwlist scanning*. 

Thank you for this guide..[http://ubuntuforums.org/images/smilies/guitar.gif](http://ubuntuforums.org/images/smilies/guitar.gif)

should get you there.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-09-01
> **cryinlion85 said:**
> hey everyone, just updated my feisty 7.0.4 kernel revision 16, now wireless doesn't work.  i have an e1505 and am using ndiswrapper v1.47 which didn't seem like it was being updated.  (there were a lot of updates i installed at once, like 23 of them i think)  i'm not sure what could have changed, anyone else had a problem lately?  i can see networks but not connect, y'all know what i'm talkin about.  i don't know how to check what just got updated or i'd list it all for you, i know it updated compiz-fusion and wine but besides that i wasn't really watching...

props for any help you got.

One weakness of this method is that it breaks when you update your kernel.  It's an easy fix, though: just uninstall the driver from ndiswrapper, recompile ndiswrapper, then reinstall the driver.  It should go something like this:

* reboot after new kernel is installed *
```

sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
cd ndiswrapper-1.47
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make
sudo make install
cd ~/DELL-DRIVER-DIRECTORY
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo ndiswrapper -m
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

You may have to adjust the above code to match your specific directories / versions, etc.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-02
It can't remove ndiswrapper from the modules list because it failed to add it in the first place. This has nothing to do with the driver, it's an issue with the kernel and the kernel module ndiswrapper. Did you update your kernel headers? That would be my first suggestion.
```
sudo apt-get install linux-headers-`uname -r`
```

---

```

### Post by st33med on 2007-09-02
How do I upgrade my wireless driver?

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-02
> **Number_6 said:**
> Yup, my kernel headers are up to date. 

```
sudo apt-get install linux-headers-`uname -r`
Password:
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree
Reading state information... Done
linux-headers-2.6.20-16-generic is already the newest version.

```
Ok, did you try the reinstall? Or did you stop when one of the first steps failed? There may have been a mishap during the make process (that's when it recompiles the kernel module that you are failing to insert). Otherwise paperdiesel might have more of an idea.

> **st33med said:**
> How do I upgrade my wireless driver?
Upgrade? From what to what? And the same way as you installed it:
```
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -e *current_driver*
cd /*new-driver-directory*
sudo ndiswrapper -i *new_driver*
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```
Or do you mean upgrading your ndiswrapper version? In which case:
```
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
cd /*new-ndiswrapper-source-directory*
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make
sudo make install
cd /*dell-driver-directory*
#make sure that "ls | grep bcmwl5" produces:
#  bcmwl5.inf
#  bcmwl5.sys
#before you run the next step.
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```
and you should be good. Note that anything I've put in italics you'll need to change to the relevant directory.

Produces:
```
05:00.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Card (rev 01)

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-03
> **dvader said:**
> Thank you  jw5801... 

will read the How To over and move the files to my laptop

One thing I noticed is no mention of wep security 

Dvader

This HowTo is for getting your driver up and running so that Linux can recognise your wireless card. WEP and WPA encryptions are both supported by the driver and by gnome-network-manager. The network manager will ask you for any key it needs whilst it's connecting to the network.

> **train2335 said:**
> K I followed this guide and FINALLY got my wireless working. So I decided I should do some updates, after all my updates were done I restarted and now my wireless doesn't work again.. Should I just redo this guide or what?

What were you updating? I just had a kernel header update come through the other day, so if that's what you've updated then yeah you will need to rerun the install process:
```
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
cd /*ndiswrapper-source-directory*
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make
sudo make install
cd /*dell-driver-directory*
#make sure that "ls | grep bcmwl5" produces:
#  bcmwl5.inf
#  bcmwl5.sys
#before you run the next step.
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

I'm not entirely sure whether you need to remove the driver and put it back in again, but you'll definitely need to recompile ndiswrapper. I'd uninstall it all and reinstall it anyway just to be on the safe side, it only takes a couple of seconds.

### Post by Snipersnest on 2007-09-04
Sounds like its not auto loading.. 
 
try 
 
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
 
If it comes up again you just need to get it to auto load on boot. I'd tell you how but I don't remember! I'm sure somebody will reply with the answer though.

### Post by bmartin on 2007-09-04
> **Snipersnest said:**
> If it comes up again you just need to get it to auto load on boot. I'd tell you how but I don't remember! I'm sure somebody will reply with the answer though.
To make it load up automatically, add the module to /etc/modules.
```
echo "ndiswrapper" | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
```
And make sure that it's not in /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist. You can use **sudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist** to modify the contents of the file. If **blacklist ndiswrapper** shows up anywhere, remove that line.

---

### Post by bmartin on 2007-09-04
At this point, there's no real reason to go out and buy a compatible device. Your device is detected and it looks like it will work. Try downloading [WICD]("http://blakecmartin.googlepages.com/wicd.html") or [wifi-radar]("http://blakecmartin.googlepages.com/wifi-radar.html") and see if one of them works. WICD comes highly recommended. If you enter an ASCII key into WICD, put "double quotes" around it. You might have to do that for wifi-radar, too.

To load either program after installation, use either **wicd** or **sudo wifi-radar**. WICD also has a tray icon; if you used the instructions I posted above, you should be able to run the tray icon using **wicd-tray**.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-09-04
> **lowracer said:**
> I've tried adding a new startup program but it won't stick.  When I quit the sessions program, and then come back in, the WICD entry I just created is gone.

I can think of three ways to do this:

!) Use the session program to add it (as suggested before).  Make sure the entry is using a valid path, hit okay, then sign out and sign back in.

2) Fire up the WICD tray program and then sign out and sign back in.  If I remember correctly, gnome asks if you want to save your session before you logout.  Choose yes.

3) Fire up the command line and type (without the quotes) "sudo gedit /etc/rc.d/rc.local".  At the bottom of the file, add the command to start your program (wicd-tray?).  Then save the file, exit, sign off, and reboot.

Ok, I just ad a squiz around my home directory! You have should have a script at "~/.gnome2/session" so take a look at that one after you try adding the startup command the normal way. If it mentions it in this file then you're set, otherwise check to see if there's still an "nm-applet" command in there, and if there is replace nm-applet with wicd-tray. I think that should work.

I looked into that file and it appears that it is maintained dynamically somehow.  In any case I couldn't grok the syntax, so went a googling...  Came up with this, which worked:

[http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/showthread.php?t=551683&page=2](http://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/showthread.php?t=551683&page=2)

The problem is that the ~/.config/autostart directory is owned by root.  I had to change the ownership of it so I could write to it.   Once that was done, the tray is now working.  

Now all I have to do is get the WPA to work.  My wireless is running but it's wide open.  Gotta lock that down ASAP.  

Thanks...
-Mark

### Post by bmartin on 2007-09-05
You don't appear to have your kernel header files installed. Try **sudo apt-get install linux-headers-$(uname -r)**. That should install the necessary files.

### Post by bmartin on 2007-09-05
Remove the invalid driver
```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
```
Then make sure the INF and SYS files are in the current directory
```
ls *.inf *.sys
```
Then install the driver again
```
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
```

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-08
> **paperdiesel said:**
> Oops.  I meant to link him to the first page.  My sarcasm was foiled by my inability to link correctly!  D'oh.
Hehe. :)
> Sundial.  Give jw's suggestions a shot and let us know if there's some part of the instructions that aren't working for you.
My suggestion is to give this how-to another shot, chances are it didn't work last time because of a typo somewhere. I asked for the output of "ndiswrapper -l" so that I could see whether you'd installed the driver or whether you'd been in the wrong directory when you tried to, ndiswrapper won't return an error if it can't find the file you're telling it to use, it just won't work later! However if this is the case then "ndiswrapper -l" will list it as an "invalid driver" in which case we'll know you simply made a typographical error, or were in the wrong directory when you tried to install the driver. If you use tab to autocomplete the bcmwl5.inf part of the "ndiswrapper -i" command then you'll know for sure that you're in the right directory, the command ```
ls
``` is also useful.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-08
> **arunkottol said:**
> Hi This How-to worked fine in the sense that it went as predicted. But now the Wireless network option does not come up on my network configuration interface. It only show wired network and Modem. However my wifi light is on.

So I dont know what I can do. Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks.

Arun

So if you right click on the little tray icon and open up manual configuration it doesn't show the wireless option there? That's a touch odd... try:
```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure gnome-network-manager
```
Then you might need to reboot. If that doesn't get you working then try wicd or one of the other graphical wireless interfaces around.

### Post by yellow99 on 2007-09-09
> **paperdiesel said:**
> This applies to **everyone** who wants to try network-manager or wifi-radar to connect to networks:

To use network-manager or wifi-radar, you need to **comment-out** all of the lines in /etc/network/interfaces that correspond to your network devices.  For example, your /etc/network/interfaces file should look something like this:

```
auto lo
iface lo inet loopback

#iface eth0 inet dhcp

#iface eth1 inet dhcp
#wireless-essid airseas

#auto eth2
#iface eth2 inet dhcp

#auto ath0
#iface ath0 inet dhcp

#auto wlan0
#iface wlan0 inet dhcp
```

Note that the # character comments-out the line.  Once you've edited and saved your changes, you need to reboot.

After trying thousand of different things, within the thread and with the help of my friend Google, this post that I quoted (and the HOWTO of course!) made me jump and and dance around the house :smile:. I finally got a wireless connection!

Well, I think I now can say goodbye to Windows.

Thanks paperdiesel!!

---

### Post by dividebyzero on 2007-09-12
Sundial:

I have had the same problem you were seeing. Here is what I did to get it to work. I have a Dell inspiron 6400 with Dell wireless 1390 WLAN mini-card (bcm4311 chipset). Try the following:

1) Reboot then do "Fn+F2" a few times to see if your "WiFi" light turns on. The F2 key has a wireless tower icon on it. This key combination switches between your Bluetooth and WiFi. 

If 1) worked, Then try clicking on the network icon in your system tray (upper right hand corner) and see if it detects any wireless network. If it does then you are all set. Don't bother with step 2). Otherwise try 2)

2) Make sure you still have ndiswrapper 1.47 (latest) installed
sudo modprobe -r ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5

Go to your directory where R151517.EXE is extracted, and use vi to remove the "^M" at the end of each line. 

sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5
modprobe ndiswrapper

Do the wireless hot key toggle and pray the light will come on.




> **sundial said:**
> I tried the code and here is the result

pcr@pcr-laptop-2:~$ ndiswrapper -v
utils version: '1.9', utils version needed by module: '1.9'
module details:
filename:       /lib/modules/2.6.20-16-generic/misc/ndiswrapper.ko
version:        1.47
vermagic:       2.6.20-16-generic SMP mod_unload 586 
pcr@pcr-laptop-2:~$

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-12
Sundial,
Check the bios to see if there is anything there about disabling the wireless card. Because you are reporting that the driver is correctly installed, it can see the harware, but the network applications in Linux are reporting that the device does not exist. This implies that the device is turned off by either a hot-key somewhere, or in the bios. So have a look around in the bios and see if you can see any buttons that you don't know what do on your laptop anywhere!

### Post by dividebyzero on 2007-09-12
Hey Sundial, I just realized that since you have a Compaq 552US, the driver for your card _might_ be different from those for the Dell. Try removing your dell driver "R151517.exe" and try the HP driver. Essentially following steps outlined in this link. (Uses Compaq F572US)
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty_No-Fluff#head-74072bb5f4d3b4b7b8c8588a990a145674d06e31](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty_No-Fluff#head-74072bb5f4d3b4b7b8c8588a990a145674d06e31)

So first remove your current driver:

sudo modprobe -r ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5

then download the HP driver:

sudo apt-get install cabextract
wget [ftp://ftp.hp.com/pub/softpaq/sp34001-34500/sp34152.exe](ftp://ftp.hp.com/pub/softpaq/sp34001-34500/sp34152.exe)
cabextract sp34152.exe

Then install that one:

sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
ndiswrapper -l
sudo depmod -a
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -m

---

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty_No-Fluff#head-74072bb5f4d3b4b7b8c8588a990a145674d06e31](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty_No-Fluff#head-74072bb5f4d3b4b7b8c8588a990a145674d06e31)

### Post by dividebyzero on 2007-09-12
So if I understand it correctly, when you run "sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5" it complains that the directory doesn't exist? If so try remove it manually by running "sudo rm -rf /etc/ndiswrapper/*" 

Then run "sudo ndiswrapper -l" and make sure nothing comes up. Then reboot to be sure. After that try installing the HP driver again.

---

### Post by sundial on 2007-09-12
> **dividebyzero said:**
> So if I understand it correctly, when you run "sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5" it complains that the directory doesn't exist? If so try remove it manually by running "sudo rm -rf /etc/ndiswrapper/*"

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-09-13
> **dividebyzero said:**
> So if I understand it correctly, when you run "sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5" it complains that the directory doesn't exist? If so try remove it manually by running "sudo rm -rf /etc/ndiswrapper/*" 

Then run "sudo ndiswrapper -l" and make sure nothing comes up. Then reboot to be sure. After that try installing the HP driver again.

Be **EXTREMELY** careful when running this command.  Any time you do a rm -rf against a directory, you're telling the system "recursively delete every file and directory under this directory and don't prompt me to confirm."  When you combine that with sudo (which gives you the right to delete anything on the system), you have a recipe for zero tolerance for mistakes.  

Again, be absolutely sure you type that command correctly.  My advice is to leave the sudo rm -rf command alone and simply do a sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5 (or whatever your driver is called).

---

### Post by teledyn on 2007-09-13
Thanks for posting this guide, it is indeed very useful; the only edit I would suggest is where you say to change to the "DRIVER" directory, saying "./DRIVER" would be more clear; the first form could be construed as a meta-token substituting for the previously unspecified token -- I first interpreted that as meaning to change to the dir where the exe had been unpacked, and had a tenth of a second of panic when there was no R151517.EXE (but then saw the DRIVER dir and was enlightened)  Not a big deal.

Another maybe-edit, especially for those new to Ubuntu, after doing the iwlist scan, I could see the base station perfectly, and in the System/Network utility I could see the DNS set and the connection listed, but there was still no connection and nothing listed; another brief moment of panic until I realized that (a) for my particular wireless hub I had to switch off Roaming mode, and (b) the little box next to the connection name is not a tree-view expand button, it is an enable/disable switch. 

No matter, all is well now, I'm [off the cat completely]("http://monster-island.org/tinashumor/humor/eincat.html")!

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-18
Is there any partitular reason you've installed bcmwl6 in place of bcmwl5? cd to the DRIVER directory you extracted and run:
```
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl6
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

### Post by yeahboy on 2007-09-20
> **yeahboy said:**

im sorry to trouble you guys again..

 now i can see the wireless networks but i cant connect to them.Yesterday i connected to the un secure network @ college but after coming  home i could not connect to the secure wireless network.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-09-21
> **yeahboy said:**
> im sorry to trouble you guys again..

 now i can see the wireless networks but i cant connect to them.Yesterday i connected to the un secure network @ college but after coming  home i could not connect to the secure wireless network.

Today i could not connect to the unsecure network @ college too.
its just showing connecting to 'xxx' network and then stopping there.But its not connecting.

i have to use the wire everytime which is uncomfortable...please suggest.

thanks in advance..

The issue is most likely related to the way that you have your wireless security settings configured on your card.  There are some very helpful threads in this forum that help users with issues similar to yours.  This thread, however, primarily deals with getting the wireless NICs recognized by linux.  Since you can scan for networks and have successfully connected, it most likely means your issue is beyond the scope of this how-to.

That said, my advice is to turn off all encryption (both on your laptop and on the router), reboot the laptop, and try to connect again.  If that works, then turn on the encryption and try again.  If you can't connect after turning on the encryption, then it's definitely a security settings issue.  Good luck!

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-26
> **jardo said:**
> ok friends,i just tried those...
and work very good...
finally i have native linux drivers! if u wish i can post some instructions :)

the only thing i don't understand is why everytime i restart pc,my card isn't seen...but if i write:
sudo modprobe bcm43xx

Howdy, you haven't told Linux that it should load the module on startup ```
sudo -s
echo bcm43xx >> /etc/modules
exit
```will fix that

This also is not the place to post instructions or extended discussions about the native drivers, there are many other threads devoted to this if you wanted to search for them.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-26
Alrighty, out of curiosity I'm giving the native driver a shot. This one has the exact same issues as the one in the current kernel used by Ubuntu. It's buggy, and more importantly, it only supports 802.11b, not 802.11g as ndiswrapper can. So if you're using it in a home network, data transfer will be significantly slower, for the internet it's highly unlikely that the wireless connection will be the bottleneck so it probably won't make a noticeable difference. Still, I'll stick with ndiswrapper, it's about as difficult to set up and supports more than the native drivers in their current state!

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-27
Not from scratch, tell the kernel it should ignore the ndiswrapper module```
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
```remove the bad driver```
sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl6
```change directories to where the original driver is and reinstall it:```
cd *insert_directory_here*
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
```and finally tell the kernel that it should use the ndiswrapper module again```
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-09-27
> **jardo said:**
> thanks for helping ! :D i didn't know that it supports only b  :(
another question...
but these drivers see network 802.11g? i mean they see that network but i would get my speed down or i can't see 802.11g?

All networks that support 802.11g also support 802.11b (as far as I'm aware anyway) you'll just connect at a maximum theoretical speed of 11Mb/s rather than 54Mb/s. So yes you'll still be able to see the networks and connect to them, just not to their full potential. Although 802.11b is more stable than g in alot of cases anyway.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-09-29
> **Caloue said:**
> Unfortunatly I could not complete the process. KUbuntu (*.15-generic) freezed when I tried the modprob ndiswrapper command line. :confused:
I tried to reboot in standard mode, but freezed again before login. 
I tried a debug mode boot, and found this error message: 
```
BUG: soft lockup detected on CPU#0!
```
then freezd again

Don't give up!  I've seen this happen before.  Try editing your grub configuration (I believe it's the /boot/grub/menu.lst file) and add these following options to your boot parameters:

```

noapic nolapic irqpoll

```

Then reboot and try again.  Make sure you add those options correctly, though.  It might help to search for a how-to on editing the grub conf first.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-04
It'll never actually, say "no files found" the message give by ndiswrapper is a bit misleading in that sense. It will tell you if there are any files to be deleted, but it won't tell you if there aren't. So if you're seeing the "Run this command a few times..." output and nothing else then it hasn't found anything to delete so you're good to go!

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-05
Everything is working perfectly as far as I can tell jtekUSA. If you're not broadcasting you ESSID then a scan will never turn it up! You'll have to manually configure it in that case. If you can't manage to connect, I'd try with an unsecured network first, as paperdiesel suggests, then turn all the security measures back on one by one to make sure they all work.

### Post by willmurray on 2007-10-08
"Reboot your machine. BEFORE it starts loading linux, go in to your BIOS setup. Scroll down to the entry that says Wireless. You'll see a section that says something about the Wifi / bluetooth hotkey. Try setting that so that the wifi is always on, and the hotkey is disabled.

I'm wondering if you're set up correctly, but the wifi hotkey is defaulting to off. Switch that BIOS setting and report back."

This is finally what got it working for me.  It was a simple matter of making the hotkey control the bluetooth only.  Thanks for the info and the fix!

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-11
Ok, the invalid driver message means you tried to install the driver from a folder that did not contain the necessary files. You need to do the following:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
cd */extracted/directory/DRIVER/* #change this to the relevant directory
```
now from this directory, if you run the following command you should see the corresponding output:```
blah@blah:/extracted/directory/DRIVER$ ls | grep bcmwl5
bcmwl564.sys
bcmwl5.inf
bcmwl5.sys
```You can safely ignore the first result, but if the other 2 are not returned then you are in the wrong folder! Use cd to change to the correct directory. Once you are in the correct directory run ```
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
```then check the output from ```
ndiswrapper -l
```If it now shows the correct result then continue with the how-to and add the module to the kernel ```
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

---

root@conference-laptop:~/R151517/DRIVER# ls | grep bcmwl5

bcmwl5.inf
bcmwl5.sys
root@conference-laptop:~/R151517/DRIVER# ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
installing bcmwl5 ...
root@conference-laptop:~/R151517/DRIVER# ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : driver installed
        device (14E4:4311) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)
root@conference-laptop:~/R151517/DRIVER#  ndiswrapper -m
adding "alias wlan0 ndiswrapper" to /etc/modprobe.d/ndiswrapper ...
root@conference-laptop:~/R151517/DRIVER# modprobe ndiswrapper
FATAL: Could not open '/lib/modules/2.6.20-15-generic/kernel/ubuntu/misc/ndiswrapper/ndiswrapper.ko': No such file or directory

What should I do now

Thanks 
Mandeep Kaur

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-11
Have you installed ndiswrapper? Because it's saying the module doesn't exist, which means you either didn't run, or something screwed up when you ran, "make" and "make install." So you're going to need to start over:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
cd */ndiswrapper/install/directory* #replace with appropriate directory
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall #three oughta do it
```
then reinstall from the same directory```
sudo make
sudo make install
cd */driver/directory*
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

If I forgot to disable the wifi before shutting down the system -----> system hanged........

Any ideas?
Francesc.
---------------------------
laptop Hp 530 with broadcom 1390
ndiswrapper 1.48
used the driver from this post.

Any time someone tells me that their system is hanging and it has something to do with wireless, I suggest this:

As root, edit your grub conf file and add these options to your boot parameter: noapic nolapic irqpoll.  It would go something like this:

```

cd /boot/grub
sudo cp menu.lst menu.lst.backup
sudo gedit menu.lst

```

(If you're using KDE, use kate instead of gedit)

In that menu.list file, add...
```

noapic nolapic irqpoll

```

...to the **end** of the line that begins with **# defoptions=quiet splash**.  So that line should look like # defoptions=quiet splash noapic nolapic irqpoll

**DO NOT** uncomment that line.  Leave it exactly as is, but add those three words to the end.

Once you've added those lines, save the file and quit.  When you're back to the command line, execute this:

```

sudo update-grub

```

Then reboot. Give that a shot and report back.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-10-12
> **Stretsh said:**
> SUCESS!!!

Wireless (finally) works!

Dell E1705
Ubuntu 7.04
ndiswrapper 1.48
Dell 151517.EXE drivers

Thanks a million paperdiesel!

Cool, glad it works.  Now that you're up and running, you probably just want to leave it alone.  BUT.. for what it's worth, if I were you I'd uninstall ndiswrapper 1.48 and put 1.47 back on there.  Once 1.47 is back on, remove those three words from the menu.lst file, re-run sudo update-grub, and reboot.

The reason I'd do this is because noapic and nolapic deal with your power management.  So adding those to your boot options adds the possibility that your suspend / hibernate / cpu throttling could be affected.  If ndiswrapper 1.47 works fine for you, then there's no reason to go 1.48 -- particularly if going to 1.48 means you have to add switches to your bootup options.

It's starting to look like ndiswrapper 1.48 has some bugs, which is why I wait a bit before I update the guide with a newer version.  I'm a huge fan of the ndiswrapper dev team, but sometimes some bugs make it into their public releases and it causes headaches for those of us on the ground 8-)

Just my $0.02.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-17
Please don't type all in caps. It's the equivalent of yelling at the top of your lungs.

Did you replace *YOUR-DRIVER-DRIECTORY* with the actual directory you have the driver in? If you didn't then you haven't actually installed anything, which is why it's not saying that your hardware has been detected.
You'll need to, uninstall the pretend driver you installed:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
```use the cd command to change to the correct directory that contains bcmwl5.inf. If you saved and extracted R151517.exe in your home directory it will be as follows:```
cd ~/DRIVER/
```It might then be an idea to use```
ls
```to verify that you are in the correct folder, ie the folder that contains bcmwl5.inf. After you're there you will then need to install the correct driver```
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
```then pick up with the how-to.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-19
Just letting anyone interested know, the new Gutsy kernel will try to use bcm43xx by default. So you probably won't be able to get an internet connection from the LiveCD (at least I couldn't), since it requires a restart after blacklisting it to stop it from working. But once installed removing the bcm43xx module and blacklisting it, then compiling and installing ndiswrapper and the driver as suggested in this tutorial works perfectly.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-21
This is almost certainly an issue with the gutsy suspend/hibernate script as opposed to an issue with this driver. You can try a few things, restart the hadware abstraction layer:```
sudo /etc/init.d/hal restart
```Restart networking:```
sudo /etc/init.d/networking restart
```If you think it's just the network manager applett that's frozen then you can try restarting that:```
sudo killall nm-applet
nm-applet &
```You can also use```
lsmod
```to check that the ndiswrapper module is still loaded after the restart. Hibernate and suspend are very tricky things to code for, and to debug however. Have a search around the launchpad bugs and see if there's anything about your particular hardware and hibernate/suspend.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-27
> **makemoneyy said:**
> how do i install WPA to work with dell driver? does anyone know?

You don't need to "install" WPA. It's an encryption protocol, which is supported by the driver and by the default Gnome network manager. When you try to connect to the network you should get a pop-up box asking for the passphrase for the network, if that doesn't happen then if you click on the network-manager icon and open up the manual configuration dialog you can configure it there.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-27
> **frojnd said:**
> I can connect to an open network, but  Ican't connect to a network that require a hex key and static IP. I set everything but I can't connect. If u can helo me please let me know what output data you need.

What happens when you try to connect? Does it ask you for key to the network? If it doesn't get that far, then open up the manual configuration and set everything as it needs to be in there and see if it helps. Otherwise try a couple of different network managers (wicd, wifi-radar, etc), some people seem to have success with one network manager where others can only get things running with another.

What do you mean by a network that "requires a static IP." If you want your machine to have a static IP, then you can either find a setting in your router to assign that IP to your MAC address only. Or else you should be able to tell Ubuntu to request a specific IP in the manual configuration section of the network manager.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-27
Absolutely! You'll just need to get all the files you'll need first. You'll need build-essential and any of it's dependancies which you should be able to find by searching [here](http://packages.ubuntu.com/). The package page should tell you what dependancies it has so just download and save all the .deb files into one folder, then from your Ubuntu install run:```
cd */where/ever/you/put/them*
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
```You'll also need to get the ndiswrapper tarball from the link in the first post. That should be about all you'll need to download while you're connected (other than a copy of the instructions :P), then you'll need to boot up Ubuntu and follow the rest of the guide!

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-28
Ok well from what I can see you should be using the same driver as for the 1390 MPCI. So go get R151517.EXE from the link in the OP. Then, assuming all of these are in the root directory of your USB drive, and that there's nothing else there, do the following:
```
cd /media/DVDDUMP/ #be aware that linux is case sensitive, so you might have to play around a bit to find the right directory
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
sudo dpkg -r ndiswrapper-utils
#those 3 commands should get cranky at you and tell you there's nothing to remove, this is a good thing.
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
#should install build-essential and it's dependencies, if you get errors about missing dependencies, make a note of the package name, because you'll need to go find it.
echo "blacklist bcm43xx" | sudo tee -a /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
#now reboot:
sudo shutdown -r now

#when you come back online open a fresh terminal and:
cd /media/DVDDUMP
tar -xzvf ndiswrapper-1.48.tar.gz
cd ndiswrapper-1.48
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
#yes you really should run the command 3 times
sudo make
sudo make install
cd ..
unzip -a R151517.EXE -d R151517/
cd R151517/DRIVER/
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo ndiswrapper -l #should give you a message saying that the driver is installed **and the hardware is present**, if it doesn't don't continue something went wrong.
sudo ndiswrapper -m
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper #at this point if you have a wifi light, it should come on.
echo ndiswrapper | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
```

If at any point anything fails, stop there, copy and paste ALL the terminal output into a file somewhere so that you can post it here (preferably enclosed in code tags as I have above, see my "reason for editing" below for how to do this).
Hope that works!

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-28
> **imgod22222 said:**
> the terminal commands for people doing this offline is what i wanted.

But lucky me managed to borrow some extra ethernet cables from the neighbors so now i'm connected. Since i have an Inspiron 600m, i have a different driver, and when i try 'unzip -a R115321.exe' it says something like 'cannot find zip archive in r115321.exe, and can't do anything about it. period.'

Heh. I went to all that effort, not even noticing you'd made another post, sorry! The error you're getting is because R115321.EXE is completely different to r115321.exe (linux is case sensitive). Try with this driver and see what happens, but I'm reasonably sure it would work with the driver in the OP (and possibly not with a newer one). ndiswrapper is weird like that.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-29
1. Yes. There is a native driver for this card, but it's buggy and doesn't work as well as ndiswrapper. So once you blacklist bcm43xx (the native module that let's it see your card to start off with) and reboot it shouldn't see your card anymore.

2. No it won't say anything. All that command does is append a line to the end of the /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist. That file is the list of kernel modules that shouldn't be loaded. If it doesn't say anything it means it worked.

What were you chmodding?! You shouldn't need to chmod anything. chmod is used to change the permissions on a file, and the way you've used it above will remove execute permissions from whatever you run it on. So I'm tipping you've probably told Linux that it can't execute something that it really needs to be able to, without being a superuser, which it wasn't. Hence the crash and consequent failure to boot.

Have you had any luck this time around?

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-29
> **imgod22222 said:**
> Well according to nachotronics (not sure if he's highly regarded in this forum)

...

And right now i'm looking for where i read to put chmod... its freaky... i don't see it anymore O.o

nachotronics is correct about the driver (I think I said that earlier didn't I?). No idea where you got chmod from! It's a useful command but one that should always be used very carefully if you give it root permissions (sudo).

And to explain about the two commands: They both do exactly the same thing, just in slightly different ways.
The command "echo" does exactly what it's name implies, it "echoes"  (prints) whatever you put after it in the commandline.
If I were to put any command and then "> *filename*", whatever would have been printed on the commandline is instead "piped" into  the file "*filename*". There are a few variations on this "piping" thing:
">" overwrites the file if it already exists or creates a new file.
">>" appends the output to the end of an existing file, or creates a new file.
The final variation is the "|" which pipes the output of the command before it, into the input of the next command.
Therefore ```
echo "blacklist bcm43xx" >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```takes the output of *echo "blacklist bcm43xx"* (blacklist bcm43xx) and appends it to the end of the file "/etc/modprobe.d/blacklist". The problem with this command is that root permissions are required to write to "/etc/modprobe.d/blacklist" and simply prepending sudo at the start of the command only gives root permissions to the echo command. The alternative is to either run the entire command as root ("sudo -s" turns the terminal into a root shell, you'll notice the command prompt becomes "root@machinename:dir$"), or to use the command I gave ```
echo "blacklist bcm43xx" | sudo tee -a /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```which takes the output of the echo command and pipes it to the input of the tee command. tee is a command used to append or prepend files, the -a option tells it that it should append the input given to it to the filename listed after. We can also use sudo to give tee root permissions so we can write to the file that we otherwise couldn't. The other difference is that tee will also output what it's given to std out (the commandline) as well as writing it to the file. Hopefully that all makes sense! If you want to take a look at what you've done you can use ```
gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```to open up the text editor to edit the file we've been using fancier methods to edit. gksudo is the same as sudo, but should be used for any graphical application (anything that opens a new window) that needs root permissions.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-29
> **giusepesm said:**
> Hello, thank you very much for this how-to... it has worked fine on my pc... didn't worked the first time, so I tried a second time and it has worked. However, there's one annoying thing and I don't if this is just with me, but my wireless actually takes about 3 minutes to turn on after I log in... what could it be? I have no idea... 

One curious thing is that when the wireless actually turns on, the terminal pops up.... also, the little panel that appears right at the middle of the screen that shows up when you log in stays there until the wireless is on... that's really weird... can anyone help me?

I do have the Dell Inspiron E1505/6400 and my wireless is the Broadcom 1390 WLAN. I didn't post any logs because I have no idea what's the problem.... well... thanks to anyone who might be helpful in this... 

I tried and searched if anyone else had this problem in this thread, but I was unsucessful. If this have already been fixed or posted please tell me where because I could not find it.

Thanks again!

Hmm... at what point does the wifi light actually turn on during the bootup process? It should happen well before you reach a login screen. It sounds like something is taking forever to load as you log in and holding everything else up with it. Is there alot of harddrive activity while it does this? If the wifi light is coming on nice and early then this isn't an issue with the driver there's something else at fault (possibly the network manager if it's taking forever to connect to a network and is holding other things up while it attempts), so it might be an idea to start a new thread about it. Post the link here though just in case anyone else encounters the issue.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-29
> **imgod22222 said:**
> 
...

AND FOR THE LOVE OF GOD...
ARGH! Okay, i was doing everything perfectly, all according to the how-to
and then
```
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```
and everything's frozen.
Turns out to be the same damn issue. Need to reinstall ubuntu. AGAIN. :'(

Just noticed the edit. When it crashed did anything alarming happen? Like the caps lock and num locks start flashing at you? This time around DON'T add ndiswrapper to /etc/modules! So don't run the command ```
sudo -s
echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
exit
```If it's the ndiswrapper module that's crashing your kernel then you don't want it to autoload on startup, else you won't be able to boot up.

**If you reinstalled, ignore everything from here on, unless the problem happens again!**

If you haven't already started the reinstall then boot up into the failsafe kernel (an option in grub during boot, you may have to press Esc at some point to see it). This should get you to a scary black terminal where you'll need to type ```
nano /etc/modules
```which will give you a command line text editor which you can use to remove the ndiswrapper line, save with ctrl+o, exit with ctrl+x, then reboot using```
shutdown -r now
```

If you hadn't already added ndiswrapper to that file, then you should just be able to hard reset and everything should be ok. If it's not then the issue isn't being caused by ndiswrapper and adding the module to the kernel, in which case I'd check the integrity of your install cd.

If removing it from /etc/modules, lets you reboot back normally, then I'll get you to check a few things:
Firstly check the following command returns the same output I have, or something similar (if you're running feisty then the 2.6.22-14 will be something different):
```
jw5801@laptop:~$ ndiswrapper -v
utils version: '1.9', utils version needed by module: '1.9'
module details:
filename:       /lib/modules/2.6.22-14-generic/misc/ndiswrapper.ko
version:        1.48
vermagic:       2.6.22-14-generic SMP mod_unload
```
Secondly verify that ```
lsmod | grep bcm43xx
``` returns absolutely nothing. If it returns anything at all run ```
sudo rmmod bcm43xx
``` and check the blacklist file ```
gksudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
```
If it doesn't return anything and "sudo modprobe ndiswrapper" is still returning failing, then try the how-to again using ndiswrapper 1.47 instead. Or just do the following commands:
```
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -r bcmwl5
wget http://internap.dl.sourceforge.net/sourceforge/ndiswrapper/ndiswrapper-1.47.tar.gz
tar -xzvf ndiswrapper-1.47.tar.gz
cd ndiswrapper-1.47
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make
sudo make install
cd */whereever/R151517.EXE/is/* # you'll need to replace that with an actual directory
unzip -a R151517.EXE -d R151517
cd R151517/DRIVER/
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

### Post by jc_madden on 2007-10-30
Hello,  I'm rather new to linux and I'm still learning the terminal commands.  This HOWTO helped me a great deal.  I recently purchased a Dell Inspiron 1420 which came with the same wireless network card.  I did not see this thread until I had already tried other methods for getting it to work.  I followed most of the advice listed in the original thread but I was unsure about trying to compile the source code myself, I tried it and did not get very far.

In the end here's what I did to get it working.  Hopefully it will help other new users.

First when I got to the bottom of the howto I read that I needed to check the dell support page to make sure I had the right driver, turns out the one listed here works fine on my 1420.  So with that knowledge I began.

1.  I uninstalled all the other network programs I had gotten to try and help fix my problem like wi-fi radar, ndiswrapper, etc.  (Despite the warnings that it probably would not work.)
2.  Then I got the build essential updates following the same instructions listed by the OP, including the dell drivers.
3.  Next I used the add/remove option to install the latest GUI version of ndiswrapper.
4.  After that I blacklisted the existing driver (bcm43xx), again using the OP's instructions.
5.  Then I rebooted.
6.  Finally I installed the driver with ndiswrapper using the terminal commands used by the OP.
7.  Lastly I tested my wifi and it worked like a charm.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-30
> **imgod22222 said:**
> well, i followed all the instructions that you gave in the bottom of your post, sorry that i had already reinstalled ubuntu. I actually found out that instead of getting gusty gibbon (7.10), i somehow got Edgy Eft (6.10) O.o So if ubuntu dies again, i'm going to install 7.10.

...

That's my whole terminal after doing everything you said. modprobe didn't return anything, i didn't blacklist those drivers like you said.

I thought that doing sudo -s at the beginning would make everything easier.

Ok. Those debugging things wouldn't have returned anything unless you already had ndiswrapper installed, so that's ok. It appears like everything ran as it should this time you installed. You do need to blacklist the driver still and then after a reboot everything should be working. The command I suggested you leave out wasn't blacklist command (ie. adding bcm43xx to /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist) but omitting adding ndiswrapper to /etc/modules. The first file is a list of modules that shouldn't be loaded. The second is a list of modules that should be loaded on boot, so if the ndiswrapper module was crashing your kernel then you don't want it to load on boot. You haven't added it this time around so I don't think you would have last time. By the way, if modprobe works correctly then it won't output anything, so that's a good thing.

Anyway, blacklist the bcm43xx module, reboot, run modprobe again, and everything should be rockin'! If it is then add ndiswrapper to /etc/modules so you don't have to run modprobe everytime you reboot.

You'll probably wanna upgrade to Gutsy anyway, it is much nicer and newer! So just run the how-to again if you do upgrade. But you know what you're doing now so it's all good.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-30
> **jc_madden said:**
> I'm running 64 bit myself.  What issues are you experiencing?  I have yet to have any problems but I've only been using Linux for a week now.

I haven't experienced any issues since ndiswrapper 1.47 when they fixed some things, but I was seeing random freezes of my network card, removing the ndiswrapper module and adding it again fixed the issue, so I'm not sure if it were a bug with ndiswrapper, or with how it interacted with the earlier kernel, or whether it was my dodgy router dropping out and causing the module to hang while it waited for data or something else entirely. But I haven't seen the issue since 1.47 (that was still with Feisty's kernel) and I'm now using 1.48 and Gutsy and haven't had any issues at all with it. Leave it how you have it, I'll be intrigued to see how it goes! My network suffers from heaps of interference and my router is utterly terrible so these are about the worst working conditions it'll have to cope with. If you ever get the problem just run:
```
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo moprobe ndiswrapper
```and you should reconnect to the network and have everything working again.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-10-31
Haha, yeah that's a start! You shouldn't need to reinstall ndiswrapper after a reboot. I dunno what happened! What does "ndiswrapper -v" return? Also check if you actually have an ndiswrapper.ko in the /lib/modules/2.6.17-12-generic/kernel/drivers/net/ndiswrapper/. Mine is stored in /lib/modules/2.6.22-14-generic/misc/ndiswrapper.ko, which would translate to /lib/modules/2.6.17-12-generic/kernel/drivers/misc/ndiswrapper.ko on your machine. Either way looks like you're going to need to reinstall ndiswrapper, but that's easy enough by now:
```
sudo -s
cd ~/ndiswrapper-1.47/
ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
make uninstall # times a few
make
make install
cd ~/R151517/DRIVER/
ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
modprobe ndiswrapper
exit #or ctrl+d will give the same effect
```
Was your wireless working after the last attempt? At any rate it should be working after this install I hope.

--Good bye, linux. Our time together really pissed me off, but i'm not completely put off. There's alot of good programs out for linux so when I'm living alone and I can have craploads of wires, then using a wired connection will be an option for me. Until then, I'll stick to windows again. Or if ubuntu has ndiswrapper as a default driver (curious to know why they use bcmlxx if its so buggy) and my wireless will work out of the box with it.

Alrighty then. Hopefully we'll see you back again at some stage. As for why Ubuntu uses bcm43xx by default: Ubuntu is based on the Debian distribution, and Debian's license requires that anyone who adapts their distribution must not include any closed source code by default. Therefore the open source bcm43xx is used by default as opposed to ndiswrapper, which is used to adapt closed source windows drivers for Linux. If you had have installed a newer version of Ubuntu (ie. Gutsy as opposed to Edgy) you would have seen that bcm43xx support is actually starting to be functional. With Gutsy's kernel, installing some firmware will get the driver functioning, it's not as good as it's ndiwrapper counterpart, but I think Ubuntu's "Restricted Drivers Manager" will do it all for you. Ah well, good luck with everything!

### Post by bigcat40 on 2007-11-03
I am a new user of Linux. I have always used Windows, but after 3 problems with Windows Vista license issues, I went back to XP (Kind of Funny. Microsoft support didn't even know how to fix the problem). Someone I work with recommended Ubuntu so I decided to give it a shot. 

I ran the Live CD for a little while and loved it. I am definitely a believer. The only thing I was disappointed in was my wireless did not work. I decided to install it anyway and figured someone from these forums would help me with the wireless. 

Well last night I spent about 4 hours trying to get my wireless to work. I tried the tutorial on the first page and I couldn't get the wireless to work. I even installed 7.10 2 times and did the tutorial again each time. It still didn't work. I could browse all the networks in my neighborhood, but couldn't connect to my own router, even without any security. 

I was about to give up, but decided to try one more time tonight. I left my wired connection in and made sure I could get to the internet using the Live CD. I then installed 7.10 again. After the OS installed and the updates ran, I checked my wireless connection and I did not see any wireless networks as I anticipated. 

I did see a little icon in the top right that was called restricted drivers manager (System->Administration->Restricted Drivers Manager.). I clicked it and a dialog box opened up. It allowed me to enable the NVIDIA graphics driver and the Broadcom Wireless firmware (bcm43xx), Once I enabled these two I had to restart my machine. After restart, I still could not connect to or view any wireless network. 

I did another search and found something recommending disabling the hotkey for the wireless in the BIOS. I disabled the hotkey for bluetooth and the wireless and rebooted my machine. As soon as I rebooted my machine I checked the wireless and BEHOLD!! I saw 6 wireless networks including my own. I successfully connected to my home network and could access the internet using my wireless. I then added WPA security to my router and my e1505 again successfully connected to the internet. I am SO HAPPY right now :guitar:

As I stated before I am so new to Linux and Ubuntu, but I figured I would share my experience. I hope this helps someone, and thanks for all the great reading material. (Sorry for such a long post.)

Jeff K

---

00:01.0 ISA bridge: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 0548 (rev a2)
00:01.1 SMBus: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 0542 (rev a2)

00:01.3 Co-processor: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 0543 (rev a2)
00:02.0 USB Controller: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 055e (rev a2)
00:02.1 USB Controller: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 055f (rev a2)
00:04.0 USB Controller: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 055e (rev a2)
00:04.1 USB Controller: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 055f (rev a2)
00:06.0 IDE interface: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 0560 (rev a1)
00:07.0 Audio device: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 055c (rev a1)
00:08.0 PCI bridge: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 0561 (rev a2)
00:09.0 IDE interface: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 0550 (rev a2)
00:0a.0 Ethernet controller: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 054c (rev a2)
00:0c.0 PCI bridge: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 0563 (rev a2)
00:0d.0 PCI bridge: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 0563 (rev a2)
00:12.0 VGA compatible controller: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 0531 (rev a2)
00:18.0 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] HyperTransport Technology Configuration
00:18.1 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] Address Map
00:18.2 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] DRAM Controller
00:18.3 Host bridge: Advanced Micro Devices [AMD] K8 [Athlon64/Opteron] Miscellaneous Control
01:09.0 FireWire (IEEE 1394): Ricoh Co Ltd Unknown device 0832 (rev 05)
01:09.1 Class 0805: Ricoh Co Ltd R5C822 SD/SDIO/MMC/MS/MSPro Host Adapter (rev 22)
01:09.2 System peripheral: Ricoh Co Ltd Unknown device 0843 (rev 12)
01:09.3 System peripheral: Ricoh Co Ltd R5C592 Memory Stick Bus Host Adapter (rev 12)
01:09.4 System peripheral: Ricoh Co Ltd xD-Picture Card Controller (rev 12)
04:00.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-PCI Card (rev 02)
bt ~ # ndiswrapper -i /mnt/sdb1_removable/driver/bcmwl5.inf
installing bcmwl5 ...
bt ~ # ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : driver installed
        device (14E4:4311) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)
```

With Gutsy bcm43xx works enough to actually light up the WiFi LED but not enough to actually see a network let alone connect to one without extra firmware. Since the driver itself is not as versatile as it's ndiswrapper equivalent alot of people choose to use ndiswrapper instead (hence the how-to). That's just for background information.

The LED will be on when you first boot up. If you follow the how-to, paperdiesel advises that you should **blacklist bcm43xx and then reboot**. If you do this the light will not turn back on again when you reboot. If this doesn't happen, something has gone wrong and you need to check /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist and make sure the line "blacklist bmc43xx" is there before you bother doing anything else. Once you have the light off, it will not light up again until you follow the rest of the how-to and run "sudo modprobe ndiswrapper" at which point you tell the kernel to use the ndiswrapper module (enabling the driver you just installed).

> **bigcat40 said:**
> I am a new user of Linux. I have always used Windows, but after 3 problems with Windows Vista license issues, I went back to XP (Kind of Funny. Microsoft support didn't even know how to fix the problem). Someone I work with recommended Ubuntu so I decided to give it a shot. 

I ran the Live CD for a little while and loved it. I am definitely a believer. The only thing I was disappointed in was my wireless did not work. I decided to install it anyway and figured someone from these forums would help me with the wireless. 

Well last night I spent about 4 hours trying to get my wireless to work. I tried the tutorial on the first page and I couldn't get the wireless to work. I even installed 7.10 2 times and did the tutorial again each time. It still didn't work. I could browse all the networks in my neighborhood, but couldn't connect to my own router, even without any security. 

I was about to give up, but decided to try one more time tonight. I left my wired connection in and made sure I could get to the internet using the Live CD. I then installed 7.10 again. After the OS installed and the updates ran, I checked my wireless connection and I did not see any wireless networks as I anticipated. 

I did see a little icon in the top right that was called restricted drivers manager (System->Administration->Restricted Drivers Manager.). I clicked it and a dialog box opened up. It allowed me to enable the NVIDIA graphics driver and the Broadcom Wireless firmware (bcm43xx), Once I enabled these two I had to restart my machine. After restart, I still could not connect to or view any wireless network. 

I did another search and found something recommending disabling the hotkey for the wireless in the BIOS. I disabled the hotkey for bluetooth and the wireless and rebooted my machine. As soon as I rebooted my machine I checked the wireless and BEHOLD!! I saw 6 wireless networks including my own. I successfully connected to my home network and could access the internet using my wireless. I then added WPA security to my router and my e1505 again successfully connected to the internet. I am SO HAPPY right now :guitar:

As I stated before I am so new to Linux and Ubuntu, but I figured I would share my experience. I hope this helps someone, and thanks for all the great reading material. (Sorry for such a long post.)

Jeff K

Haha. Don't worry, I've done the hotkey thing. When I was a new user, I had my cat jump on my keyboard and manage to hit the hotkey and turn off my wireless, after it had already been working for about a week. I wound up reinstalling twice and trying a heap of how-to's before I found this thread with paperdiesel telling people they should check their bios and disable the hotkey and finally worked out that there WAS a hotkey to toggle it, pressed it and everything worked! Felt rather sheepish after that.

So you're currently using bcm43xx? That's the native driver for this series of broadcom chipsets. If you're interested in trying out the ndiswrapper equivalent, then it should work now that you've disabled the bios thing. It has support for 802.11g connections as well as 802.11b, unlike the native driver in it's current state. It does also tend to be slightly more stable. But if bcm43xx is working for you, stick with it! ndiswrapper works best on some machines and bcm43xx can work best on others. Just be aware that the alternative is there, that's the beauty of Linux!

### Post by fooman on 2007-11-04
i hate anything on my desktop so i would never leave it there.

i would make a couple of folders in my home directory.  one called "ndiswrapper" and one called "dell_driver".  place the ndiswrapper file in the folder you created for it and the "R151517.EXE" in the dell_driver folder.   sooo...say my username is fooman,  in a terminal,  i would run the command as in step 3 of this guide (you would of course replace "fooman" with your username):

```
cd /home/fooman/ndiswrapper
```

then the following commands (*be sure you run this "sudo make uninstall" command a few times to make sure you got it all and then continue):

```
sudo make uninstall
```

```
sudo make

```
 
```
sudo make install
```

next to install the driver (step 4),  i would "cd" to the dell_driver directory:

```
cd /home/fooman/dell_driver
```

then extract the driver with:

```
unzip -a R151517.EXE
```

now after the driver is extracted,  it will make another directory inside the dell_driver folder called "DRIVER".  you need to "cd" to that directory like so:

```
cd /home/fooman/dell_driver/DRIVER
```

then:

```

sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo ndiswrapper -l
```

and continue with the rest of step 4 etc...

hope that helps

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-11-04
In the above post, the second directory you need to cd to is NOT ~/dell_driver/driver, it's ~/dell_driver/DRIVER. There is a substantial difference!

EDIT: Fixed now :D

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-11-04
haha. It's "eight, right bracket." If you put code tags around things like that then that doesn't happen.
Having bcml5 there shouldn't bother anything but I'd remove it anyway:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcml5
```
