### Post by gerdt on 2007-11-15
When I installed Gutsy on my laptop I was very glad to see that the restricted drivers handled my WLAN automatically. This meant I didn't need to do it manually using ndiswrapper. This worked fine, until ... I wanted to connect to the WLAN of my university-campus. This required WPA2 Enterprise w/ wpa_supplicant, and that didn't work with the restricted drivers from Ubuntu. So I removed the restricted drivers for my WLAN, and I installed wlan w/ ndiswrapper using this howto.

I already used this howto under Feisty, but WLAN didn't work very well then. It disconnected very often for no apparent reason. Now, under Gutsy I installed ndiswrapper following everything mentioned in this how-to, and WLAN now works [SIZE="1"](almost)[/SIZE] perfectly! At home with no security *and* at the university with WPA2 Enterprise with a CA certificate and everything... I don't even have to use wpa_supplicant with commandline, I can do it through the GUI of NetworkManager. So first of all a big thank you for this howto, it solved a very big problem of mine ...

But [SIZE="1"](there is always a but ... :( )[/SIZE], after every reboot, I have to reinstall ndiswrapper again. This is actually very annoying. even though I finally have a stable WLAN-connection at the university, and WLAN at home also works perfectly.

My solution now is: I created a script which I execute manually every time I boot my laptop:

```
$ cat fixwlan 
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
cd /home/gerard/Drivers/ndiswrapper/ndiswrapper-1.49
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make
sudo make install
cd /home/gerard/Drivers/dell_wireless_1390_wlan_minicard/R151517/DRIVER
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo ndiswrapper -l
sudo ndiswrapper -m
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo iwlist scanning

```

After sudo iwlist scanning wlan appears again in my NetworkManager, and it starts to connect to WLAN.

I could run this script automatically at startup, but that is obviously not the way it should be done. I was hoping someone could tell me how I can fix this problem, i.e. how I can install ndiswrapper correctly so that it doesn't need a reinstall after each reboot.

Grtz, Gerard

PS: these are my specs:
Dell Inspiron 1501
dell_wireless_1390_wlan_minicard
driver: R151517.EXE w/ bcmwl5.inf
ndiswrapper-1.49

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-11-15
> **gerdt said:**
> When I installed Gutsy on my laptop I was very glad to see that the restricted drivers handled my WLAN automatically. This meant I didn't need to do it manually using ndiswrapper. This worked fine, until ... I wanted to connect to the WLAN of my university-campus. This required WPA2 Enterprise w/ wpa_supplicant, and that didn't work with the restricted drivers from Ubuntu. So I removed the restricted drivers for my WLAN, and I installed wlan w/ ndiswrapper using this howto.

This is exactly why this thread is still alive.  Even when the restricted drivers seem to "work", they almost always have problems.  It's been like this for several versions now, and people keep coming back to ndiswrapper.

> But [SIZE="1"](there is always a but ... :( )[/SIZE], after every reboot, I have to reinstall ndiswrapper again. This is actually very annoying. even though I finally have a stable WLAN-connection at the university, and WLAN at home also works perfectly.


First, what makes you think that you have to reinstall ndiswrapper every time?  What kind of errors messages are you getting?

Second, don't use ndiswrapper 1.49.  Stick with 1.47 for now.

---

$ cat fixwlan 
sudo rmmod ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
cd /home/gerard/Drivers/ndiswrapper/ndiswrapper-1.47
sudo make uninstall
sudo make uninstall
sudo make
sudo make install
cd /home/gerard/Drivers/dell_wireless_1390_wlan_minicard/R151517/DRIVER
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo ndiswrapper -l
sudo ndiswrapper -m
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo iwlist scanning

```

### Post by fooman on 2007-11-15
gert,  have you tried the following from the instructions in the first post?  i think i've read (in another thread here) that in gutsy,  "modprobe ndiswrapper" does not load it into the modules folder...you have to manually place it there:

```
sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
```

if you get a "permission denied" message,  then try:

```
sudo -s
echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
exit
```

then reboot....see if it works.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-11-15
> **fooman said:**
> gert,  have you tried the following from the instructions in the first post?  i think i've read (in another thread here) that in gutsy,  "modprobe ndiswrapper" does not load it into the modules folder...you have to manually place it there:

```
sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
```

if you get a "permission denied" message,  then try:

```
sudo -s
echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
exit
```

then reboot....see if it works.

Exactly. Out of your "reinstall script" the only thing you actually need to run to be working again is:```
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

### Post by gerdt on 2007-11-15
I am sorry I didn't comment on those things in my first post. 
/etc/modules does contain the line ndiswrapper. I executed sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules the first time I followed this howto, so /etc/modules was and still is correct. (see below)

About sudo modprobe ndiswrapper. Whenever WLAN stops working after a reboot, I always first check lsmod, to see if ndiswrapper is still in there... And yes, every time it is there. So sudo modprobe ndiswrapper is unfortunately not the answer to my problem ...

```
$ lsmod |grep ndiswrapper
ndiswrapper           188700  0 
usbcore               138632  5 ndiswrapper,usbhid,ehci_hcd,ohci_hcd

```

But now I found out/realized another thing:

Assuming that I've WLAN working during a certain session. When I reboot graphically, [SIZE="1"]i.e. pressing the "green running man" you can see in my previous attached thumbnail ... ;)[/SIZE], WLAN stops working after rebooting. But, if instead of rebooting graphically, I reboot through the commandline (sudo reboot), WLAN works fine after the reboot.

The weirdest thing is this:
WLAN is working
I reboot graphically, WLAN doesn't work.
I don't do anything at all, and now I reboot through commandline (sudo reboot)
Now WLAN works fine...

I don't understand... What's the difference between a graphical reboot and a sudo reboot ??? And why does WLAN starts working again after I do a sudo reboot through commandline?

Perhaps it has something to do with the fact that a graphical shutdown/reboot saves your session-data. And perhaps it is saving it wrongly in my case, and therefore it messes up my WLAN. But what I don't understand is why after sudo reboot it starts working again... 

I hope I'm clear explaining all this. In any case, thanks in advance for your help!
Gerard

```
$ cat /etc/modules
# /etc/modules: kernel modules to load at boot time.
#
# This file contains the names of kernel modules that should be loaded
# at boot time, one per line. Lines beginning with "#" are ignored.

fuse
lp
**ndiswrapper**

```

```
$ cat /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
# This file lists those modules which we don't want to be loaded by
# alias expansion, usually so some other driver will be loaded for the
# device instead.

# evbug is a debug tool that should be loaded explicitly
blacklist evbug

# these drivers are very simple, the HID drivers are usually preferred
blacklist usbmouse
blacklist usbkbd

# replaced by e100
blacklist eepro100

# replaced by tulip
blacklist de4x5

# causes no end of confusion by creating unexpected network interfaces
blacklist eth1394

# snd_intel8x0m can interfere with snd_intel8x0, doesn't seem to support much
# hardware on its own (Ubuntu bug #2011, #6810)
blacklist snd_intel8x0m

# causes failure to suspend on HP compaq nc6000 (Ubuntu: #10306)
blacklist i2c_i801

# WLAN: we gebruiken ndiswrapper
**blacklist bcm43xx**

```

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-11-15
So if you boot normally, you wireless card is not recognised at all (the WiFi light doesn't turn on)? But if you run something like "sudo shutdown -r now" (or "sudo reboot") then it works when you come back up? Very strange. Can you boot normally and run ```
dmesg
``` then post the output from that command (in code tags please) up here for us to look at. Secondly if you run the following command you should see the corresponding output:```
jw5801@laptop:~$ lsmod | grep ndiswrapper
ndiswrapper           243488  0 
```If the module is being added on bootup you should see something similar, if it isn't then this command shouldn't return anything, in which case running "sudo modprobe ndiswrapper" should bring your WiFi up.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-11-16
> **gerdt said:**
> I hope I'm clear explaining all this. In any case, thanks in advance for your help!
Gerard

I'm going to go out on a limb here and say that I think you've probably tried to install / use some other wireless method before you got to this how-to.  There are a couple of strange things happening here:

First, it looks like you have two wired NICs -- is that true?  Both eth1 and eth0 are reporting "Ethernet".  I suspect that you don't actually have two wired NICs, but that you've made some other attempt at getting your wireless installed.

Are you only using network-manager to manage your Wi-Fi connections?  Are you sure that other programs such as wifi-radar aren't also trying to use the wireless NIC's resources?

Take a look at the programs that are stored as part of your default session.  If there's anything in there about wireless or networks, let us know.

---

### Post by gerdt on 2007-11-16
I also realized that when booting, the Wifi-led burns, even though WLAN doesn't work at that moment. During my reinstall script, it turns off at a certain moment, and then comes back on.

> **paperdiesel said:**
> I'm going to go out on a limb here and say that I think you've probably tried to install / use some other wireless method before you got to this how-to.
No, I haven't. I cleanly installed Gutsy, using the restricted drivers. Last week I installed ndiswrapper according to this howto and removed the restricted drivers. I do have been working with wpa_supplicant and dhclient before trying this howto.

> **paperdiesel said:**
> First, it looks like you have two wired NICs -- is that true?  Both eth1 and eth0 are reporting "Ethernet".  I suspect that you don't actually have two wired NICs, but that you've made some other attempt at getting your wireless installed.
I don't have to wired NICs. I don't know why they both report Ethernet. What should the wifi-card be reporting? Last week I followed some instructions from [[COLOR="Blue"]**_this_**[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=601671") thread in an attempt to use vpn. It worked. The only thing I had to do was change the names of eth0 and eth1 in this file:
```
/etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistant-net.rules.d
```
Apparantly network-manager-pptp only works with eth0. So I had to change my eth1 into eth0 in order to get vpn working. So now, eth0 is my wifi, and eth1 is my wired NIC.
```
gerard@INSPIRON:/etc/udev/rules.d$ cat 70-persistent-net.rules
# This file maintains persistent names for network interfaces.
# See udev(7) for syntax.
#
# Entries are automatically added by the 75-persistent-net-generator.rules
# file; however you are also free to add your own entries.

# PCI device 0x14e4:0x170c (b44) ###KABEL###
SUBSYSTEM=="net", DRIVERS=="?*", ATTRS{address}=="00:19:b9:62:49:04", NAME="eth1"

# PCI device 0x14e4:0x4311 (bcm43xx) ###WIFI###
SUBSYSTEM=="net", DRIVERS=="?*", ATTRS{address}=="00:19:7e:53:4b:7b", NAME="eth0"

```

> **paperdiesel said:**
> Are you only using network-manager to manage your Wi-Fi connections?  Are you sure that other programs such as wifi-radar aren't also trying to use the wireless NIC's resources?
Yes, only network manager. Never tried nor installed any other network manager.

> **paperdiesel said:**
> Take a look at the programs that are stored as part of your default session.  If there's anything in there about wireless or networks, let us know.

```

I marked some lines in bold in this dmesg. It shows that ndiswrapper is not able to initialize eth0, but I don't understand why. Perhaps someone else does understand what dmesg is saying?

And I still don't understand why after a [FONT="Courier New"]sudo reboot[/FONT]/[FONT="Courier New"]sudo shutdown -r now[/FONT], ndiswrapper can initialize eth0, but after a normal reboot is cannot...

dmesg also says:
```
ndiswrapper: driver bcmwl5 (Broadcom,10/12/2006, 4.100.15.5) loaded
```
Why? I've put the bcm driver in a blacklist, haven't I ?
```
$ cat /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
...
...
blacklist bcm43xx
```

What am I doing wrong ?!?!?! :)
Grtz, Gerard

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-11-16
> **gerdt said:**
> Last week I followed some instructions from [[COLOR="Blue"]**_this_**[/COLOR]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=601671") thread in an attempt to use vpn. It worked. The only thing I had to do was change the names of eth0 and eth1 in this file:
```
/etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistant-net.rules.d
```
Apparantly network-manager-pptp only works with eth0. So I had to change my eth1 into eth0 in order to get vpn working. So now, eth0 is my wifi, and eth1 is my wired NIC.

Bingo.  To my knowledge, ndiswrapper tries to set wlan0 (the normal device name for wireless) to eth1.  If eth1 is already taken by some other resource, you'll have problems.  This would also explain why wireless works again after your run your reinstall script.  Think of it as a battle for eth1 assignment:  eth1 gets assigned to wifi first, then your other script sets eth0 (ethernet) to eth1, which drops your wifi.  Then you run your reinstall script, and ndiswrapper probably detects that eth1 is in use, and assigns eth0 instead.  Wifi and VPN are both competing for eth1, and since the VPN script is run after the initial wifi config, VPN wins and wifi loses.

> dmesg also says:
```
ndiswrapper: driver bcmwl5 (Broadcom,10/12/2006, 4.100.15.5) loaded
```
Why? I've put the bcm driver in a blacklist, haven't I ?

No, that's a good message.  Ndiswrapper is successfully loading the windows drivers.  All good.

---

### Post by Tatterdemalian on 2007-11-16
> **gerdt said:**
> dmesg also says:
```
ndiswrapper: driver bcmwl5 (Broadcom,10/12/2006, 4.100.15.5) loaded
```
Why? I've put the bcm driver in a blacklist, haven't I ?
```
$ cat /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
...
...
blacklist bcm43xx
```

What am I doing wrong ?!?!?! :)

Nothing, bcmwl5 is a different driver from bcm43xx. bcm43xx is the restricted driver that Ubuntu will keep trying to install over ndiswrapper and bcmwl5 unless you specifically blacklist it. bcmwl5 is the official Broadcom Windows driver that ndiswrapper lets you use in Linux.

---

### Post by Tatterdemalian on 2007-11-16
Are there any plans for ndiswrapper to support Vista drivers like the new version of the Broadcom adapter (bcmwl6)? Or are the new post-Vista wifi cards going to be using too many firmware / hardware lockouts to make it legal and/or worthwhile?

---

### Post by gerdt on 2007-11-17
> **paperdiesel said:**
> Bingo.  To my knowledge, ndiswrapper tries to set wlan0 (the normal device name for wireless) to eth1.  If eth1 is already taken by some other resource, you'll have problems.  This would also explain why wireless works again after your run your reinstall script.  Think of it as a battle for eth1 assignment:  eth1 gets assigned to wifi first, then your other script sets eth0 (ethernet) to eth1, which drops your wifi.  Then you run your reinstall script, and ndiswrapper probably detects that eth1 is in use, and assigns eth0 instead.  Wifi and VPN are both competing for eth1, and since the VPN script is run after the initial wifi config, VPN wins and wifi loses.

Thanks for the reply, I'll test it by putting switching eth0 and eth1 again. But, if you're right, this means that I either have WLAN at startup or VPN. It seams that both things are incompatible... In that case, I think I'll stick to the script as I otherwise cannot use VPN ...

There is one thing that I quite don't understand yet. Why does it sometimes work, after e.g. a commandline reboot?

---

### Post by SaberCat on 2007-11-17
I thought I would contribute some information on how I got paperdiesel's HOWTO procedure to work on my Dell Inspiron 1501 laptop.

After installing ubuntu 7.04 I performed the procedure described in the HOWTO *exactly* as described. But when I rebooted, the machine hung during startup (I disconnected the battery to get it to shut down).

After reinstalling ubuntu 7.04 I decided that this time I would omit the following operations from STEP 4: INSTALL DRIVERS in the HOWTO:

sudo ndiswrapper -m
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules

Since this causes the module to be loaded at boot, my reasoning was that I would let the machine boot first and then manually load the module once the system was up. With this change, the machine booted properly.

At that point I discovered that wireless access was available! In addition the system rebooted correctly and wireless access was still available. So I have been running with it this way ever since.

I don't know why the above operations were not necessary. Anyway, I thought I would pass this experience along -- in case it would help others...

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-11-19
He means bcm43xx. Which for the record *isn't* a proprietry driver. It's the open source driver that Ubuntu will try and enable by default, that we blacklist in the how-to because it doesn't work as well. The driver we're installing with ndiswrapper is the one that's proprietry. That being said it make work quite well for the card you have, it only supports 802.11b connections though. There have been many pages of discussion about it throughout this thread if you care to use the search feature. Also if you use "lspci" to find out what wifi card is in the laptop then you should be able to find out more information about what works well with it and what doesn't. bcm43xx requires some fancy firmware which is installed via the restricted drivers manager in gutsy, in any earlier versions it's unusable. So hopefully the open source driver will be good enough that we won't have to bother with ndiswrapper soon, but for the moment it isn't really.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2007-11-19
> **GinKen said:**
> I decided to go to Dell's site and try driver recommended for this model, which was R151522.exe, removed bcmwl5 and installed this one (bcmwl6) but with same results, though tested ok and light was on, no real internet connection.  

To my knowledge, the bcmwl6 driver will NOT work with ndiswrapper because bcmwl6 is the vista driver and is not supported by ndiswrapper.  Stick with the bcmwl5 driver instead. 

And, oh yah, what jw said :cool:.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-11-27
> **collegedude10 said:**
> I need help...

 when i get to this part




i get an error when i do "sudo ndiswrapper -l", terminal tells me "bcmwl5 : invalid driver!"

what to do?

If you're getting this error then you weren't in the right directory when you tried to install the driver. You need to remove the driver first before. ```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
```Then you'll need to pick up with the how-to at the appropriate point and make sure you're in the right directory when you install the driver.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-12-06
Whatever directory you put the ndiswrapper tarball in, will have a folder in it called ndiswrapper-1.47 (or something similar depending on the version you have) after you extract it. Assuming you had the tarball in your home directory open a terminal and type ```
cd ndiswrapper-1.47
```You can also use the <tab> key to autocomplete a directory like this, try typing "cd ndiswrapper-" then pressing "tab" and seeing what happens.
I'm not sure what you mean when you ask "what directory do I use for dell wireless utility," so some clarification of the question might be necessary.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-12-06
> **latestgood said:**
> Hello,

I'm stuck at step 4. I extracted the driver.

latestgood@latestgood-laptop:~$ cd R151517.EXE/home/latestgood
bash: cd: R151517.EXE/home/latestgood: Not a directory
latestgood@latestgood-laptop:~$ cd /home/latestgood/R151517.EXE
bash: cd: /home/latestgood/R151517.EXE: Not a directory
latestgood@latestgood-laptop:~$ 

I don't know what I'm doing wrong. 
My overall problem is I don't know why I have to change directory or how. I thought everything will be in my home directory? If there is a link on how to on directory, I would REALLY APPRECIATE IT.

thank you

Your approach is correct, however R151517.EXE is not a directory, hence the bash response you got. If you ran the command to extract the .exe as it is given in the howto, then the directory you need to change to is DRIVER, so run:
```
cd DRIVER
```then continue with the howto, "sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5" etc.

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-12-08
> **ZenOneMike said:**
> i'm new to the forum and new to Ubuntu/Linux. I am also having problems with my wireless connection on my laptop. I am still not sure how the instructions are to be use. do i use windows when installing these drivers or Linux? see i need windows xp to connect to the internet, and in order to download all those items. Please can someone let me know what programs do i use when i need to do the changes or installations, linux or windows xp?

in other words, can i use windows when i'm downloading the dswrapper and then install them while still on windows. that's where i'm kinda  lost. 

thanks.:confused:

No you can't. You need to use ndiswrapper, a Linux program, to install and run the WinXP driver in Linux. You could download the necessary files for the installation via windows, then boot into linux and actually run the install, but you can't install something in a different operating system to the one you are booted into.

### Post by cgm12mgc on 2007-12-23
i have a dell 1405 with the 1390 wlan card, i have done the make uninstall for the ndiswrapper many times and there is nothing left for it, my problem comes when i type in "make" i get error 2, i have tried make install aswell and get the same result, anyone have a solution?

### Post by walkerk on 2007-12-23
> **cgm12mgc said:**
> i have a dell 1405 with the 1390 wlan card, i have done the make uninstall for the ndiswrapper many times and there is nothing left for it, my problem comes when i type in "make" i get error 2, i have tried make install aswell and get the same result, anyone have a solution?

Thanks,
Charlie

try deleting that directory and then re-exrtacting ndiswrapper*.tar.gz

example:
```
rm -rf ndiswrapper*
```

untar
```
tar -xzvf ndiswrapper*.tar.gz
```

cd back into ndiswrapper
```
cd ndiswrapper*
```
```
make
```
```
sudo make install
```

now follow the instructions...

### Post by jw5801 on 2007-12-23
Check that you have the build-essential package:
```
sudo apt-get install build-essential
```
If you do and this is still happening then try downloading the tarball again, it may have corrupted during download.

### Post by Ryo964 on 2008-01-02
Bless you! I am a brand spanking new Linux user and tried all kinds to get my wireless driver working. This worked! Mines is a Dell Inspiron 1501. One difference--within the terminal I couldn't get the driver recognized by ndiswrapper, but when I deleted the driver and readded within the graphical ndiswrapper (windows wireless driver (System-->Admin-->Windows Wireless Driver) which I had from a previous install) it all worked! Thanks again! Hope to be Linux full-time very soon.

with apologies for my lack of language!

"lo        Interface doesn't support scanning.

eth0      Interface doesn't support scanning."

Panda

Your wireless card should be set up as the eth1 interface by ndiswrapper. Since this interface isn't present, the install didn't work at all. Give it another try and then report back. If it doesn't work, copy and paste everything you typed in and it's output and post it here in [code] tags.

### Post by theRightNee on 2008-01-04
thank you soo much! although i do not have any of the hardware devices this tutorial was aimed towards, it got me through and helped me set up my wireless network! one little not tho, instea of using "sudo make  uninstall" the ndiswrapper guide said to use "sudo make distclean" and it seem to work better. please comment!

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2008-01-04
> **theRightNee said:**
> thank you soo much! although i do not have any of the hardware devices this tutorial was aimed towards, it got me through and helped me set up my wireless network! one little not tho, instea of using "sudo make  uninstall" the ndiswrapper guide said to use "sudo make distclean" and it seem to work better. please comment!

I noticed that with ndis 1.51 as well.  I think it's a new feature, but it doesn't do exactly the same thing as make uninstall.  As I understand it, it's similar to the generic "make clean", which removes the compiled objects and modules and forces the subsequent "make" to start over and compile everything from scratch.  I'll probably update the guide to use the newer ndiswrapper version, and add the sudo make distclean as part of the cleaning steps.

Good catch.

---

### Post by dugh on 2008-01-06
These instructions have worked perfectly for me over the past 6 months, thanks.

I think this thread needs to be renamed though, because it works for many other models than the Dell E1505.
Pretty much any broadcom 4318 or related wireless card I believe.

I think a lot more people are trying and failing to get their wireless working using other threads on the broadcom 4318 when they should be using these instructions.
Also, the people on the #ubuntu irc channel seem to have no clue about this thread, and people come in asking how to get broadcom wireless working all the time.

Perhaps this should be put on the ubuntu wiki even.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2008-01-07
> **dugh said:**
> These instructions have worked perfectly for me over the past 6 months, thanks.

I think this thread needs to be renamed though, because it works for many other models than the Dell E1505.
Pretty much any broadcom 4318 or related wireless card I believe.

I think a lot more people are trying and failing to get their wireless working using other threads on the broadcom 4318 when they should be using these instructions.
Also, the people on the #ubuntu irc channel seem to have no clue about this thread, and people come in asking how to get broadcom wireless working all the time.

Perhaps this should be put on the ubuntu wiki even.

Dugh,

You bring up some good points.  I've often thought about broadening the scope of the title as a way of making the thread more relevant to others.  The only thing that's keeping me from doing so is the fact that I can't personally verify if this method works with any other models.  I have access to a Dell Inspiron E1505 laptop with the Broadcom 1390 wireless NIC, so I know for a fact that it works and I'm willing to back that up with support via this thread.

There are probably hundreds of combinations of laptops and wireless NICs inside them.  The methods of this how-to can probably be generically applied to anyone's laptop.  From a high level, all we're doing is is compiling ndiswrapper from source, extracting the windows wireless driver, and then installing that windows driver into ndiswrapper.  For any given laptop manufacturer/model, a user could follow the same steps: compile ndiswrapper, install the windows driver for their model, and they should be all set.

One thought I do have is to rename this thread to something more broad, and then add some comments like "if you have a different laptop, use your laptop manufacturer's drivers instead of these."  

As for IRC users and the wiki, well, I wish that they knew about this thread and pointed people in its direction.  Before I wrote this how-to, I recall how frustrated I was that there were so many supposed how-tos for broadcom wireless, but none of them really worked and they were all poorly written and/or unsupported by the original author.  That inspired me to write this and commit myself to supporting the users who come here asking for help.  This thread has had almost 300,000 views since it began, and the success rate hovers around 80%.  Those numbers speak for themselves.  (plus I'm convinced that those who didn't vote success in the poll either gave up too soon or tried to make it work without using a clean install).

---

### Post by bradleymccaskill on 2008-01-08
hey thanks for the code. the structures seem great and easy to follow. but when I'm having problems with step 3. after rebooting i enter the following code and get the following result:

cd YOUR-NDISWRAPPER-DIRECTORY
bash: cd: YOUR-NDISWRAPPER-DIRECTORY: No such file or directory

i'm definitely a noob at this, so if the answer is really simple i apologie. Anyone have any ideas?

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2008-01-08
> **bradleymccaskill said:**
> hey thanks for the code. the structures seem great and easy to follow. but when I'm having problems with step 3. after rebooting i enter the following code and get the following result:

cd YOUR-NDISWRAPPER-DIRECTORY
bash: cd: YOUR-NDISWRAPPER-DIRECTORY: No such file or directory

i'm definitely a noob at this, so if the answer is really simple i apologie. Anyone have any ideas?

YOUR-NDISWRAPPER-DIRECTORY isn't a real directory.  It's meant to mean "fill this is with whatever your directory is called".  If you're using ndiswrapper 1.51, the directory will most likely be called ndiswrapper-1.51.  So try something like
```

cd ndiswrapper-1.51
```

The same principle applies to the DRIVER directory where you extracted the windows driver from dell.

Ok, so the problem is that my wireless internet still doesn't work. the "wifi" light is not illuminated and it doesn't come up when I run the iwlist scanning or iwconfig. So I guess I need to know if I am doing something wrong. I did all the steps in order and everything. This is the driver that I am using (instead of the one for the inspiron): R155386.EXE

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2008-01-11
> **chrini said:**
> 
03:03.0 Network controller: Intel Corporation PRO/Wireless 2200BG Network Connection (rev 05)


That's your problem right there.  You don't have a broadcom wireless card -- you have an Intel one.  This how-to is for broadcom cards.

However, the methods should still work, the only difference being you need to address Intel issues.  Instead of doing things like blacklisting bcm43xx, you'll probably have to blacklist whatever intel drivers try to load by default.  I have no experience with the Intel chips so I can't be of much help.

---

### Post by chrini on 2008-01-11
Darn, that's too bad. I didn't even think about it being a different type of card (I mean it crossed my mind, because I obviously had to find different drivers), but I figured it might at least work. I did blacklist the bcm43xx as well as the ipw2200 (I think that's the one) whic is the default driver that Ubuntu installs (and is what I'd think to be the working one) but still doesn't work. I have a link to download the ipw2200 driver for linux, so maybe I'll try to download it myself. 

Thanks for your help, let me know if you come up with any ideas.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2008-01-16
> **ErikTheRed said:**
> 1. your laptop model: Dell Vostro 1500
2. ubuntu version: 7.10
3. your lspci output: attached
4. the relevant rows from /var/log/syslog: 
5. the relevant rows from dmesg: [  924.604000] ADDRCONF(NETDEV_UP): wlan0: link is not ready
6. any unexpected output when following the steps in the how-to: 

Upon completing the how-to i was able to see a bunch of SSIDs. As soon as I tried to connect, however, they all disappeared and I can't seem to get any to list any more. Probably has something to do with the dmesg output I listed.

Reboot, then open a terminal:

```

sudo ndiswrapper -l
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo iwlist scanning
dmesg
```

Then come back here and post the results from the 3 sudo commands and the relevant rows from the dmesg command.

> Perhaps this info might also help.  Here is the contents of the file unzipped.

AMD64         bcmwlnpf.sys  data2.cab       MFC71.DLL    setup.iss
ATL71.DLL     bcmwlpkt.dll  DellInfo64.exe  msvcp71.DLL  Version.txt
bcm1xsup.dll  bcmwls32.exe  DellInfo.exe    msvcr71.DLL  WLBCGCBPRO731.DLL
BCMLogon.dll  bcmwls64.exe  dellinst.exe    preflib.dll  wltray.exe
Bcmnpf64.sys  bcmwls.ini    DRIVER          R151517.EXE  wltrynt.dll
bcmwlcpl.cpl  bcmwltry.exe  ikernel.ex_     README.rtf   wltrysvc.exe
bcmwlhlp.cab  bcmwlu00.exe  is.exe          setup.exe
bcmwlhlp.chm  data1.cab     launcher.ini    Setup.ini
bcmwliss.dll  data1.hdr     layout.bin      setup.inx

No problem.  You see that entry called DRIVER?  That's a directory.  Go inside that directory, and you should find your file.  i.e. cd DRIVER.

It looks like you didn't blacklist the bcm43xx firmware.  Did you try other methods before coming here?  Did you make sure to do the blacklist step in the howto?  Do a ```
sudo cat /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
``` and post the rows back here.

### Post by pingnak on 2008-01-23
Thanks, paperdiesel!

I had been 'tinkering' with my wireless for some time, and the caveat in the O.P. about this 'not working' if I had done so was disheartening.  With the previous ubuntu version, I'd previously gotten it to the point where it would FIND my router, but wouldn't connect to it.  This time around, I was able to connect to it, which made my day.  But I upgraded because I needed other things that 7.10 finally fixed.

Fortunately, I'm eviler than my computer, and remembered some of what I had previously done all those months back when I'd tinkered with Feisty.  For one thing, another version of 'ndiswrapper' can be installed with 'Add/Remove Applications' or 'Synaptic Package Manager'.  So if you had ever done it with those, be sure to REMOVE it with those before following the instructions.

'ndiswrapper -r' adamantly refused to remove the previous version of the dell driver from its little cache.  Fortunately, it told me where the cache was, so I just 'sudo rm -rf' the folder and started over.  Mass destruction makes me happy.

What I also discovered was the various installable 'wireless manager' toys all don't work.  Only the 'nm-applet' thing on the top gnome panel that was installed by default would connect right.  The other 'helpers' only confused matters.  For whatever reason, left-clicking gives you more options than right-clicking, and everything you need is in there if you just grope around for it a bit.

Anyways, now between wine and vmware to keep my remaining costly Windoze apps alive, I can finally make the plunge and never (well, seldom) run Windows again as my primary development platform.  

Thank you to Dell and the Microsoft Vista team for making my PC such a useless pain in the butt to use that I finally began using Linux full-time instead of just building and testing things in Linux under VMWare.

Thank you paperdiesel, for removing this last little barrier and helping make this proprietary Dell hunk of steaming excrement mostly 'work'.

Actually, I did have a 'Plan B', and that was to order another mini-card that DEFINITELY worked with Ubuntu and give that rotten proprietary Dell card to the cat on the end of a string.  OK, maybe not, since the solder probably has lead in it.  It really irks me that Dell would 'recommend' the card and not have a Linux driver available for it.  Not quite as much as Microsoft's paranoid 'authorization' model, closed drivers, degredation of ALL media playback "just in case" my personal audio/video files might have something copyrighted in them. the wholesale breakage of software that required multiple expensive 'upgrades' to (only partially) fix, and the shuffling of configuration interfaces that made the Linux scripting mess look quite homey and familiar by comparison... but Dell's role in this was irksome nonetheless.

---

Excellent post and instructions!  I'm so glad someone finally figured this out!  No thanks to Broadcom or Dell.  This issue was definitely keeping me from using Linux, and now I can.  thanks!

### Post by ryanattar on 2008-01-27
Worked great.   One tip though---

Make sure you press CTRL+F2 (the wireless button on the dell inspiron 1505), to turn on your wireless hardware,  by default when you start your system it is turned off.

pls help me,after doing as your guide, i got this:

root@myaccount:~# sudo iwlist scanning
lo        Interface doesn't support scanning.

eth0      Interface doesn't support scanning.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-01-29
> **chitahihu said:**
> pls help me,after doing as your guide, i got this:

root@myaccount:~# sudo iwlist scanning
lo        Interface doesn't support scanning.

eth0      Interface doesn't support scanning.

pls help me and i did not see wireless option in my network any longer, i saw that before i installed but now it does not show up.

Something very important somewhere has been missed! It appears as though ndiswrapper has not been installed at all, if your wireless isn't being recognised at all however, you have blacklisted the native driver correctly. Try the how to again starting from the beginning. If you still can't get it to work, post up the entire terminal input and output as you went along and we'll see what's going wrong.

### Post by midnightray on 2008-02-03
Thanks, the restricted drivers took 2-3 trys to connect!

Now its first time!

BTW is it safe to delete the folders made by the ndiswrapper and dell's exe. there kinda in my home folder :(

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-02-03
You can safely delete everything you extracted from the ndiswrapper tarball and R151517.EXE. The actual ndiswrapper program and kernel modules are installed somewhere different and ndiswrapper creates it's own entry for bcmwl5.

---

### Post by gtg465x on 2008-02-03
I'm not sure if this has been covered yet in this thread, but after following this guide I can connect to any wireless network except those with a hidden ssid. I'm trying to connect to my campus network, which uses a WEP key and hidden ssid.

I have a Dell Inspiron 1501 with the 1390 wireless card and Ubuntu 7.10.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-02-03
If you're using nm-applet (the default Gnome network manager) then clicking on it's icon in the tray should give you an option to "connect to other wireless network" where you should be able to enter the settings as you need.

---

### Post by gtg465x on 2008-02-03
> **jw5801 said:**
> If you're using nm-applet (the default Gnome network manager) then clicking on it's icon in the tray should give you an option to "connect to other wireless network" where you should be able to enter the settings as you need.

Yeah I know. It doesn't work. It never connects. It tries for a while and then either pops up a box to enter the WEP key again or just connects to a different network.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-02-03
Hmm... well if you can connect to other wireless networks with no issues then I would doubt that it's a driver problem. You could try a different network manager? Try network-manager or wifi-radar, and see if either of them give you better results. They're both in the repositories.

---

### Post by gtg465x on 2008-02-03
Thanks guys, but never mind. I figured out a different solution. In about 3 clicks and no command lines I installed the bcm43xx restricted driver and hidden ssids work great now. I don't know why I tried all of this tedious ndiswrapper stuff when I should have just tried the easy way first.

---

### Post by mobiusdim on 2008-02-07
These instructions worked on my brand new Dell XPS 420 but with two caveats.

1. Although the system comes with a dell broadcom 4328 (Rev 3.0) card on a vista platform, the actual vista driver for it doesn't work. This is duly noted by the presence of a  network manager "unknown symbol" error message in dmesg and in /var/log/syslog . But the good news is that the R151517.EXE driver (perhaps written for XP ?) works just fine. Get this at [http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R151517.EXE](http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R151517.EXE) . 

Yet another caveat is that the network manager may not be able to connect automatically to WEP encrypted connections but can do so when commanded through the terminal. For me its no problem since I use the terminal. The forums have umpteen number of guides on how to do this and scripts that automate the process.
 
Finally , thanks a lot. Setting up wireless is really the first step that makes all other mods and hacks that much more simpler. 

Happy Ubuntu !

---

### Post by nmarcos on 2008-02-07
Hi Guys,

I've made this command as much is possible and I always get this message:

NOTE: Not all installed files are removed, as different distributions install ndiswrapper files at different places.
Run uninstall as many times as necessary until no "removing" messages appear below.

However I continued with the others steps and IT WORKED!

Thank you so much!:lolflag:

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-02-07
> **nmarcos said:**
> Hi Guys,

I've made this command as much is possible and I always get this message:

NOTE: Not all installed files are removed, as different distributions install ndiswrapper files at different places.
Run uninstall as many times as necessary until no "removing" messages appear below.

However I continued with the others steps and IT WORKED!

Thank you so much!:lolflag:

That does appear to be a common misconception! If there was anything left to remove the output would be:
```
NOTE: Not all installed files are removed, as different distributions install ndiswrapper files at different places.
Run uninstall as many times as necessary until no "removing" messages appear below.

removing blah.fileextension
removing things-you-dont.want
```

I was confused the first time I went past that too!

---

### Post by brted on 2008-02-16
I got a Dell Vostro 1400 which came with a 1395 card. That required the Dell driver R174291.exe but otherwise everything else was the same (thanks!). Dell says that driver works for the 1390 and a number of others, so maybe it would be good to update the instructions?

Here's the link:

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-02-17
Step four involves extracting the driver needed for the Broadcom 1390 WLAN. Since you don't have this wireless chipset, you probably don't want to follow the rest, since it won't get you anywhere. This is your wireless chipset:
```

Ethernet controller: Atheros Communications, Inc. AR5006EG 802.11 b/g Wireless PCI Express Adapter (rev 01)
```

So search the forums for AR5006EG and see what you come up with.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2008-02-22
> **Phaser said:**
> Thank you very much I followed this step by step and it worked just fine. Any idea how to get my Dell 1390 working on a encrypted network? Also I am curious to how you got this info or knew how to do it, any info would be greatly appreciated.

The driver from Dell supports wep 64 & 128 in ascii, text, and passphrase.  It also supports WPA and WPA2, and it's possible there are other derivatives supported as well.  If you're having trouble connecting to a secured network, it's most likely NOT a driver issue on your laptop.

As far as how I figured out the steps, it was nothing magic or fancy -- just trial and error.  I read the ndiswrapper documentation and then customized those instructions to work with ubuntu.  For example, the ndiswrapper docs talk about compiling the ndiswrapper module.  I knew, however, that ubuntu has its own ndiswrapper binary supplied and managed by ubuntu's package manager.  In this case, it was necessary to remove ubuntu's ndiswrapper version BEFORE compiling and installing the ndiswrapper version from source.  That process helped me create Steps 1, 2 and 3.  It's also important to note that the how-to continues to evolve as users report back with their experiences of using it.  The how-to has changed quite a bit since its initial version.

I've had several requests from people who want to publish the how-to on various websites and web journals, and I'm always very happy to let people do so.  I only ask that they give credit by linking to the how-to here at ubuntuforums.org, and that they don't change the content.  Sometimes they're okay with that, sometimes not.

---

### Post by jeffeb3 on 2008-02-25
I'd just like to add my $0.02.

With 101+ pages of comments, this probably won't be read by any organic being.  But, now that my tx1000 series laptop has working wireless, I'd like to add two places that I got snagged.

First, was that I had a hard time finding the right windows drivers, I kept getting errors (I think they were in dmsg) that said ndis couldn't load the driver, no device found.  So I just had ndiswrapper point to the windows ones.  The ones on the other side of my dual boot.  I can't remember, but I think I then copied them over to the Linux side, just in case windows changed.  

Second, and this is important.  Is that after I had the ndiswrapper working, and I'd reboote. 

YOU NEED TO PRESS THE BUTTON ON THE BOTTOM  to activate the wifi.  

I had the solution hours before I had the network working just because I didn't try to press the button that turns on the WiFi.  I think this is pretty important info, maybe editing the original instructions?

Give it a shot, but before you install these new drivers, remember to UNINSTALL the drivers you tried previously.  That's very important.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-02
It doesn't print "Nothing to install." If there's nothing to install then there won't be any messages that say "uninstalling program-you-don't-won't." So if it's just printing the "run this command until no uninstalling messages appear below" message then there's nothing to uninstall.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-02
Run ```
lsmod | grep ndis
```and post the output here. That should tell you whether the module was loaded into the kernel or not. If it wasn't, run ```
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
``` and that should either fix it, or fail but give us some output as to why it failed.

### Post by paperdiesel on 2008-03-03
> **scotc said:**
> Wirless on Dell Vostro 1500



Thanks paperdiesel.  No, the WLAN card is internal, looks just like an average card.  I believe it to be a Broadcom BCM4310, but I agree, the "USB controller" part is very confusing.  
That might not even be referring to the WLAN at all, in which case neither lspci or lsusb are finding the card as there's no mention of any network other than the ethernet.
I have physically removed the card and reinstalled it. No change.

Because I am new, can you remind me how I uninstall my bcmwl5.inf driver that I have installed using ndiswrapper as per thread instructions.

Sure, just do: ```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
sudo ndiswrapper -l
```

That second command lists the drivers installed into ndiswrapper -- it should return nothing.

> I have the option to swap a WLAN card from another machine, one that seems to be supported according to the HCL. If I do so, will Ubuntu recognise it on boot or do I need to intervene with hardware or driver installation?

Thanks again for the post

I guess it depends on the chipset.  If it's a broadcom card, you'll probably need ndiswrapper.  Other chipsets have different levels of support, so it really depends on who makes it.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-06
We're a long way past gutsy alpha 6... And ndiswrapper works fine in gutsy. Did you blacklist the bcm43xx module?

Do you mean hardy? If so, then that was an issue with an addition to the kernel (globally, not just in Ubuntu) denying ndiswrapper access to GPLONLY symbols, which obviously stopped it from working. I'm not sure what the current state of the kernel is, but there was a patch to fix it, you would have to compile your own kernel however. I don't actually know which kernel Hardy is using however, so I'm not sure of the accuracy of this.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-06
The patch is here:
[http://kerneltrap.org/mailarchive/linux-kernel/2008/2/28/1018334](http://kerneltrap.org/mailarchive/linux-kernel/2008/2/28/1018334)

and for a bit of commentry on the issue, look here:
[http://kerneltrap.org/Linux/NDISwrapper_and_the_GPL](http://kerneltrap.org/Linux/NDISwrapper_and_the_GPL)
[http://lkml.org/lkml/2008/3/4/300](http://lkml.org/lkml/2008/3/4/300)

how to you use this patch?  do you just paste it into the kernel?

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-07
Using the "patch" command. See "man patch" for more info. Also look at the master kernel thread: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=311158](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=311158)

**Unless you're using the Hardy Alpha _and_ my suspicions are correct, there is _no need_ to compile a new patched kernel to use ndiswrapper.**

For those actually using Hardy, it's probably significantly simpler to revert to the older kernel (it'll still be installed, just set grub's menu.lst to boot it by default, if you don't know how to do that, you shouldn't be using an alpha). I'm sure that the Ubuntu devs will update the kernel without this problem sometime soon, so in the mean time use the old(er) kernel and wait for a kernel update.

EDIT: I am currently installing the new kernel to test whether ndiswrapper will work.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-07
Ok, so I'm double posting. But I upgraded to the current Hardy kernel, and you are correct. ndiswrapper fails miserably, can't even create an interface (eth1) for itself, which makes me think that this is the issue we're seeing. So I would declare this a bug, go hunting for a bug report and see what it has to say. If you can't find one, start one.

On the plus side, the hardy kernel does ship with a new broadcom module (b43), so you could give that a whirl and report back on how effective it is. Have a look at the miscellaneous fixes towards the end of this how-to to see how to use it.

Hmm... the second issue there (interface naming), could also be the culprit. If I can be bothered I'll check into that, but you may want to first. Try it without installing the b43 firmware (make sure you blacklist the b43 module too), and then follow the instructions using ndiswrapper as the driver.

Please report back with whatever your findings may be!

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-07
Ok, so it's effectively the same as bcm43xx. And yes synaptic is a GUI for apt. So it would just have been reconfiguring it that did it. sudo dpkg-reconfigure b43-fwcutter probably would have done the same thing. I'd wait for a kernel update and see if that fixes it, which it should.

It's been a while since I've used KNetworkManager.  If it's at all similar to gnome's, there should be a setting that says "enable wireless" -- make sure that's checked.  Also, see if there's an option for automatic configuration -- don't use the manual one if you want to be able to pick wireless networks from a list.  Finally, you might try rebooting (if you haven't done so already) to see if the network manager picks up on the new wireless interface.

### Post by Xephyrind on 2008-03-09
The author of this post offers a great solution that after nearly 12 hrs... more like 9+ hrs straight.... I was able to get wireless working. While I am in everyway thankful for the author of this post, I must disclose the fact that certain parts of the procedure was accomplished by another post on another forum due to certain procedure written by this author was being denied. This is in no way to attack the efficiency or correctness of the author of this post but rather trying to offer an alternative solution to all other readers who encounter this painful similar problem. The other author's post, which I am about to list below, has a better solution to certain parts of the problem presented here. My personal experience after 9 Hrs of dreading through the solution and reattempts has yielded 1 simple statement: Combine the 2, If one doesn't work for certain part then use the other solution given by the other poster: 

[http://www.ubuntu1501.com/2007/01/fixing-wifi-on-dell-1501.html](http://www.ubuntu1501.com/2007/01/fixing-wifi-on-dell-1501.html)

Hopefully, with both extensively well written documents and my personal general suggestions will yield a much better experience for everyone who are new to linux and setting up wireless for Dell 1505 for the first time just like me.

A few personal noobie ways to get around some potential problems not listed here are as follows:

1. As I've said before, combine both solutions from these two great posters.

2. Both solutions require you to get updates from the internet as well as the original CD. As a n00b to Linux who doesn't really understand how to use terminal operation on other computers and transfer the updates to other systems, I got around this problem by simply using a wired network since your wired network in general doesn't really mess up like the wireless one. The CD problem is well... Get a CD.

With luck on finding the right posts quickly and with my personal experience added in, people who run into this problem after me will be able to solve this much faster and less pain than I have experienced.

Finally: the newest ndiswrapper is 1.52 and the newest WiFi driver for Inspiron E1505 is 151519.EXE as of today Sunday March 9th. Both of which can be obtained easily with the code given by both authors then simply substitute in 1.52 and 151519.EXE, respectively instead of the original outdated files. ### Post by paperdiesel on 2008-03-10
> **mpgarate said:**
> this how to works fine for me, but will this dell wireless card ever be fully supported out of the box by any distro? or will I be cursed to forever use how-tos to get it working

Good question.  From a business perspective, I doubt that Broadcom will ever release the specs for its cards to the community.  Therefore, the community will have to rely on the reverse-engineering efforts of those who are willing to do so and release a driver.

The out-of-the-box support for these cards has slowly gotten better over the last year or two.  With Gutsy, there's a decent chance that your broadcom card will work without ndiswrapper, albeit slow and somewhat buggy.

Until the default driver is improved to be faster and comparably stable, it looks like using ndiswrapper to load the windows driver into the kernel is the best solution.  

On the other hand, you could target your next laptop purchase towards using a wireless chipset that has better support in the first place.  But Dell (and others) tend to limit your choices, if you even get a choice at all.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-15
As far as I'm aware, ndiswrapper doesn't work with the current kernel in Hardy. See my post a page or two back for a bit more info.

I think the "ndiswrapper -m" output is because you already had the ndiswrapper module inserted in the kernel.

EDIT: Previous discussion started [here](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=4467608#post4467608), and there's some more discussion [here](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=4468363#post4468363).

---

### Post by erunaheru on 2008-03-18
Working on a E1705, so it may be a little different. The new version of ndiswrapper (1.52) works with the Hardy kernel, but ssb gets in the way. Unfortunately, it's needed to load the drivers for the broadcom wired connection. If you don't need wired networking, just blacklist b44, b43, and ssb. I got both to work by running:

```
sudo modprobe -r b44
sudo modprobe -r b43
sudo modprobe -r ssb
sudo modprobe -r ndiswrapper
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
sudo modprobe b44
```

i.e. remove all drivers, and reload them in an order that allows ndis to grab the wifi before ssb can (ssb loads as a dependency of b44). This needs to be run after every login (I just made a script to do it). I'm sure there is a more elegant way to do this, but I'm still fairly inexperienced and have no idea how to change the order that the modules originally load in.

EDIT: changed code slightly based on jw5801's comments and further testing.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-18
Good to know! I couldn't see much of an improvement to anything whilst I was running the Hardy kernel so I'm still using 2.6.22 for the moment. b43 is the new version of bcm43xx, which only deals with the broadcom wireless chipsets, so you don't need to reload that one, in fact it might mess with ndiswrapper. Good to know that ndiswrapper is back to working! What kernel is Hardy using now? 'uname -r' will give you the version.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-21
You could either add it to your desktop session so it runs after you log in. Or put it in /etc/init.d/ then you'll need to use "update-rc.d" to tell it when it should run during startup. Not sure what runlevel to give it though. erunaheru might be able to help more.

---

### Post by mkzimms on 2008-03-21
working great now, anyone else in need here you go...

i created the script called "wireless_fix.sh" and dropped it into /etc/init.d/

```

#!/bin/bash

modprobe -r b44
modprobe -r b43
modprobe -r ssb
modprobe -r ndiswrapper
modprobe ndiswrapper
modprobe b44

```

```

sudo chmod 755 wireless_fix.sh
sudo update-rc.d wireless_fix.sh defaults

```

defaults will start at run levels 2345, and stop the service in run levels 016.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-21
> **mkzimms said:**
> yeah... that worked for all this morning but stopped working again when I got home from work.  grrrr

Heh, hate it when that happens. I didn't suggest using defaults because I'm not sure at what point the modules you're removing initially get added to the kernel. My suggestion would be to try blacklisting all of them (including ndiswrapper) and then just modprobe ndiswrapper and b44 in the order you want in your boot script. From what I understand all the blacklist does is prevent the modules from being loaded automatically. If you manually load them they should still work correctly. I'm not 100% positive on that but give it a try and see what happens!

### Post by mkzimms on 2008-03-22
>  I've been on Dell and there's no drivers for this model of laptop for linux so i'm not sure where this leaves me. Thanks for any help...

the ndiswrapper makes use of windows kernel API to allow your windows driver to run natively in linux ... i believe the correct driver for your computer is R151519.EXE

you should be able to find the correct one here...
[http://support.dell.com/support/downloads/devices.aspx?c=us&l=en&s=gen&SystemID=LATITUDE%20D620&os=WW1&osl=EN](http://support.dell.com/support/downloads/devices.aspx?c=us&l=en&s=gen&SystemID=LATITUDE%20D620&os=WW1&osl=EN)
under the network catagory - Wireless (US) WLAN Card

the procedure should be the same.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-23
> **mkzimms said:**
> Yup, that was it.  Ran through the tutorial again and now its working like a champ!  Finally i can get off the floor next too the network jack and go sit on my couch!  :biggrin:

If you had a kernel update then you would have needed to recompile the kernel modules for the new kernel. I would assume that's what happened. So every time there's a kernel update you'll need to:
```
sudo -s
modprobe -r ndiswrapper
ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
cd *blah/ndiswrapper-1.52/*
make clean
make uninstall
make
make install
cd *blah/R151519/*DRIVER/
ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
modprobe ndiswrapper
exit
```
You could make a script to do it if Hardy is still going through lots of kernel upgrades!

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-23
> **mikepry said:**
> First off I am really glad to see so many people trying to help each other out here! I am totally new to UBUNTU and have it running exclusively on a Dell desktop as well as my iMac via VMWare fusion. I am really into the whole Ubuntu concept and my son is as well and we installed Ubuntu on his Dell Inspiron B120. So, befor I go any further, can I use this tutorial to enable the Dell 1390 Wireless card with Ubuntu?

I tried with zero success and am really ignorant to Linux and command lines in general but want to learn. It is daunting when there are over 100 pages on this post and my eyes start to glaze over! I know this sounds really stupid but how do I open a file from WITHIN a directory? And to add to my confusion, I don't know where or how to put a file into a directory via terminal. 

Where I ran into this was the section after "REBOOT NOW." If anyone could help I and my son as well would really appreciate it.

Thank you in advance.

Mike Pry

Hi Mike and welcome!

This howto is written for the Dell 1390 WLAN, so yes it will work! I'm unsure what exactly the issue is. If you use the wget commands as listed in the howto, then the files that are downloaded are stored in whatever directory you're currently in. If you've just opened a terminal this will be your user's home directory. This means you shouldn't have to move anything around using the command line. Simply follow the commands listed in the howto. The only time you'll need to change anything, is in the cd (change directory) commands. 

If you've unzipped the Windows driver in your home directory all you'll need to do is "cd DRIVER" before running "ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf"

Similarly if you've untarred the ndiswrapper source in your home directory all you'll need is "cd ndiswrapper-1.52".

As for "opening a file from within a directory," I assume you mean opening a file from the command line (terminal)? It would depend entirely on what the file you're trying to open is. Being in a certain directory only affects how specific you need to be with the path to the file you want. For example: if the file is in your current working directory, simply running "*programname filename*" will suffice. If it's in another directory you may need to be more specific, eg "*programname /full/path/to/filename*."

I hope that cleared things up a bit. If you're still having issues, you could post the whole output from your attempt here for us to analyse. Please wrap it in "code" tags however (use the # in the toolbar for advanced edit), so it's easier to read!

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-23
> **cashman286 said:**
> Will I need to download anything besides the .EXE file from Dell? And can I download those other files on Windows and burn them onto a CD? Or must I used a wired connection on Ubuntu first? Because I don't think I will be allowed to hook my laptop up to the modem.

You'll need the Dell .EXE, ndiswrapper-1.52.tag.gz and you'll also need to install the build-essential packages from somewhere. The first will be easy enough to download and copy across to your computer. The easiest way to do the last is probably to use the LiveCD. Open up a terminal and run:```
sudo apt-cdrom add
# you'll be prompted for a cd, 
# make sure the Gutsy LiveCD is in the drive then hit enter.
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install build-essential
```

Then you should be right to follow the how-to! You'll just need to remember where you put the the executable and the tarball and make sure you're in the right directories whilst you're trying to extract them.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-23

Thanks for that. Now to get the ATI Radeon X1270 drivers playing nicely....I wonder if ndiswrapper will help with that?!

ndiswrapper won't help with your graphical driver. It's only for network interfaces since that's all [NDIS](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NDIS) is for. I would have thought you'd just need fglrx?
```
sudo apt-get install xorg-driver-fglrx
```

Anyway, this definitely isn't the place for this discussion!

### Post by lex on 2008-03-24
I stumbled on that be it a happy accident but is there a way to make it (the wireless) come on when I boot up?  
Hi, I was just reading through the pages looking for help for myself and I noticed that somewhere in there first page or two. that someone had added an extra command so that it would load at start up. I'll see if I can find it again.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-24
> **mikepry said:**
> Hey, thanks for the great help! I'm almost there now. I got everything working, kind of. It doesn't come on at the time of boot so I have to go into terminal and enter "sudo modprobe ndiswrapper" and then it works fine. I stumbled on that be it a happy accident but is there a way to make it (the wireless) come on when I boot up?  Kind of a hassle to do that every time. Thanks so much for your time and viva le Ubuntu!
Mike

No problems! The command modprobe is used to load a module (driver) into the kernel. So you're effectively loading the driver every time you start. The way to get around this is to tell the kernel that it should load this driver when it boots up, to do this you need to add ndiswrapper to the /etc/modules text file. You can do this with any old text editor, but you need to be root to have permission to edit the file. You can open it by running your text editor as root (gksudo gedit) or you can use a command that will append the line you want to the end of the file. You'll find this is the route that most of us who help out in the forums take as it means you simply have to copy and paste one line of code so there can't be any errors!

All explanations aside, the command that'll do this for you is:
```
echo "ndiswrapper" | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
```
If you're curious as to how the command works I gave an explanation somewhere earlier in this thread:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3666276#post3666276](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3666276#post3666276)

---

Whatever directory you were in when you ran```
tar xvf ndiswrapper-1.52.tar.gz
```will now have a subdirectory called ndiswrapper-1.52. So go into that directory```
cd ndiswrapper-1.52
```then run all the make commands.

You'll notice in your output above that when you attempted to change to a directory that didn't exist, bash tried to tell you there was an issue:```
hentai@hentai-laptop:~$ cd YOUR-NDISWRAPPER-DIRECTORY
_bash: cd: YOUR-NDISWRAPPER-DIRECTORY: No such file or directory_
```

If i type in 
hentai@hentai-laptop:~$ cd R174292

hentai@hentai-laptop:~$ 
I'm not sure how this directory thing works.
before did you just write cd then the name of the file eg
cd ndiswrapper-1.52
to get the directory?
What do I for a .exe file?
When I look in the home folder I can see 3 folders; Driverr JPN, Driver_row and Driver_US.
But I cant see R174292 anywhere.
How can I find out where it has gone?
Thanks

It inflates it in the same folder you were in. If you watch the output of the ndiswrapper extraction you'll see it looks like this:
```
ndiswrapper-1.52/
ndiswrapper-1.52/AUTHORS
ndiswrapper-1.52/ChangeLog
ndiswrapper-1.52/INSTALL
ndiswrapper-1.52/Makefile
ndiswrapper-1.52/README
ndiswrapper-1.52/ndiswrapper.spec
ndiswrapper-1.52/ndiswrapper.8
ndiswrapper-1.52/loadndisdriver.8
ndiswrapper-1.52/utils/
ndiswrapper-1.52/utils/Makefile
ndiswrapper-1.52/utils/ndiswrapper
ndiswrapper-1.52/utils/loadndisdriver.c
ndiswrapper-1.52/utils/ndiswrapper-buginfo
ndiswrapper-1.52/driver/
ndiswrapper-1.52/driver/divdi3.c
.
.
.
```
As you can see, the directory ndiswrapper-1.52/ is extracted from the tarball and everything else is extracted within that folder.

If you read the unzip output, there isn't a root directory extracted. There are a couple of directories extracted (DRIVER_JPN, DRIVER_US, x64) and a bunch of single files.

However, if you look at what johnno31 writes on the previous page. R174292.EXE is _NOT_ the one you want. You want R174291.EXE for this card. You'll notice that the .EXE you have only contains bcmwl6.inf in the DRIVER_ folders after extraction. I think this is the Vista driver, which won't work at all! So (assuming ndiswrapper is installed) try running:
```
wget http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R174291.EXE
unzip -a R174291.EXE -d R174291
cd R174291/DRIVER
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
```
Then pick up with the how-to.

device (14E4:4311) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)

It's equally valid to call a piece of "hardware" a "device"... :p

It's working fine, that's the output you should be seeing! I'm currently using ndiswrapper-1.52 with the driver from R151519.EXE which works great! So wipe out all of your ndiswrapper install (as is listed at the start of the how-to) and run it again using those two. Then you should be fine!

### Post by mikepry on 2008-03-24
> **jw5801 said:**
> No problems! The command modprobe is used to load a module (driver) into the kernel. So you're effectively loading the driver every time you start. The way to get around this is to tell the kernel that it should load this driver when it boots up, to do this you need to add ndiswrapper to the /etc/modules text file. You can do this with any old text editor, but you need to be root to have permission to edit the file. You can open it by running your text editor as root (gksudo gedit) or you can use a command that will append the line you want to the end of the file. You'll find this is the route that most of us who help out in the forums take as it means you simply have to copy and paste one line of code so there can't be any errors!

All explanations aside, the command that'll do this for you is:
```
echo "ndiswrapper" | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
```
If you're curious as to how the command works I gave an explanation somewhere earlier in this thread:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3666276#post3666276](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3666276#post3666276)

Worked like a charm! Thanks so much for your kind help.
Mike

### Post by jodylove on 2008-03-24
I know that a piece of hardware can be called a device, but there are two reasons why I said that it wasn'r working correctly.  First, when I had it fixed before I got the specific message that my hardware had been detected. ### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-24
> **jodylove said:**
> I know that a piece of hardware can be called a device, but there are two reasons why I said that it wasn'r working correctly.  First, when I had it fixed before I got the specific message that my hardware had been detected.  Secondly, I tried to continue the process, but when I typed $modprobe ndiswrapper$ my wireless was no longer recognized.  Also, when it was recognized it wasn't WLAN0...instead it was eth1.

If the interface wasn't eth1 last time, you weren't using ndiswrapper for it. The correct output to ndiswrapper -l should look like this:
```
jw5801@jw5801:~$ ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : driver installed
        device (14E4:4311) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)
```

Which is what you have. So if it's being recognised as eth1 then it is working. What makes you say that it isn't?

Hate to burst your bubble, but that's not what it should look like if you're using ndiswrapper. In fact that's the output you'd get using bcm43xx which you were supposed to blacklist if you followed this how-to.

Looking at your other output:
> hentai@hentai-laptop:~$ wget [http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R174291.EXE](http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R174291.EXE)
--11:34:48--  [http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R174291.EXE](http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R174291.EXE)
           => `R174291.EXE.1'
Resolving ftp.us.dell.com... 143.166.170.10
Connecting to ftp.us.dell.com|143.166.170.10|:80... connected.
HTTP request sent, awaiting response... 200 OK
Length: 94,233,152 (90M) [application/octet-stream]

100%[====================================>] 94,233,152   281.95K/s    ETA 00:00

11:40:15 (282.34 KB/s) - `R174291.EXE.1' saved [94233152/94233152]

wget saved the file you want as R174291.EXE.1. Read what bash is telling you!! You'll also notice below that, that you didn't unzip anything, since you tried to unzip a file that didn't exist! You need to read at least some of the output that you get from entering these commands, because if you see things like "file/directory does not exist" it means something isn't right and you should stop to work out what it is. Enter commands one at a time, and make sure they work before continuing!

So, go back to the directory you were in and run:
```
unzip -a R174291.EXE.1 -d R174291
sudo rmmod bcm43xx #don't worry if this reports an error
echo blacklist bcm43xx | sudo tee -a /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl6
cd R174291/DRIVER
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
echo ndiswrapper | sudo tee -a /etc/modules
```

Then reboot, and when you come back up it should be working.
Remember, enter those commands *one at a time* and if anything complains, reports errors or tells you it can't find something, stop, and try and figure out why!

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-24
> **jodylove said:**
> When I typed code $ndiswrapper -m$ and $modprobe ndiswrapper$ the eth1 was no longer there.  However, when I did receive the message "hardware detected" in the past, this problem did not occur.  I tried rebooting, but then eth1 was not seen at all.  Also, even when it did see it for a moment, it did not see my essid, and the access point was either invalid or not-associated.  Like I said, I had it working.  I wish I had written it down.  Have any suggestions of other drivers that I might try?  And why is it listing bcm4311 as the alternate driver when I specifically blacklisted it in /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist?

It always lists that, because it's hardwired into ndiswrapper to inform you of the alternative open source driver. If you reboot and come back and eth1 isn't present, that's because the ndiswrapper module isn't loaded. Running ```
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
``` will fix that. I've posted this already in the last day, if you'd noticed. The message about forcing parameters is **not** a good one. You shouldn't see messages like that.

Simply installing the driver will not make you connect to your SSID, iwconfig will not list your SSID, because you haven't told it that it should connect to your SSID! If your eth1 interface is up use network manager or something similar to try and connect.

If that's not working, please post the outputs of:
```
ifconfig eth1
ndiswrapper -l
iwlist scan
```

---

Simply installing the driver will not make you connect to your SSID, iwconfig will not list your SSID, because you haven't told it that it should connect to your SSID! If your eth1 interface is up use network manager or something similar to try and connect.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-25
ifup only brings an interface up, it won't be able to do anything with the interface. Take a look at [this thread](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=571188) for some info on conecting to a network via the command line. The program you're describing would be network-manager, I don't know of a command line method of accessing network-manager.

You could try ```
sudo -s
ifconfig eth1 up
iwconfig eth1 essid "jody"
dhclient eth1
```

If your network is unencrypted that will tell it to try and establish a connection using DHCP (automatically getting an IP). Is "iwlist scan" showing your Access Point?

Then when I typed $iwconfig eth1$, I got a listing of things, but none of them included my essid.  I think they were something like "essid off/any."  As I am blind it is difficult for me to post the exact code.  I'm currently using my alternate pc in windows.

Also, last time I had this working, there is a network click item next to the clock from where you can connect to a network.  However, the speech program (orca) included in Linux is unable to access this.  Is there a command I can run from terminal to connect directly to my network?  $ifup$ does not seem to do this.

There would be no reason if everything were done properly, which most likely isn't the case.  Reboot, then immediately do a 
```
sudo cat /etc/modules | grep ndis 
sudo cat /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist | grep bcm
sudo ndiswrapper -l
sudo lsmod | grep ndis
```
and post the output for each one.

That icon you're referring to is executed from the command line with "nm-applet".  It's GUI only, though.  You'll have to use the wireless tools such as iwconfig if you want to use the command-line to setup essid, security, etc.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-25
That all looks ok to me. What makes you think it's not working?

You appear to be connected via your wired connection (eth0), so disconnect that, then you'll need to use something like network manager to connect to your access point. It won't do it automatically, you need to tell it which ESSID to connect to and what the WEP key for the network is.

---

### Post by upthelum on 2008-03-25
Duh.

Went into network tools, added my wep key and i am now 100% head over heels in love with Linux. Thanks a ton you dont know what this means it's so cool.

This is what i like about Ubuntu its such a neat place.

### Post by jodylove on 2008-03-26
> **paperdiesel said:**
> There would be no reason if everything were done properly, which most likely isn't the case.  Reboot, then immediately do a 
```
sudo cat /etc/modules | grep ndis 
sudo cat /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist | grep bcm
sudo ndiswrapper -l
sudo lsmod | grep ndis
```
and post the output for each one.

That icon you're referring to is executed from the command line with "nm-applet".  It's GUI only, though.  You'll have to use the wireless tools such as iwconfig if you want to use the command-line to setup essid, security, etc.


What is the straight line between the first thing and grep?  My speech says "vertical bar."  How is this typed?  I don't see a key combo for it on the keyboard.

---

### Post by paperdiesel on 2008-03-26
> **jodylove said:**
> What is the straight line between the first thing and grep?  My speech says "vertical bar."  How is this typed?  I don't see a key combo for it on the keyboard.

In console terms, it's commonly referred-to as a "pipe", and can usually be found on the same key as the backslash (usually above the return key).  

It's probably best that you just copy / paste those lines into your terminal to avoid typos and confusion.  Then copy / paste their outputs to this forum.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-03-29
What about ```
lsmod | grep bcm43xx
```

Given you have a wlan0 interface and no eth1 interface, it would appear as though you've forgotten to blacklist bcm43xx and this module is preventing ndiswrapper from working.

### Post by chadeldridge on 2008-03-30
I found out my issue was with the drivers I installed with ndiswrapper and not the wrapper itself.  If you have a m1710 xps dell then use the attached drivers and things will work again at 54mb.
