### Post by Stephen Webber on 2008-04-05
New edit, new issue.

Laptop: Dell Latitude D531 (but the directions have been working)
OS: Mandriva 2008.0...

Here's what's going on:

```

[root@localhost ~]# cd DRIVER
[root@localhost DRIVER]# ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
installing bcmwl5 ...
[root@localhost DRIVER]# ndiswrapper -l
bcmwl5 : driver installed
        device (14E4:4311) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)
[root@localhost DRIVER]# modprobe ndiswrapper
FATAL: Module ndiswrapper not found.
[root@localhost DRIVER]# 

```

How do I fix the fact that I'm getting a fatal error?

---

### Post by pingnak on 2008-04-07
If there are any spaces in your path to the ndiswrapper source code, it will fail to build, citing piece of your path.  If it was something descriptive, like 'Wireless', like mine was, this can send you on quite a snipe hunt.

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-04-07
> **pingnak said:**
> If there are any spaces in your path to the ndiswrapper source code, it will fail to build, citing piece of your path.  If it was something descriptive, like 'Wireless', like mine was, this can send you on quite a snipe hunt.

Hmm... that's probably something the writers of ndiswrapper should know about. I'd let them know by filing a [bug](http://ndiswrapper.sourceforge.net/joomla/index.php?/component/option,com_openwiki/Itemid,33/id,bugs/) [report](http://sourceforge.net/tracker/?atid=604450&group_id=93482&func=browse).

### Post by anderwm on 2008-04-07
wireless card:BCM94311MCG wlan mini-PCI(rev01)
PCI ID:14e4:4311
Laptop: Dell Inspiron e1505

After playing with the native drivers for a while I have tried this tutorial.  The result is that my device is recognized and the wireless light comes on(in fact I can achieve these results with the native drivers as well).  However, in network manager no networks show up (there are several there and broadcasting in windows).  If I type in my ssid(no security for this test) and try to connect the Network-Manager icon whirls around like it is trying then just returns to normal with no message (not connected).  I should also mention that if i use command line code
```
sudo iwlist scanning
```
it also finds no networks.
I am using the hardy heron beta and am probably not linux advanced enough to Beta.  However, since it is a fresh ndiswrapper install I feel it shouldn't matter.  I am downloading Gutsy now and will try it if suggested.
  
Does anyone still monitor this thread and have any ideas?

  I would have (and still will upon request) provided more command prompt information about the install, but from reading about 40 pages of replies it sounds like if I get the light and Network Manager sees wireless the install went well.

As a side note I am impressed and astounded at the never ending patience and understanding of j5801 and paperdiesel.  I bet I read 100 posts about invalid driver/wrong directory and the response was never angry.

  I would have (and still will upon request) provided more command prompt information about the install, but from reading about 40 pages of replies it sounds like if I get the light and Network Manager sees wireless the install went well.

As a side note I am impressed and astounded at the never ending patience and understanding of j5801 and paperdiesel.  I bet I read 100 posts about invalid driver/wrong directory and the response was never angry.

Well since you asked so nicely :cool:.

Do a sudo iwlist scanning, then immediately do a dmesg and sudo cat /var/log/syslog, and post the relevant rows from those two commands (most likely the rows at the bottom).

---

Because you're using Hardy there is a different native driver for this card, it's now called b43. The problem you're seeing here is that native module interfering with ndiswrapper.

Have a look at [this](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=297092&page=121) page for some workarounds. Once Hardy is officially released I'll upgrade and I'm sure at that point either myself or paperdiesel will endeavour to provide a new Hardy how-to!

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-04-08
> **khaliln said:**
> I am also a new user to Ubuntu, running 7.10 on a Compaq Presario C500.  

I know the Dell drivers may not function with my Compaq, but cannot at all find any support for my laptop, in fact I have found people say that this how-to actually worked for them! But I am stuck on Step 3.  When I insert the 'sudo make uninstall' command, I get 'make: *** No rule to make target `uninstall'.  Stop.' 

I think I misinterpreted this, and continued on to Step 4, unzipping R151517.EXE, is that a problem?  Since my ndiswrapper was unzipped in my home directory, I simply continued (did not change directories or anything) and when I ran 'sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf' it didn't work saying

'sudo: ndiswrapper: command not found'

Maybe because ndiswrapper was not compiled in Step 3? I am grateful to those who have assisted in this how-to and hope I can get some, too! 

Oh, and kind of moving ahead a little bit, but how do I get to my driver directory? Is it simply 'cd DRIVERS' command? :S Sorry I am quite the newbie.

It's exam time, and I HATE having to use Vista on campus to get wireless Internet access.  Thanks in advance!

You are correct, you weren't in the correct folder when you were trying to compile (and remove any left over parts), thus nothing happened so everything else will fail. You need to use the "cd" command to change into the directory you extracted. When you run "tar xvf ndiswrapper-1.52.tar.gz" you'll see some output, listing what was extracted, in this instance every file shown has the same root directory (ndiswrapper-1.52/), so you'll know that you need to type "cd ndiswrapper-1.52" before you continue. The "ls" command will show you the contents of your current directory and you can also use the tab key to autocomplete any commands or directories as you're typing, if autocomplete can find it then it means you'lre in the right place!

As for the DRIVER directory, it's simply a subdirectory that is extracted from R151517.EXE. So once you extract that you should notice a directory called "DRIVER" in whatever directory you're in (note that "DRIVER" is completely different to "driver").

### Post by hmbuhler on 2008-04-12
princevaolorum:

i'll assume that in the terminal window you are at a username~$ prompt.

type ls 
and you'll see what is in that directory.

if you see ndiswrapper-1.52 in blue, then that directory is present.

type cd ndiswrapper-1.52
you'll then be at username-ndiswrapper-1.52~/nidswrapper-1.52$

type ls
and you should see all the files and directories listed within it.

Can you post the "litany of errors" here? It's most likely that you don't have the build-essential package installed. So if you're connected to the internet via a wired connection or other means, ```
sudo aptitude install build-essential
``` should fix the problem.

### Post by don.clark on 2008-04-15

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-04-15
> **don.clark said:**
> Will this how to work for 8.04 or is the wireless problem fixed with 8.04.  Will there be a difference between Ubuntu and Kubuntu 8.04 for wireless connections? In version 7.04 and 7.10 Ubuntu seems to be easier to configure than Kubuntu.

No this will not work for 8.04. There is some discussion on getting it to work at around page 120. I will be releasing a Hardy How-to after the official release of Hardy when I've had a chance to thoroughly test it.

The only difference between Ubuntu & Kubuntu is the graphical interface and default applications. So the only difference you'll see is the default network manager, which you can uninstall and change anyway, if you're so inclined. Under the hood they are identical.

Nothing should be output when you do the blacklist command, but that doesn't mean it didn't do anything. If nothing is output that's good. ```
lspci
``` is a terminal command which outputs all the pci hardware you have (including your wireless chipset)

The reason it's not working is because you're using the wrong driver. If memory serves that's the Vista drivers (which don't work). You should use the drivers linked in the howto, or [this one](http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R151519.EXE), which is the newer version of the driver linked in the how-to.

So undo everything you've done and try again using that driver. Following the how-to properly should do both of these.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-04-17
> **Moneyless said:**
> Thanks... ^_^ '

How do I undo everything I did? Would it be easier if I just re-installed Mint?

You don't need to undo absolutely everything you did. Basically just uninstall the driver and install the right one.
```
sudo modprobe -r ndiswrapper
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl5
ndiswrapper -l
```
Verify that the last command doesn't show anything else installed. Then make sure you have the right driver in the directory you're in (so most likely your home directory) and do:
```
unzip -a R151519.EXE -d R151519/
cd R151519/DRIVER/
sudo ndiswrapper -i bcmwl5.inf
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper
```

And then if nothing bad happened your wifi light should come on. If it's still not working, copy and paste the output and what you entered and put it here in [code] tags.

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-04-18
lo is your local or loopback interface, so it will never support scanning! The interface you want to look at is "eth1". My "ndiswrapper -l" output looks like this:
```
bcmwl5 : driver installed
        device (14E4:4311) present (alternate driver: bcm43xx)
```

If you don't get the second line, run: ```
lspci -nn
``` and you should see a line that looks (approximately) like this:
```
05:00.0 Network controller [0280]: Broadcom Corporation BCM94311MCG wlan mini-PCI [14e4:4311] (rev 01)
```
Most of it doesn't have to be precise, the important part is the [14E4:4311] at the end. If that's not there then either you don't have this particular wireless, or something has gone wrong somewhere other than in ndiswrapper. If you can't see it, post the whole output up here.

If that's there, it's possible you used the wrong driver. I notice you have both R151517.EXE and R174291.EXE in your home directory. Which did you use?

Try toggling the WiFi button, or playing with it in the bios. That seems like the most likely thing that's happened. I know if I toggle the WiFi off then reboot without toggling it back on, it won't be recognised at all and I'll have to reset it in the bios to get it to work again. My cat jumped on my laptop once and toggled it, freaked me out for about an hour before I worked it out, thought the cat had broken my wireless card!

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-04-19
Yep, you need a different driver! According to the thread [here](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty_No-Fluff) (search for your lspci line and you'll get to the bit I'm referencing), you need this driver:
[ftp://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R174291.exe](ftp://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R174291.exe)

So give it a try with that one and see what happens.

EDIT: Apparently that download is rather large, so if you want a smaller one, get this one:
[http://myspamb8.googlepages.com/R174291-pruned.zip](http://myspamb8.googlepages.com/R174291-pruned.zip)

### Post by amirman on 2008-04-26
if anybody is reading this and just wants their wireless to work, it's very easy. i use a dell e1505 with the broadcom wirelss mini card and this has worked perfectly on many occasions.

first i must note that this is in the new 8.04 version of ubuntu, i don't know if it works on older versions but it should.

go to: 'system > administration > software sources' in your menu

click the checkboxes beside both of the 'third-party' sources to enable them

click the checkboxes on the next tab for 'proposed' and 'backports'

*you will need to use a LAN cable to hook up your laptop to your router temporarily until your wireless card driver is installed.*

something should come up and tell you that you need to update some list. go ahead and do that.

make sure to get all updates through the little updater thing that pops up on your 'notification area'

then go to: 'system > administration > hardware drivers' in your menu.

your new wireless driver should be ready to download and/or install

this will require a reboot to work after it's done

this method is awesome not only because it's easy but this will also cure a lot of other hardware problems you may have.

my dell came with the ATI mobolity radeon x1400 which i've spent many hours trying to get to work with all kinds of complicated terminal tweaks with no success. but the hardware drivers menu had a driver available for my graphics card as well which works amazingly.

have fun.

:guitar:

---

my dell came with the ATI mobolity radeon x1400 which i've spent many hours trying to get to work with all kinds of complicated terminal tweaks with no success. but the hardware drivers menu had a driver available for my graphics card as well which works amazingly.

have fun.

:guitar:

I have the dell 1390 wlan and for some reason i can not checkmark the box. Any ideas?

### Post by don.clark on 2008-05-09
> **amirman said:**
> if anybody is reading this and just wants their wireless to work, it's very easy. i use a dell e1505 with the broadcom wirelss mini card and this has worked perfectly on many occasions.

first i must note that this is in the new 8.04 version of ubuntu, i don't know if it works on older versions but it should.

go to: 'system > administration > software sources' in your menu

click the checkboxes beside both of the 'third-party' sources to enable them

click the checkboxes on the next tab for 'proposed' and 'backports'

*you will need to use a LAN cable to hook up your laptop to your router temporarily until your wireless card driver is installed.*

something should come up and tell you that you need to update some list. go ahead and do that.

make sure to get all updates through the little updater thing that pops up on your 'notification area'

then go to: 'system > administration > hardware drivers' in your menu.

your new wireless driver should be ready to download and/or install

this will require a reboot to work after it's done

this method is awesome not only because it's easy but this will also cure a lot of other hardware problems you may have.

my dell came with the ATI mobolity radeon x1400 which i've spent many hours trying to get to work with all kinds of complicated terminal tweaks with no success. but the hardware drivers menu had a driver available for my graphics card as well which works amazingly.

have fun.

:guitar:

I did this and the wireless began working but is really really slow.  So slow it won't load some pages.  Where do you tell to change from 1M to 54.

Don

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-05-09
> **don.clark said:**
> I did this and the wireless began working but is really really slow.  So slow it won't load some pages.  Where do you tell to change from 1M to 54.

Don

That's why we use ndiswrapper :P

Those steps are completely different to this how-to and will load the b43 firmware, which is slower and in most cases less stable (in my opinion). If you'd like to load ndiswrapper in hardy, have a look at my how-to here:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=760568](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=760568)

---

DMESG:
35.700677] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMRegisterInterruptEx'

[   35.700689] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMIndicateStatusEx'

[   35.700697] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisFreeNetBufferList'

[   35.700704] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisAllocateMdl'

[   35.700712] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisFreeMdl'

[   35.700732] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMDeregisterScatterGatherDma'

[   35.700742] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMDeregisterInterruptEx'

[   35.700754] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMSynchronizeWithInterruptEx'

[   35.700762] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMRegisterMiniportDriver'

[   35.700770] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMSetMiniportAttributes'

[   35.700781] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMGetBusData'

[   35.700789] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMRegisterScatterGatherDma'

[   35.700799] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisAllocateNetBufferListPool'

[   35.700811] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMOidRequestComplete'

[   35.700819] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisAllocateIoWorkItem'

[   35.700826] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMSetBusData'

[   35.700834] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisAllocateNetBufferAndNetBufferList'

[   35.700849] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMDeregisterMiniportDriver'

[   35.700863] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisFreeNetBufferListPool'

[   35.700872] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisOpenConfigurationEx'

[   35.700879] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisFreeIoWorkItem'

[   35.700887] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMSendNetBufferListsComplete'

[   35.700895] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisMIndicateReceiveNetBufferLists'

[   35.700906] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisAllocateMemoryWithTagPriority'

[   35.700917] ndiswrapper (import:242): unknown symbol: NDIS.SYS:'NdisQueueIoWorkItem'

[   35.700920] ndiswrapper (load_sys_files:210): couldn't prepare driver 'bcmwl6'

[   35.702090] ndiswrapper (load_wrap_driver:112): couldn't load driver bcmwl6; check system log for messages from 'loadndisdriver'
```

Please use 'code' tags for long outputs like that! bcmwl6 is not what you want. If you have a look at dmesg you'll see ndiswrapper complaining about it alot. You want bcmwl5 (as stated in the how-to). So remove bcmwl6 and try again:
```
sudo ndiswrapper -e bcmwl6
```

### Post by Moneyless on 2008-05-13
I did what you show in your other How-To and now it works, but parcially. I can view wireless networks but whenever I try to join one it just keeps asking me for my WEP key then eventually just stops...?

---

### Post by jw5801 on 2008-05-13
> **Moneyless said:**
> I did what you show in your other How-To and now it works, but parcially. I can view wireless networks but whenever I try to join one it just keeps asking me for my WEP key then eventually just stops...?

Posting in the other How-To is probably the best idea.

Try taking down the WEP encryption (just for a second) and see if you can connect to an unencrypted network. If you can, it's almost certainly a network manager issue rather than a driver issue.

### Post by AiBo on 2008-05-15
Fizz,

Don't know if it will help, I just did a fresh install of 8.04 and was trying to follow this guide and had the same problem.  In the end I went back to the BIOS and turned the wi-fi off saved, re-booted, turned it on again, re-booted and then completely confused, I let it boot into windows (i have a dual boot machine), the machine had not booted into windows for almost 6 months, from there I used my function key to activate the wi-fi, light comes on!!!  Then I rebooted again, back into Ubuntu, and the light stayed on!  No idea, but maybe it will work for you ... then I followed this guide:  [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty_No-Fluff](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/WifiDocs/Driver/bcm43xx/Feisty_No-Fluff)

5 mins. later wi-fi is active and working great.  Good luck.  Sometimes it really seems like voodoo science that seems to work?!!

---

### Post by RSingh on 2008-06-02
After upgrading to 8.04 Hardy Heron, I did not require this How-TO anymore because it seems that the restricted drivers manager has taken care of it. My Wifi driver was automatically installed by it and it works perfectly.

Yet I would still like to thank the author for the How-To as it really helped in the older releases.

---

### Post by tylerisfat on 2008-06-03
really? on a dell 1505?

---

### Post by RSingh on 2008-06-03
Yes, I have the Dell 1505 laptop. After Installing 8.04, It automatically notified me of "restricted driver availability", and thus I went to the restricted drivers manager and clicked on enable on my hardware and followed the installation procedure. It automatically configured everything and now the wireless light turns on and it detect and connects perfectly.

---

### Post by tylerisfat on 2008-06-04
why the heck aren't i getting those messages?

---

### Post by RSingh on 2008-06-04
Its okay if you are not getting the message. Just go to : System ">> Administration >> Restricted Drivers Manager" and click enable in front of your hardware.

---

### Post by fearailhold on 2008-06-26
I've done my best to skim through the pages of this thread to ensure that a problem like mine hasn't already been discussed, and I'm fairly sure that it hasn't.

I am using a Dell Latitude D820, which uses the same Wireless Broadcom 1390 Driver as the Inspiron E1505 (i.e., following the links for the D820 at support.dell.com leads to the same file as the one posted here). Also, a call to "lspci -nn | grep 14e4" yields
```
0c:00.0 Network controller [0280]: Broadcom Corporation BCM94311MCG wlan mini-PCI [14e4:4311] (rev 01)
```
which appears to be the correct card. **Note:** I am using Ubuntu 8.04 - Hardy Heron.

I followed the guide at the beginning of this thread with no problems, but after restarting my laptop I found that the wireless had stopped working. After ensuring that I had correctly typed "ndiswrapper" on its own line in /etc/modules, I tried removing the b43, ssb, and ndiswrapper modules (using rmmod) and then re-adding the ndiswrapper module (using modprobe). Upon doing this, the wireless started working again. Unfortunately, this happens after every reboot. I added "blacklist b43" and "blacklist ssb" (each to its own line) in /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist and restarted but to no avail. It seems that b43 is successfully being blacklisted, but for some reason ssb continues to load. Indeed, upon fresh reboot (i.e., before executing any of the aforementioned commands), a call to "dmesg | grep b43" yields nothing, and a call to "dmesg | grep ssb" returns the following:

```
[   15.061715] ssb: Core 0 found: ChipCommon (cc 0x800, rev 0x11, vendor 0x4243)
[   15.061743] ssb: Core 1 found: IEEE 802.11 (cc 0x812, rev 0x0A, vendor 0x4243)
[   15.061765] ssb: Core 2 found: USB 1.1 Host (cc 0x817, rev 0x03, vendor 0x4243)
[   15.061786] ssb: Core 3 found: PCI-E (cc 0x820, rev 0x01, vendor 0x4243)
[   17.432373] ssb: Sonics Silicon Backplane found on PCI device 0000:0c:00.0

```

It appears that for some reason, ssb is starting and beating ndiswrapper to the punch. Indeed, the inclusion of ndiswrapper happens at a later timestep, and has a shortened output (a call to "dmesg | grep ndiswrapper"):
```
[   34.528754] ndiswrapper version 1.53 loaded (smp=yes, preempt=no)
[   32.242418] usbcore: registered new interface driver ndiswrapper
```

Below is my /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist file, of which the last two entries were added by me.

```
# This file lists those modules which we don't want to be loaded by
# alias expansion, usually so some other driver will be loaded for the
# device instead.

# evbug is a debug tool that should be loaded explicitly
blacklist evbug

# these drivers are very simple, the HID drivers are usually preferred
blacklist usbmouse
blacklist usbkbd

# replaced by e100
blacklist eepro100

# replaced by tulip
blacklist de4x5

# causes no end of confusion by creating unexpected network interfaces
blacklist eth1394

# snd_intel8x0m can interfere with snd_intel8x0, doesn't seem to support much
# hardware on its own (Ubuntu bug #2011, #6810)
blacklist snd_intel8x0m

# causes failure to suspend on HP compaq nc6000 (Ubuntu: #10306)
blacklist i2c_i801

# replaced by p54pci
blacklist prism54

# replaced by b43 and ssb.
blacklist bcm43xx

# most apps now use garmin usb driver directly (Ubuntu: #114565)
blacklist garmin_gps

# replaced by asus-laptop (Ubuntu: #184721)
blacklist asus_acpi

# inserted to fix dell wireless
blacklist ssb

# also inserted to fix dell wireless
blacklist b43

```

Also, below is my /etc/modules file, just to be sure.
```
# /etc/modules: kernel modules to load at boot time.
#
# This file contains the names of kernel modules that should be loaded
# at boot time, one per line. Lines beginning with "#" are ignored.

fuse
lp
sbp2
ndiswrapper

```

Thank you for any advice you can offer.

I followed the guide at the beginning of this thread with no problems, but after restarting my laptop I found that the wireless had stopped working. After ensuring that I had correctly typed "ndiswrapper" on its own line in /etc/modules, I tried removing the b43, ssb, and ndiswrapper modules (using rmmod) and then re-adding the ndiswrapper module (using modprobe). Upon doing this, the wireless started working again. Unfortunately, this happens after every reboot. I added "blacklist b43" and "blacklist ssb" (each to its own line) in /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist and restarted but to no avail. It seems that b43 is successfully being blacklisted, but for some reason ssb continues to load. Indeed, upon fresh reboot (i.e., before executing any of the aforementioned commands), a call to "dmesg | grep b43" yields nothing, and a call to "dmesg | grep ssb" returns the following:

```
[   15.061715] ssb: Core 0 found: ChipCommon (cc 0x800, rev 0x11, vendor 0x4243)
[   15.061743] ssb: Core 1 found: IEEE 802.11 (cc 0x812, rev 0x0A, vendor 0x4243)
[   15.061765] ssb: Core 2 found: USB 1.1 Host (cc 0x817, rev 0x03, vendor 0x4243)
[   15.061786] ssb: Core 3 found: PCI-E (cc 0x820, rev 0x01, vendor 0x4243)
[   17.432373] ssb: Sonics Silicon Backplane found on PCI device 0000:0c:00.0

```

It appears that for some reason, ssb is starting and beating ndiswrapper to the punch. Indeed, the inclusion of ndiswrapper happens at a later timestep, and has a shortened output (a call to "dmesg | grep ndiswrapper"):
```
[   34.528754] ndiswrapper version 1.53 loaded (smp=yes, preempt=no)
[   32.242418] usbcore: registered new interface driver ndiswrapper
```

Below is my /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist file, of which the last two entries were added by me.

```
# This file lists those modules which we don't want to be loaded by
# alias expansion, usually so some other driver will be loaded for the
# device instead.

# evbug is a debug tool that should be loaded explicitly
blacklist evbug

# these drivers are very simple, the HID drivers are usually preferred
blacklist usbmouse
blacklist usbkbd

# replaced by e100
blacklist eepro100

# replaced by tulip
blacklist de4x5

# causes no end of confusion by creating unexpected network interfaces
blacklist eth1394

# snd_intel8x0m can interfere with snd_intel8x0, doesn't seem to support much
# hardware on its own (Ubuntu bug #2011, #6810)
blacklist snd_intel8x0m

# causes failure to suspend on HP compaq nc6000 (Ubuntu: #10306)
blacklist i2c_i801

# replaced by p54pci
blacklist prism54

# replaced by b43 and ssb.
blacklist bcm43xx

# most apps now use garmin usb driver directly (Ubuntu: #114565)
blacklist garmin_gps

# replaced by asus-laptop (Ubuntu: #184721)
blacklist asus_acpi

# inserted to fix dell wireless

```

Also, below is my /etc/modules file, just to be sure.
```
# /etc/modules: kernel modules to load at boot time.
#
# This file contains the names of kernel modules that should be loaded
# at boot time, one per line. Lines beginning with "#" are ignored.

fuse
lp
sbp2
ndiswrapper

```

Thank you for any advice you can offer.

You are correct. In the 2.6.24 series kernels (the one used in Hardy), ssb conflicts with ndiswrapper for broadcom cards. Take a look at my how-to [here](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=760568). Basically you need to ensure that ndiswrapper is loaded before ssb, however this is difficult, as ssb is required by b44 (the module for broadcom wired cards) and b44 is in the initramfs in case you need to do a network boot.

If you're confident in dealing with modules you can stop loading b44 in the initramfs by editing /etc/initramfs-tools/initramfs.conf and changing the BOOT=eth0 line to something else, like BOOT=lo. You'll also need to blacklist b44. This means if you want to use your wired card, you'll need to manually load b44, which isn't ideal for a newer user.

The alternative is to remove b44 and ssb and reload them during boot using an initscript, which my how-to will detail. You'll just need to take a look at Step 2, since you've already installed the necessary components (make sure you remove ndiswrapper from /etc/modules however).

---

### Post by tovarunvs on 2008-08-05
DELL Vosto 1400 with Dell 1390 wire-less minicard.
Ubuntu 8.04 LTS (Hardy Heron) Live DVD from DVD Drive

My Wifi did not work on first live boot.
Notification came automatically near top corner to ugrade driver.
It tried to fetch software called 'firmware' and faied.
!!!! IT NEEDS INTERNET TO FETCH !!!!
I Connected the network cable, and INITERNET is ready !
Again launched the driver upgrade from top corner.
checked the option to fetch firware. In 3 minutes I got the sucess message. Clcked to connect to wireless and it showed my wifi name.
Gave the password but did not work. Again gave pasword and changed the option below password to HEX. It worked.

So my solution was "Just connect the Network cable" .It worked like a charm. :-)

Thanks to this thread and forum. Otherwise I may not have tried to install it.

### Post by JMac82 on 2008-09-02
paperdiesel, hope you're still checking this since you had the solution!

I also had the BUG: soft lockup CPU#0 stuck for xxs (loadndisdriver:xxxx) error like a few of the others.  tried your solution: adding noapid nolapic irqpoll to the end of defoptions and not uncommenting that, then sudo update-grub.  didn't work!  here's what I'm using:

Ubuntu Hardy Heron, ndiswrapper 1.53, dell dimension 9100, blacklisted bcm43xx & b43, Linksys wireless USB adapter WUSB54GSv2 (on the ndiswrapper accepted list), Intel Core 2 Duo ~ 2.14 GHz
uname -a = Linux jmac 2.6.24-19-generic #1 SMP Wed Jun 18 14:15:37 UTC 2008 x86_64 GNU/Linux

ndiswrapper is aliased as wlan0 in the /etc/modprobe.d/ folder, and I thought this was rather curious about the error - it only happens when:

1). ndiswrapper is NOT listed in /etc/modules file

if I put ndiswrapper in the /etc/modules file, I get a huge long error-list with a bunch of what looks like addresses, kernel/function calls, etc.  

here's a solution I've found which obviously only works for wireless USB setups: just unplug the usb adapter before selecting your default linux boot option, and replug it at the login screen, and everything works fine.  Haven't seen this listed for usb setups as a temporary "fix."  but yeah, I've seen some places that say having ndiswrapper in /etc/modules file is necessary, and it isn't on my setup.

I plan to write on the ndiswrapper forums to find out more about this...since it's the latest version.

any other solution ideas?

### Post by jackcmiller on 2008-09-14
For those whom it might help, I was able to get my Dell Inspiron 1525 wireless 1390 adapter running Ubuntu 8.0.4  running using these instructions with a couple of minor changes:

I downloaded this set of drivers:

wget [http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R173590.exe](http://ftp.us.dell.com/network/R173590.exe)

In addition to following the instructions, I had to run these commands to get the proper ndiswrapper alias in place in /etc/modprobe.d/ndiswrapper:

ndiswrapper -ma 
ndiswrapper -mi 
ndiswrapper -m  (this command didn't work for me until I did the first two)   

After that everything worked great.

Happy to be back on linux after almost 8 years!  Woot!

:guitar:

                    Extra:atim=0

eth0      Interface doesn't support scanning.


However, I cannot connect using Network Manager. I have made sure I am using the latest version.

I am running Ubuntu 8.04.1

Help!

---

### Post by Keith S. on 2008-10-23
Hello,

I have a Dell E1505 laptop cpu, and am having difficulty hooking up the Verizon USB727 Modem. I had no technical support from Dell, because my warranty ran out three weeks ago!!  I will be keeping that in mind when I purchase my next laptop. After spending countless hours on the phone with verizon, and trying three different brand new devices, the tech at verizon brought me to this conclusion.  He said "the current network on my laptop (my office laptop is the Dell E1505) is preventing the proper drivers from loading off of the setup disk.  I was told there is something configured in my device manager preventing the computer to recognize the drivers, and effectively blocking the new Verizon USB727 drivers to function.  I have spent two days trying to solve this problem.  I am hoping that someone can help me figure out how to make the laptop read the proper drivers.  can someone help me?

:confused:

Thank you,
Keith S.
[email]keiths@doctorfeinberg.com[/email]

---

### Post by shmuelu on 2008-10-26
Hi,

I had just successfully install the wireless driver.

Dell XPS M1730
Broadcom  BCM4328
AMD 64 bit UBUNTU 4.10 release candidate.

ndiswrapper 1.53 (I had to modify one file iw_ndis.c:

  in ndis_translate_scan :  I added the following code
	struct iw_request_info iw_info;
	memset (&iw_info,0,sizeof(iw_info));

    And added the &iw_info as first parameter of each call to 
	event = iwe_stream_add_event(&iw_info,event, end_buf, &iwe, IW_EV_ADDR_LEN);



Thank you,

Shmuel

---

### Post by nolajohn on 2008-11-10
Thanks. This worked despite my best attempts to mess it up! Needed an extra reboot at the end though.

---

### Post by fire7882 on 2008-12-10
Good How to but I was able to make the wireless work on my dell e1405 with Broadcom 1390 WLAN by following these steps

Installed ubuntu 8.10
Ran update manager   (System/Administration/Update Manager)
Restarted computer
Went to Hardware Drivers  (System/Administration/Hardware Drivers)
Activated Broadcom B43 wireless driver
Restarted computer

Everything works great now.  I'm a first time linux user and loving it so far.

EDIT:  BTW, This method requires you to be plugged into a network cable.  Once everything gets installed, your good to go.

---

### Post by Sulvena on 2008-12-11
> **fire7882 said:**
> Good How to but I was able to make the wireless work on my dell e1405 with Broadcom 1390 WLAN by following these steps

Installed ubuntu 8.10
Ran update manager   (System/Administration/Update Manager)
Restarted computer
Went to Hardware Drivers  (System/Administration/Hardware Drivers)
Activated Broadcom B43 wireless driver
Restarted computer

Everything works great now.  I'm a first time linux user and loving it so far.

EDIT:  BTW, This method requires you to be plugged into a network cable.  Once everything gets installed, your good to go.

This also worked for me, running an Dell e1505 Inspiron.

For the ones having problems with ndiswrapper a couple of posts up, I suspect the issue is that 8.10 is using gcc 4.3 and that's causing compilation errors. I was going to look more into it, but since the above worked, I'm not sure.

### Post by joshualef on 2008-12-24
For I have a Dell E1505 Wireless (Broadcom 1390).  

Thank you thank you thank you.  I am a Linux newbie with not much command line experience.

I had to try this method a few times before I got it tow work for Debian GNU/Linux.

A few notes for other noobs.

1)  when I ran the uninstall command in the ndiswrapper directory, I ran it more than a dozen times and I still received the message to keep uninstalling.  I decided to plug ahead and was able to get my wireless up and running.

2)  JUST IN CASE THIS IS NOT OBVIOUS--When procuring the ndiswrapper make sure you are not plugging in the version posted in this how to guide (1.51) BUT GET THE LATEST ONE.  

3)  TIP:  put the R151517.EXE file and ndiswrapper folder somewhere that is easy to access so you don't drive yourself insane trying to find the right place to 'cd' to.  I put them both in the same location, an easily accessible place

Don't give up.  It may take a few tries.  Walk away when your computer starts looking like a basketball and the window starts looking like a basketball hoop.

---

### Post by joshualef on 2008-12-25
The strange thing is that after I rebooted, I could no longer connect to a network through wireless.  The device works, but I cannot connect to a network.  There must be something I do not understand.  I went through many posts to figure it out.  I am going to start fresh and see if I can figure out the problem.

I am using Debian GNU/Linux as opposed to Ubunto where I was able avoid the ndiswrapper method.  If you have a hard line connection to the internet or an external USB wireless connection, you should be able to avoid using the ndiswrapper method for Dell Inspiron e1505 for Ubuntu.

If anyone has a suggestion, great.  Otherwise I will report back.

I would like to say that despite the difficulty in getting the wireless to behave the way I would like, I found this tutorial a great tool for beginning to understand Linux.  No doubt this is not an easy task, for is noobs, but if you are patient and embrace your OCD you will make progress.

### Post by anandjm on 2009-01-11
You seem to be having an Intel Wireless card as indicated by the following line in lspci output:

06:00.0 Network controller: Intel Corporation PRO/Wireless 3945ABG [Golan] Network Connection (rev 02)

One way to be sure is to check if your laptop has an Intel Centrino/Centrino Duo/Centrino 2 logo on it. Intel wireless should work without any additional drivers/ndiswrapper straight out of the box. 

Anand

> hi,

I have a problem after this step :

after the second command, I get this message :

### Post by PostaL cm on 2009-03-10
> **fire7882 said:**
> Good How to but I was able to make the wireless work on my dell e1405 with Broadcom 1390 WLAN by following these steps

Installed ubuntu 8.10
Ran update manager   (System/Administration/Update Manager)
Restarted computer
Went to Hardware Drivers  (System/Administration/Hardware Drivers)
Activated Broadcom B43 wireless driver
Restarted computer

Everything works great now.  I'm a first time linux user and loving it so far.

EDIT:  BTW, This method requires you to be plugged into a network cable.  Once everything gets installed, your good to go.

This method actually worked for me..! My brother broke his WAN wired port, so I was relying on my desktop and a flash drive. I fought with it for hours until I remembered I had Belkin USB wifi adapter, so I slapped in. Well, following the instructions at the beginning of he thread actually wasn't working for me. It kept giving me arguements during the installs. I about pulled my hair out until I came across this post. After copying this, it is working perfectly! Awesome. Great find :popcorn:

---

### Post by sanemanmad on 2009-03-24
Hi ~ 

I wanted to post here and point out, that while all of you may now be able to use your wireless cards... you are circumventing Ubuntu's capabilities altogether. I just finished loading Hardy 8.04 on a lady friend's laptop (Dell Inspiron E1505 (Broadcom) ) and also experienced this same issue. Here are my details:

*Here the Wifi card is detected*
```
anna@anna-laptop:~$ lspci|grep -i network
0b:00.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation BCM4311 802.11b/g WLAN (rev 01)

```

And if I try the network manager, I cannot see any networks. Yes, I verified the adapter was active using the keyboard functions.... (However, I still cannot see any networks.)

Now I try using the CLI...

```
anna@anna-laptop:~$ iwlist eth1 scan
eth1      Interface doesn't support scanning.

```

as you can see I also am unable to choose from a list of wifi networks unless I manually enter in my settings (Which I have *not* attempted yet).

[COLOR="Black"][SIZE="5"]RESOLUTION[/SIZE][/COLOR]:

for me this was too easy....

```
anna@anna-laptop:~$ sudo aptitude update && sudo aptitude safe-upgrade

```
*then*
```
sudo apt-get -f install
```
*then*
```
sudo apt-get install wifi-radar
```

That's all folks... Now if you goto the Network manager icon and choose the drop down, you should be able to list available networks in your area. 

Hope this helps and enjoy!


[COLOR="Red"][SIZE="6"]IN NO WAY DID I USE NDISWRAPPER AT ALL![/SIZE][/COLOR]

---

### Post by oraclexml on 2009-05-04
My dell laptop was not able to recognize the dell wireless card. After looking into syslog, it indicated that I should download the latest firmware for the wireless card and it gave the http location too. After installing the latest firmware, I was able to connect successfully

---

### Post by oraclexml on 2009-05-04
I was getting the same error, so I downloaded the latest ndiswrapper which is 1.54 (not 1.51) and all the errors went away

### Post by raks81 on 2009-12-20
> **sanemanmad said:**
> Hi ~ 

I wanted to post here and point out, that while all of you may now be able to use your wireless cards... you are circumventing Ubuntu's capabilities altogether. I just finished loading Hardy 8.04 on a lady friend's laptop (Dell Inspiron E1505 (Broadcom) ) and also experienced this same issue. Here are my details:

*Here the Wifi card is detected*
```
anna@anna-laptop:~$ lspci|grep -i network
0b:00.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation BCM4311 802.11b/g WLAN (rev 01)

```And if I try the network manager, I cannot see any networks. Yes, I verified the adapter was active using the keyboard functions.... (However, I still cannot see any networks.)

Now I try using the CLI...

```
anna@anna-laptop:~$ iwlist eth1 scan
eth1      Interface doesn't support scanning.

```as you can see I also am unable to choose from a list of wifi networks unless I manually enter in my settings (Which I have *not* attempted yet).

[COLOR=Black][SIZE=5]RESOLUTION[/SIZE][/COLOR]:

for me this was too easy....

```
anna@anna-laptop:~$ sudo aptitude update && sudo aptitude safe-upgrade

```*then*
```
sudo apt-get -f install
```*then*
```
sudo apt-get install wifi-radar
```That's all folks... Now if you goto the Network manager icon and choose the drop down, you should be able to list available networks in your area. 

Hope this helps and enjoy!


[COLOR=Red][SIZE=6]IN NO WAY DID I USE NDISWRAPPER AT ALL![/SIZE][/COLOR]



So this worked for with one extra step, After installing wifi-radar, I had to got to System -> Administration -> Hardware Drivers and from there select the b43 driver and activate it. This got the WLAN status in Network Manager to 'Device not ready'  After a reboot the wireless networks were detected. Eureka!

Thanks for the post! :)

rr

---

### Post by militarymd on 2010-01-17
Just today loaded Ubuntu 9.10 as a fresh OS onto my Dell e1405 with the Broadcom 1390 WLAN and was having difficulty with the wireless card.  I spent hours trying to load drivers through ndiswrapper 1.55 without success.  Then, i stumbled on a elegant solution.

Three lines of  code are all you need.  No ndiswrapper or other apps.

sudo apt-get install b43-fwcutter
sudo /usr/share/b43-fwcutter/install_bcm43xx_firmware.sh
sudo /etc/init.d/dbus restart

After I put these in the terminal, it worked like a charm.

This was originally a solution for an earlier version, but it works perfectly for v9.10.  I found the info at:

[http://flavor8.com/index.php/2008/06/08/howto-broadcom-1390-wireless-on-ubuntu-hardy/](http://flavor8.com/index.php/2008/06/08/howto-broadcom-1390-wireless-on-ubuntu-hardy/)

Hope you have luck!

militaryMD

---

### Post by SpectralDesign on 2010-02-25
@sreerajan

when you do the command `lspci` in a terminal window, does it say you  have a broadcom controller? (If so, what ID?)

i.e.
```
lspci | grep -i network
```

For instance mine says:

02:00.0 Network controller: Broadcom Corporation BCM4311 802.11b/g WLAN (rev 02)

the bit "BCM4311" is important... if yours says something differnt I can't say if my instructions would help you or not...

### Post by niedzielski on 2010-03-14
I'm on a Dell E1705 with 'Dell Wireless 1390 WLAN Mini-Card' on Ubuntu Karmic Koala. This HOWTO worked for me with a couple distinctions:

I used [Dell_multi-device_A17_R174291.exe]("ftp://ftp.us.dell.com/network/Dell_multi-device_A17_R174291.exe").
After running through the HOWTO, from System -> Admin -> Hardware Drivers I activated Broadcom STA wireless driver. My WiFi light turned on and I rebooted.

### Post by NUboon2Age on 2010-06-06
> **tr1sth3t said:**
> WARNING: All config files need .conf: /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist, it will be ignored in a future release.
module configuration already contains alias directive

Note: Those are red herring warning messages.  You can make them go away by renaming /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist to /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist.conf

**tr1sth3t Here's how I'd proceed from here:**

1) Did the reinstall/reboot of modem-manager work?

2) If not:

**tr1sth3t in post #166 it says:**

> **dp_wiz said:**
> **Newest kernel in feisty works after supplying correct firmware.**

Which may relate to why your dmesg complains about not having firmware.  As we discussed, you can connect to the internet and **run jockey**(System->Administration->Hardware Drivers), and **add/enable the driver/firmware** with fwcutter

3) okay is it working now?  If not again try the reinstall of modem-manager reboot routine.

4) working now?  If not, I think I'd use Synaptic to install ndisgtk (System->Administration->Windows Wireless Drivers) and add the windows driver (point to the .inf file for the driver you're using from the file you unzipped. I think it would be one bcmwl5.inf)

5) working now?  try the reinstall modem-manager reboot

6) If its still not working, see my the link in my signature for GUI wireless installation and see if i'm forgetting anything.

-----------------------------------------------
Relating to what we were discussing.. so that you don't need to be 
To make a repository of an iso image:

```
sudo mv <the/path on your system><your iso name>.iso /media
sudo mkdir /media/iso
#add  "/media/<your iso name>.iso /media/iso iso9660 user,loop 0 0" to /etc/fstab
sudo mount -a
```

Then in either Synaptic (or Software Sources)
Settings->Repositories->Other Software->Add

Paste the following:
```
deb file:///media/<your iso name>.iso  lucid main restricted
```

------------------------------------------------------

example blacklist.conf file:
```
# This file lists those modules which we don't want to be loaded by
# alias expansion, usually so some other driver will be loaded for the
# device instead.

# evbug is a debug tool that should be loaded explicitly
blacklist evbug

# these drivers are very simple, the HID drivers are usually preferred
blacklist usbmouse
blacklist usbkbd

# replaced by e100
blacklist eepro100

# replaced by tulip
blacklist de4x5

# causes no end of confusion by creating unexpected network interfaces
blacklist eth1394

# snd_intel8x0m can interfere with snd_intel8x0, doesn't seem to support much
# hardware on its own (Ubuntu bug #2011, #6810)
blacklist snd_intel8x0m

# Conflicts with dvb driver (which is better for handling this device)
blacklist snd_aw2

# causes failure to suspend on HP compaq nc6000 (Ubuntu: #10306)
blacklist i2c_i801

# replaced by p54pci
blacklist prism54

# replaced by b43 and ssb.
blacklist bcm43xx

# most apps now use garmin usb driver directly (Ubuntu: #114565)
blacklist garmin_gps

# replaced by asus-laptop (Ubuntu: #184721)
blacklist asus_acpi

# low-quality, just noise when being used for sound playback, causes
# hangs at desktop session start (Ubuntu: #246969)
blacklist snd_pcsp

# ugly and loud noise, getting on everyone's nerves; this should be done by a
# nice pulseaudio bing (Ubuntu: #77010)
blacklist pcspkr

# EDAC driver for amd76x clashes with the agp driver preventing the aperture
# from being initialised (Ubuntu: #297750). Blacklist so that the driver
# continues to build and is installable for the few cases where its
# really needed.
blacklist amd76x_edac
```

### Post by Biaspoint on 2011-05-08
For 11.04:

[LIST]
[*]open the '*Synaptic Package Manager*' and search for 'bcm'
[*]uninstall the '*bcm-kernel-source*' package
[*]make sure that the '*firmware-b43-installer*' and the '*b43-fwcutter*' packages are installed
[*]reboot
[/LIST]
worked for me (from askubuntu.com), system: Dell Inspiron E1505

### Post by jheneghan on 2011-08-26
> **arthur_kalm said:**
> I get the same result as before, no wireless. The light doesn't come up and the scan is empty. I can't connect to any networks. This is really annoying because wireless was working **PERFECTLY** under Dapper. I'm really _really_ disappointed with Edgy. Nothing seems to work (wireless, XGL/Beryl, suspend and hibernate). I'm going to mess around with it for a while but I might just go back to Dapper.
 
 
I have a Dell inspiron 1501. It had a broadcom wireless card which worked when i had windows xp on the computer. When i loaded ubuntu 11.04, the wireless would not work.
 
Solution was:
 
Select additional drivers in your applications folder and de-activate the braodcom wireless card. (A common problem is that people are trying to load B43 firmware while the broadcom seems to be automatically turned on when you load ubuntu. They block eachother.)
Then go into synaptic manager and load B43 firmware and installer. 
Restart the laptop and wireless works perfectly.

---

### Post by mchaco1 on 2011-11-17
I just did this fix and restarted and it worked great! WiFi light popped up and I was back in business. Then I shut it down for the night, and this morning when I booted up, it was gone. I checked that the driver was still there and opened the terminal and redid the last step:
> sudo ndiswrapper -m 
sudo modprobe ndiswrapper 
sudo echo ndiswrapper >> /etc/modules
rebooted and wifi was back. Is there a way to make this "stick" through a shutdown?

I just installed Xubuntu 11.10 onto an old Dell laptop with Broadcom wireless.  For 11.10, I only had to do this:
sudo apt-get install firmware-b43-installer

It took care of the nitty gritties.  Although, I wish the installation CD includes all of the wireless packages to begin with, so that wireless works right out of the box...

I'm not even sure where the Synaptic Package Manager is in Precise/Gnome fallback, but but after installing the Broadcom STA additional driver and having no luck, I found this post.  After removing the STA driver (via the additional drivers section of the settings) and installing the b43 packages from the command line, I got the Broadcom on this Dell E1505 working after a reboot.

### Post by bijuneyyan on 2013-03-06
> **paperdiesel said:**
> Cab,

Try this:  

Reboot your machine.  **BEFORE** it starts loading linux, go in to your BIOS setup.  Scroll down to the entry that says Wireless.  You'll see a section that says something about the Wifi / bluetooth hotkey.  Try setting that so that the wifi is always on, and the hotkey is disabled.

I'm wondering if you're set up correctly, but the wifi hotkey is defaulting to off.  Switch that BIOS setting and report back.

Thanks a lot!
Although this tutorial had helped my system to detect that card, WiFi was not turning at all!!
This tip really helped.
